These three static methods on the `Object` constructor convert object data into arrays, which you can then loop over using any array method (`forEach`, `map`, `filter`, etc.).

- `Object.keys(obj)` → array of key strings
- `Object.values(obj)` → array of values
- `Object.entries(obj)` → array of `[key, value]` pairs

sections.title: Using Object.entries() with Destructuring
sections.explanation: By combining `Object.entries()` with `for...of` and destructuring (`[fruit, price]`), you get clean access to both the key and value in each iteration. This is the most modern and readable approach for looping through objects.

sections.title: Quick Decision Guide
sections.content:
- Need just the **keys**? → `Object.keys(obj)`
- Need just the **values**? → `Object.values(obj)`
- Need **both** keys and values? → `Object.entries(obj)` or `for...in`
- Want to use array methods (map, filter)? → `Object.keys/values/entries`
- Simple iteration? → `for...in` is quick and easy

sections.title: Practical Example: Counting and Summarizing
sections.explanation: This is where object iteration gets powerful. By converting to arrays, you can chain array methods like `reduce`, `filter`, and `map` to perform complex data transformations. This pattern is extremely common in real-world JavaScript applications.

sections.hint: Use Object.entries() to loop. Use Object.values() with reduce() for the average. Use Object.entries().filter() for high achievers.
sections.title: Try It: Student Grade Report
sections.expected_output:
Alice: 92/100
Bob: 85/100
...
Class average: 87.6
High achievers: Alice, Diana

questions.explanation: `Object.keys(obj)` returns `["a", "b", "c"]`, which is an array with 3 elements. `.length` gives us `3` — the number of properties in the object.

questions.prompt: Inside a `for...in` loop, why must you use bracket notation (not dot notation) to access the value?
choices: Dot notation doesn't work with objects
The loop variable holds a string, and dot notation can't use variables
Dot notation only works with arrays
questions.answer: The loop variable holds a string, and dot notation can't use variables
questions.explanation: In `for (let key in obj)`, the variable `key` holds a string like `"name"`. With dot notation, `obj.key` looks for a property literally called `key`. With bracket notation, `obj[key]` evaluates the variable to get `"name"`, then accesses `obj["name"]`. Only bracket notation can use variables.

questions.prompt: Given an object of country populations, write code to find and log the country with the highest population.

lessons.title: Nested Objects and Complex Data
objectives: Create and navigate nested objects
Access deeply nested properties safely
Use optional chaining (?.) to avoid errors
Work with arrays of objects — the most common real-world pattern

sections.content:
# Nested Objects and Complex Data

Real-world data is rarely flat. A user has an address, which has a city, state, and zip code. A company has departments, each with employees, each with skills. This is **nested data** — objects inside objects, arrays inside objects, objects inside arrays.

Learning to navigate nested structures is one of the most important skills in JavaScript. It's what you'll do every single day as a developer.

sections.title: Navigating Nested Objects
sections.explanation:
To access nested properties, you simply chain dot notation (or bracket notation). Each dot goes one level deeper:
- `company.address` → the address object
- `company.address.city` → the city inside the address object
- `company.ceo.contact.email` → email, inside contact, inside ceo, inside company

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: Russian Nesting Dolls 🪆
sections.content:
Nested objects are like **Russian nesting dolls (Matryoshka)**. Each doll contains a smaller doll inside. To get to the tiny doll at the center, you have to open each outer doll first.

`company.ceo.contact.email` is like opening the company doll → finding the CEO doll inside → opening it to find the contact doll → opening that to finally reach the email.

sections.title: The Danger of Missing Properties
sections.explanation: If you try to access a property of `undefined`, JavaScript throws a `TypeError`. This is one of the most common errors in JavaScript. `user.address` returns `undefined` (the property doesn't exist), and then trying to access `.city` on `undefined` causes a crash.

sections.title: Optional Chaining (?.) — The Safety Net
sections.explanation: The `?.` operator (optional chaining) checks if the value before it is `null` or `undefined`. If it is, the entire expression short-circuits and returns `undefined` instead of throwing an error. Combined with `??` (nullish coalescing), you can provide a fallback default value. This is a lifesaver when working with API data that might have missing fields.

sections.definition: A safe way to access nested properties. If any part of the chain is `null` or `undefined`, the expression returns `undefined` instead of crashing. Syntax: `obj?.prop?.deeperProp`

sections.title: Arrays of Objects — The Most Common Pattern
sections.explanation: **Arrays of objects** are the bread and butter of real-world JavaScript. API responses, database results, user lists, product catalogs — almost all of them are arrays of objects. Knowing how to filter, map, and reduce arrays of objects is arguably the single most practical JavaScript skill you can have.

sections.hint: Use school.departments.science.head for #1. For #2, remember arrays are zero-indexed, so index 1 is the second item.
sections.title: Try It: Explore Nested Data
sections.expected_output:
Ms. Johnson
Mr. Brown
3
No art department

questions.answer:
Alice
undefined
questions.explanation: The first line navigates successfully: `data.user` exists, `data.user.profile` exists, `data.user.profile.name` is `"Alice"`. The second line: `data.user` exists, but `data.user.settings` is `undefined`, so optional chaining short-circuits and returns `undefined` instead of crashing.

questions.prompt: Given an array of products, write code to find all products that are in stock AND cost less than $50. Log their names.
What does `user.address?.city` return if `user.address` is `undefined`?
questions.answer: undefined
questions.explanation: Optional chaining (`?.`) returns `undefined` when the value before it is `null` or `undefined`. It short-circuits the rest of the expression, preventing the TypeError that would normally occur.

lessons.title: Object Destructuring and Spread
objectives: Use destructuring to extract values from objects elegantly
Rename and set defaults during destructuring
Use the spread operator to copy and merge objects
Apply these patterns in function parameters

sections.content:
# Object Destructuring and Spread

As you work with objects more, you'll find yourself writing code like `user.name`, `user.age`, `user.email` over and over. **Destructuring** is a modern JavaScript feature that lets you "unpack" properties from an object into separate variables in a single, elegant line. The **spread operator** lets you copy and combine objects effortlessly.

sections.title: Basic Object Destructuring
sections.explanation:
Destructuring uses curly braces `{}` on the **left side** of the assignment. The variable names inside must **match** the property names in the object. JavaScript automatically pulls out those values and assigns them to the new variables.

One line replaces three! And it scales: you can destructure as many properties as you need.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: Unpacking a Suitcase
sections.content:
Destructuring is like unpacking a suitcase. Instead of reaching into the suitcase every time you need something (`suitcase.shirt`, `suitcase.toothbrush`), you unpack everything onto the bed once:

```javascript
const { shirt, toothbrush, passport } = suitcase;
```

Now each item is right there, ready to use by its own name.

sections.title: Renaming and Default Values
sections.explanation:
Sometimes API property names don't match what you want in your code. Renaming lets you transform `user_name` into `username`.

Default values provide a fallback if the property doesn't exist in the object. This is incredibly useful for handling optional configuration.

sections.title: Destructuring in Function Parameters
sections.explanation: Destructuring in function parameters is a game-changer. Instead of passing multiple arguments in a specific order, you pass a single object. This makes function calls self-documenting (you can see what each value means) and order-independent. This pattern is used extensively in React, Express, and most modern JavaScript libraries.

sections.title: The Spread Operator (...) with Objects
sections.explanation:
The spread operator `...` unpacks all properties from one object into another. It's like pouring the contents of one filing cabinet into a new one.

**Key behaviors:**
- Creates a **shallow copy** (nested objects are still shared)
- When merging, **later values override earlier ones** (last one wins)
- You can add new properties or override existing ones at the same time

sections.title: Shallow Copy Warning!

sections.hint: The spread operator merges objects. Later properties override earlier ones, so userConfig's theme and fontSize win.
sections.title: Try It: Config Merger
sections.expected_output:
Theme: dark
Notifications: true
Language: en
Font Size: 18px

questions.explanation: `x` gets `1`, `y` gets `2` from the object. `z` doesn't exist in the object, so it uses the default value of `10`.
When spreading, later properties override earlier ones. `a` contributes `x: 1` and `y: 2`. Then `b` contributes `y: 3` (overriding the previous `y: 2`) and `z: 4`. Result: `{ x: 1, y: 3, z: 4 }`.

questions.prompt: Write a function called `introduce` that takes a person object and uses destructuring in the parameter to extract name, age, and hobby (with a default hobby of 'reading'). It should return a formatted string.
What does `{ ...obj }` create?
choices: A reference to the same object
A deep copy of the object
A shallow copy of the object
questions.answer: A shallow copy of the object
questions.explanation: The spread operator creates a new object with copies of all top-level properties. However, nested objects are still references (not copies), making it a shallow copy. For a full deep copy, you'd need `structuredClone()` or another method.

lessons.title: Useful Built-in Object Methods
objectives: Use Object.keys(), Object.values(), Object.entries() fluently
Use Object.assign() and Object.freeze()
Check for property existence reliably
Convert between objects and arrays

sections.content:
# Useful Built-in Object Methods

JavaScript provides several powerful static methods on the `Object` constructor. These are your Swiss Army knife for working with objects — transforming them, combining them, protecting them, and converting them.

sections.title: Object.assign() — Merge Objects
sections.explanation: `Object.assign()` copies properties from source objects into a target object. It mutates and returns the target. The spread syntax `{ ...a, ...b }` achieves the same thing without mutation and is generally preferred in modern code.

sections.title: Object.freeze() — Make Objects Immutable
sections.explanation: `Object.freeze()` makes an object completely immutable — you can't add, remove, or change any properties. This is perfect for constants and configuration objects that should never change. Note: like spread, this is only a shallow freeze.

sections.title: Object.fromEntries() — Array to Object
sections.explanation: `Object.fromEntries()` is the reverse of `Object.entries()`. It takes an array of `[key, value]` pairs and creates an object. This completes the round-trip: Object → Array (transform) → Object. It's incredibly useful for transforming object values using array methods.

sections.content:
Object Methods Round-Trip:

  Object ──Object.entries()──▶ Array of [key, value] pairs
    ▲                              │
    │                              ▼
    │                     .map() / .filter() / etc.
    │                              │
    │                              ▼
  Object ◀──Object.fromEntries()── Transformed Array

sections.hint: Replace the comment with: Math.round(((f - 32) * 5/9) * 100) / 100 to get 2 decimal places.
sections.title: Try It: Transform Object Values
sections.expected_output: { Monday: 22.22, Tuesday: 20, Wednesday: 23.89, Thursday: 26.67, Friday: 18.33 }

questions.prompt: What does `Object.freeze()` do?
choices: Deletes all properties from an object
Makes the object and all nested objects immutable
Prevents adding, removing, or changing top-level properties
Converts the object to a string
questions.answer: Prevents adding, removing, or changing top-level properties
questions.explanation: `Object.freeze()` creates a shallow freeze — top-level properties can't be modified, but nested objects inside can still be changed. For a deep freeze, you'd need a recursive solution.
`Object.fromEntries()` converts the array of pairs into `{ x: 1, y: 2 }`. Then `obj.y` is `2`.

questions.prompt: Given an object of prices, use Object.entries(), filter, and Object.fromEntries() to create a new object containing only items that cost more than $10.

lessons.title: Module 8 Challenge: Build a Student Record System
objectives: Apply all object concepts in a single cohesive project
Work with nested objects and arrays of objects
Use destructuring, spread, and object methods
Build a mini application with real-world utility

sections.content:
# Module 8 Challenge: Student Record System

Time to put everything together! You'll build a student record system that stores, retrieves, updates, and analyzes student data. This challenge uses every object concept from this module.

sections.title: The Challenge Starter Code
sections.explanation:
This project demonstrates:
- **Objects with methods** (`addStudent`, `findStudent`, etc.)
- **`this` keyword** to access the students array
- **Arrays of objects** (the students array)
- **Nested objects** (grades inside each student)
- **Object.values()** and **Object.entries()** for iteration
- **Destructuring** in the for...of loop
- **Spread operator** to create new objects with added properties
- **Array methods** (find, filter, map, reduce)

sections.title: Your Turn — Extend the System!
sections.content:
Try adding these features:
1. `removeStudent(name)` — Remove a student by name
2. `getSubjectAverage(subject)` — Average grade across all students for a subject
3. `getTopStudent()` — The student with the highest overall average
4. `exportData()` — Return a clean copy of all student data (using spread to avoid mutation)

sections.hint: For removeStudent, use this.students = this.students.filter(s => s.name !== name). For getSubjectAverage, filter students who have that subject, then calculate the average.
sections.title: Your Challenge: Extend the System
sections.expected_output: Your extended system should handle all new operations correctly

questions.prompt: Add a `getTopStudent()` method to the studentDB that returns the student object with the highest average grade.
In the studentDB, why do we use `this.students` instead of just `students`?
choices: It's just a coding style preference
Because `students` is inside the object, and methods need `this` to reference their own object's properties
Because `this` makes the code run faster
questions.answer: Because `students` is inside the object, and methods need `this` to reference their own object's properties
questions.explanation: Inside a method, `this` refers to the object the method belongs to. Without `this`, JavaScript would look for a standalone variable called `students` in the outer scope, which doesn't exist. `this.students` correctly accesses the `students` array that belongs to the `studentDB` object.

questions.prompt: What will `studentDB.getHonorRoll()` return if no students have an average above 90?
questions.answer: An empty array []
questions.explanation: The `filter()` method returns all elements that pass the test. If no students have an average >= 90, no elements pass, and `filter()` returns an empty array `[]`.

lessons.title: Functions as Values (First-Class Citizens)
objectives: Understand that functions are values that can be stored, passed, and returned
Store functions in variables and arrays
Pass functions as arguments to other functions
Return functions from other functions

sections.content:
# Functions as Values

Here's a mind-bending idea that separates JavaScript from many other languages: **functions are values**, just like numbers, strings, and objects. You can store a function in a variable, put it in an array, pass it as an argument, and even return it from another function.

This concept is called **first-class functions**, and it's the foundation of some of the most powerful patterns in JavaScript.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: Recipes as Objects
sections.content:
Imagine recipes written on index cards. A recipe *is* a set of instructions (like a function). But the card itself is an *object* — you can:

- 📋 **Store it** in a recipe box (assign to a variable)
- 📬 **Give it to someone** (pass as an argument)
- 📦 **Put it in a collection** (store in an array)
- 🔄 **Hand it back** when asked (return from a function)

In JavaScript, functions work exactly like this. They're values you can move around.

sections.title: Functions Stored in Variables
sections.explanation: When you write `const greet2 = function(name) { ... }`, you're literally assigning a function value to a variable, just like `const x = 42` assigns a number. The variable `greet2` now holds a function, and you call it with `greet2()`.

sections.title: Passing Functions as Arguments
sections.explanation:
`doMath` takes a function as its third argument. It doesn't know or care *what* operation will be performed — it just calls whatever function you pass. This is incredibly flexible: one function can do completely different things depending on what function you give it.

A function that accepts another function as an argument (or returns one) is called a **higher-order function**.

sections.definition: A function that either (1) takes another function as an argument, or (2) returns a function as its result. Examples include Array.map(), Array.filter(), and setTimeout(). Higher-order functions enable powerful abstraction patterns.

sections.title: Returning Functions from Functions
sections.explanation: This is a **function factory** — a function that creates and returns other functions. `createMultiplier(2)` returns a brand new function that will always multiply its input by 2. This pattern is called a **closure** — the inner function "closes over" and remembers the `factor` variable even after `createMultiplier` has finished executing.

sections.hint: Return an arrow function: return (name) => `${greeting}, ${name}!`;
sections.title: Try It: Build a Greeting Factory
sections.expected_output:
Hello, Alice!
Hola, Bob!
Bonjour, Charlie!

questions.prompt: What does 'first-class functions' mean?
choices: Functions can only be declared at the top of a file
Functions can be treated as values — stored, passed, and returned
Functions have higher priority than variables
questions.answer: Functions can be treated as values — stored, passed, and returned
questions.explanation: First-class functions means functions are treated like any other value in the language. You can assign them to variables, pass them as arguments, return them from other functions, and store them in data structures.
`applyTwice` calls `fn` on `value`, then calls `fn` again on the result. First call: `addThree(7)` = `10`. Second call: `addThree(10)` = `13`.

`makeCounter` returns a function that "closes over" the `count` variable. Each call increments `count` and returns it. The variable persists between calls because of closure. First call: 1. Second: 2. Third: 3.

lessons.title: Callback Functions — The Foundation
objectives: Understand what a callback function is
Recognize callbacks in familiar JavaScript APIs
Write custom functions that accept callbacks
Understand the callback pattern before learning array methods

sections.content:
# Callback Functions

A **callback** is simply a function that you pass to another function as an argument, to be called ("called back") at a later time. You've already seen callbacks without knowing it — `setTimeout`, event listeners, and soon, every array method uses callbacks.

Callbacks are the workhorse pattern of JavaScript.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: The Pizza Delivery
sections.content:
Ordering pizza is a perfect callback analogy:

1. You **call** the pizza place (invoke a function)
2. You give them your **phone number** (pass a callback)
3. They make the pizza (do their work)
4. When it's ready, they **call you back** (execute the callback)

Your phone number is the callback — it's *how they reach you* when they're done. You don't wait by the phone doing nothing; you go about your life and they contact you when the result is ready.

sections.title: Callbacks You Already Know
sections.explanation:
In every example above, a function is passed as an argument. That function doesn't run immediately — it runs *later*, when the time is right:
- `setTimeout`: after 2 seconds
- `addEventListener`: when the user clicks
- `forEach`: once for each element in the array

The function being passed is the **callback**.

sections.title: Writing Custom Functions That Accept Callbacks
sections.explanation: We just built our own version of `Array.map()`! The `processArray` function doesn't know or care what transformation will happen — that's decided by whoever calls it by passing the appropriate callback. This separation of concerns is what makes higher-order functions so powerful.

sections.title: Aha! Moment: Callbacks = Pluggable Behavior
sections.content:
Here's the key insight: callbacks let you write **generic** functions that can behave differently based on what callback you plug in.

It's like a power drill with interchangeable bits. The drill (higher-order function) provides the motor and mechanism. The drill bit (callback) determines what actually happens — drill a hole, drive a screw, sand a surface. Same tool, different results.

sections.hint: Use a for loop: for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) { callback(i); }
sections.title: Try It: Custom repeat() Function
sections.expected_output:
Iteration 0
Iteration 1
Iteration 2
⭐
⭐⭐
⭐⭐⭐
⭐⭐⭐⭐
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

questions.prompt: What is a callback function?
choices: A function that calls itself recursively
A function passed as an argument to another function, to be executed later
A function that returns another function
A function that only runs once
questions.answer: A function passed as an argument to another function, to be executed later
questions.explanation: A callback is a function you hand off to another function, saying 'call this when you need it.' The receiving function decides when and how to invoke the callback.

questions.answer:
ALICE
alice
questions.explanation: The first call passes a callback that uppercases: `"alice"` → `"ALICE"`. The second call passes a callback that lowercases: `"ALICE"` → `"alice"`. Same function, different behavior based on the callback.

questions.prompt: Write a function `filterArray(arr, testFn)` that returns a new array with only the elements where `testFn` returns true. Then use it to filter out negative numbers from [-3, 5, -1, 8, 0, -7, 12].

lessons.title: forEach — Doing Something with Each Item
objectives: Use `forEach()` to iterate over arrays
Understand the callback signature (element, index, array)
Know when to use `forEach` vs a `for` loop
Recognize that `forEach` doesn't return a value

sections.content:
# forEach — The Gateway Array Method

`forEach` is the simplest array method to understand: it runs a callback function **once for each element** in the array. It's like a `for` loop, but more expressive and functional.

sections.title: forEach in Action
sections.explanation:
The callback passed to `forEach` receives up to three arguments:
1. **element** — the current item
2. **index** — the current position (0, 1, 2, ...)
3. **array** — the original array (rarely used)

You only need to include the parameters you actually use.

sections.title: forEach Returns undefined!
sections.content:
`forEach` always returns `undefined`. It's for performing **side effects** (logging, modifying external variables, updating the DOM), NOT for transforming data.

```javascript
const result = [1,2,3].forEach(n => n * 2);
console.log(result); // undefined! 😱
```

If you want to **transform** data and get a new array, use `.map()` instead.

sections.title: Practical Uses of forEach
sections.explanation: `forEach` is great for executing logic for each item — logging, accumulating values, or triggering actions. It's essentially a prettier for loop.

sections.hint: Inside forEach: console.log(`${index + 1}. ${item.item} - $${item.price.toFixed(2)}`); and total += item.price;
sections.title: Try It: Shopping Receipt

questions.answer: undefined
questions.explanation: `forEach` always returns `undefined`, regardless of what happens inside the callback. If you want a new transformed array, use `.map()` instead.

questions.prompt: Which parameters does the forEach callback receive?
choices: Element, index, and the original array
questions.answer: Element, index, and the original array
questions.explanation: The callback receives three parameters: `(element, index, array)`. You can use as many or as few as you need. Most commonly you'll use just the element, or element and index.

questions.prompt: Use `forEach` to count how many numbers in the array are greater than 50.

lessons.title: map() — Transforming Data
objectives: Use `map()` to create a new array by transforming each element
Understand that `map()` returns a NEW array (no mutation)
Use `map()` with objects to extract or transform properties
Chain `map()` with other methods

sections.content:
# map() — Transform Every Element

`map()` is one of the most important array methods in JavaScript. It creates a **new array** by running a callback function on **each element** of the original array and collecting the results.

If `forEach` is "do something with each item," then `map` is "**transform each item into something new.**"

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: The Assembly Line
sections.content:
Imagine a factory assembly line. Raw materials (original array) go in one side. Each item passes through a machine (your callback function) that transforms it. Finished products (new array) come out the other side.

The raw materials aren't destroyed — they still exist. The machine produces a completely new collection of transformed items.

🔧 `[1, 2, 3].map(x => x * 2)` → `[2, 4, 6]`

Original `[1, 2, 3]` is unchanged!

sections.title: map() Basics
sections.explanation:
Key points about `map()`:
1. It **always returns a new array** with the same length as the original
2. The original array is **never modified**
3. Each element in the new array is the **return value** of the callback for that element
4. If you forget to return a value in the callback, that position will be `undefined`

sections.title: map() with Objects — The Power Move
sections.explanation:
Mapping over arrays of objects to extract or reshape data is arguably the most common real-world use of `map()`. API responses, database records, user lists — you'll constantly need to transform data from one shape into another.

Note the parentheses around the object literal `({ ... })` when returning an object from an arrow function. Without them, JavaScript thinks `{}` is a code block, not an object.

sections.title: Arrow Function + Return Object = Wrap in Parentheses!

sections.hint: return Math.round(((f - 32) * 5/9) * 10) / 10;
sections.title: Try It: Temperature Converter

questions.explanation: `map()` applies the callback to each element. `"hello".length` is `5`, `"world".length` is `5`. The result is a new array of lengths: `[5, 5]`.

questions.prompt: What is the key difference between `forEach` and `map`?
choices: map returns a new array; forEach returns undefined
forEach can take a callback; map cannot
map modifies the original array; forEach doesn't
questions.answer: map returns a new array; forEach returns undefined
questions.explanation: `map()` creates and returns a new array of transformed values. `forEach()` always returns `undefined` — it's for side effects only. Neither modifies the original array.

questions.prompt: Given an array of product objects, use `map()` to create a new array of strings in the format: `"ProductName - $Price"` (price with 2 decimal places).

lessons.title: filter() — Finding What You Need
objectives: Use `filter()` to select elements that meet a condition
Understand that `filter()` returns a subset (new array)
Combine `filter()` with `map()` for powerful data pipelines
Use `find()` to get the first matching element

sections.content:
# filter() — Keep Only What Matches

`filter()` creates a **new array** containing only the elements that pass a test (where the callback returns `true`). It's like a sieve: pour in all the data, and only the matching items come through.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: Airport Security
sections.content: Think of `filter()` like airport security. Every passenger (element) goes through the checkpoint (callback function). If they pass the screening (callback returns `true`), they proceed to the gate (new array). If not (callback returns `false`), they don't make it through. The original line of passengers (original array) is unchanged.

sections.title: filter() Basics
sections.explanation:
The callback must return a **boolean** (or a truthy/falsy value). For each element:
- If the callback returns `true` → the element is **included** in the new array
- If the callback returns `false` → the element is **excluded**

The result can be shorter than, the same length as, or even empty compared to the original.

sections.title: Filtering Objects — Real-World Scenario
sections.explanation: Filtering arrays of objects is one of the most practical things you'll do. Search functionality, data dashboards, admin panels — they all rely heavily on `filter()`. The search example shows a real-world pattern: case-insensitive partial matching.

sections.title: find() — Get the First Match
sections.explanation: `find()` is like `filter()` but stops at the first match and returns the element itself (not wrapped in an array). Use `find()` when you want exactly one result, like looking up a user by ID.

sections.hint: Affordable: p => p.category === 'electronics' && p.price < 500. Top rated: p => p.rating >= 4.5
sections.title: Try It: Product Filter
sections.expected_output:
Affordable Electronics: ["Headphones", "Monitor"]
Top Rated: ["Laptop", "Book", "Monitor"]
Cheapest: Pen ($3)

questions.explanation: No elements in `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]` are greater than 10, so `filter()` returns an empty array `[]`. It doesn't return `null` or `undefined` — always an array.

questions.prompt: What's the difference between `filter()` and `find()`?
choices: filter returns an array of all matches; find returns only the first match
find is faster because it checks all elements
filter modifies the original array; find doesn't
questions.answer: filter returns an array of all matches; find returns only the first match
questions.explanation: `filter()` tests every element and returns an array of all that pass. `find()` stops at the first match and returns that element directly (not in an array), or `undefined` if nothing matches.

questions.prompt: Use `filter()` and `map()` together to get a list of names of students who scored 80 or above.

lessons.title: reduce() — The Swiss Army Knife
objectives: Understand how `reduce()` works step by step
Use `reduce()` to sum, count, group, and flatten data
Know how the accumulator and initial value work
Recognize that map and filter can be built with reduce

sections.content:
# reduce() — The Most Powerful Array Method

`reduce()` is the Swiss Army knife of array methods. While `map()` transforms each element and `filter()` selects elements, `reduce()` can do *anything* — it takes an entire array and **reduces** it down to a **single value**.

That single value could be a number, a string, an object, another array, or anything else. This flexibility makes `reduce()` both the most powerful and the most confusing array method.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: The Snowball
sections.content:
Imagine rolling a snowball down a hill. It starts small (the **initial value**). As it rolls, it picks up more snow with each revolution (each element in the array). By the time it reaches the bottom, it's one big snowball (the **final accumulated result**).

The snowball at any point is the **accumulator**. Each patch of snow it rolls over is the **current element**. The final giant snowball is the **return value** of `reduce()`.

sections.title: Understanding reduce() Step by Step
sections.explanation:
How `reduce()` works, step by step:

1. **Initial value**: The accumulator starts at `0` (the second argument to `reduce`)
2. **Iteration 1**: acc=0, current=10 → return 0+10 = 10 (new accumulator)
3. **Iteration 2**: acc=10, current=20 → return 10+20 = 30
4. **Iteration 3**: acc=30, current=30 → return 30+30 = 60
5. **Iteration 4**: acc=60, current=40 → return 60+40 = 100
6. **Final result**: 100

The callback takes `(accumulator, currentElement)` and must **return** the new accumulator value.

sections.description:
Step-by-step visualization of reduce() processing [10, 20, 30, 40] with initial value 0:

Step 0: accumulator = 0 (initial)
Step 1: 0 + 10 → accumulator = 10
Step 2: 10 + 20 → accumulator = 30
Step 3: 30 + 30 → accumulator = 60
Step 4: 60 + 40 → accumulator = 100 (final result)

Visualize as a conveyor belt where each item is 'folded' into the accumulator.

sections.title: Common reduce() Patterns
sections.explanation:
These four patterns cover the vast majority of real-world `reduce()` usage:
1. **Summing** — Most common. Start at 0, add each value.
2. **Finding max/min** — Compare each element to the current best.
3. **Counting** — Build an object tally. Initial value is `{}`.
4. **Grouping** — Build an object with arrays. Initial value is `{}`.

The key insight: the initial value determines the type of the result. Start with `0` for numbers, `{}` for objects, `[]` for arrays, `""` for strings.

sections.title: Always Provide an Initial Value!

sections.hint: counts[word] = (counts[word] || 0) + 1;
sections.title: Try It: Word Frequency Counter
sections.expected_output: { the: 3, quick: 1, brown: 1, fox: 2, jumps: 1, over: 1, lazy: 1, dog: 1 }

questions.explanation: Starting with accumulator = 1: 1*1=1, 1*2=2, 2*3=6, 6*4=24. This computes the product of all numbers (factorial-like). The initial value is 1 because 1 is the identity for multiplication.

questions.prompt: What should the initial value be when using reduce to build an object?
questions.answer: An empty object {}
questions.explanation: The initial value determines the type and starting state of the accumulator. When building an object (like counting or grouping), start with `{}`. When summing numbers, start with `0`. When building an array, start with `[]`.

questions.prompt: Use `reduce()` to find the longest word in an array of words.

lessons.title: Chaining Methods and Other Useful Array Methods
objectives: Chain multiple array methods for elegant data pipelines
Use `sort()`, `some()`, `every()`, `includes()`, and `findIndex()`
Know the right method for each common task
Read and write chained method expressions confidently

sections.content:
# Chaining Methods Together

The real magic happens when you **chain** array methods together. Since `map()`, `filter()`, and `sort()` each return a new array, you can call the next method directly on the result, creating elegant data pipelines.

sections.title: Method Chaining in Action
sections.explanation:
Read the chain from top to bottom like a recipe:
1. **Start** with all products
2. **Filter** → keep only electronics
3. **Filter** → keep only items under $700
4. **Sort** → arrange by price (ascending)
5. **Map** → transform into display strings

Each step passes its result to the next. The original `products` array is never modified.

sections.content:
Data Pipeline:

[All Products]  ──filter(electronics)──▶  [3 Electronics]
                                              │
                                        filter(<$700)
                                              │
                                              ▼
                                       [2 Affordable]
                                              │
                                         sort(price)
                                              │
                                              ▼
                                        [2 Sorted]
                                              │
                                        map(format)
                                              │
                                              ▼
                                    ["Tablet: $449",
                                     "Phone: $699"]

sections.title: More Useful Array Methods
sections.explanation:
Quick reference:
- `sort(compareFn)` — Sorts the array. **Mutates** the original! Spread first to avoid this.
- `some(fn)` — Returns `true` if at least one element passes the test
- `every(fn)` — Returns `true` if ALL elements pass the test
- `includes(value)` — Returns `true` if the array contains the exact value
- `findIndex(fn)` — Like `find()` but returns the index instead of the element
- `flat(depth)` — Flattens nested arrays by a specified depth

sections.title: sort() MUTATES the Original Array!
sections.content:
`sort()` is the exception to the rule — unlike `map()` and `filter()`, it modifies the original array in place.

```javascript
const arr = [3, 1, 2];
arr.sort((a, b) => a - b);
console.log(arr); // [1, 2, 3] — original is changed!
```

**Best practice:** Always sort a copy: `[...arr].sort((a, b) => a - b)`

Note: In 2023+, `toSorted()` was added, which returns a new sorted array without mutation.

sections.hint: .filter(s => s.score >= 75).sort((a,b) => b.score - a.score).map(s => `🏅 ${s.name}: ${s.score}`)
sections.title: Try It: Student Leaderboard
sections.expected_output: ["🏅 Diana: 95", "🏅 Alice: 92", "🏅 Charlie: 85", "🏅 Eve: 78"]

questions.answer:
true
false
questions.explanation: `some()` returns `true` because at least one element (4, 5) is greater than 3. `every()` returns `false` because NOT all elements are greater than 3 (1, 2, 3 fail the test).

questions.prompt: Which array method MUTATES the original array?
questions.answer: sort()
questions.explanation: `sort()` modifies the original array in place. `map()`, `filter()`, and `reduce()` all return new values without changing the original. Always spread-copy before sorting: `[...arr].sort()`.

questions.prompt: Given an array of transactions, find the total amount of all 'credit' transactions, sorted from highest to lowest, and return the top 3 as formatted strings.

lessons.title: Module 9 Challenge: Data Analytics Dashboard
objectives: Apply higher-order functions to solve real data analysis problems
Chain multiple array methods for complex transformations
Process realistic datasets using JavaScript
Build a mini analytics dashboard

sections.content:
# Module 9 Challenge: Sales Data Dashboard

You're a junior developer at an e-commerce company. Your manager has given you a dataset of sales transactions and needs several analytics reports. Use everything you've learned about array methods!

sections.code:
const sales = [
  { id: 1, product: "Laptop", category: "Electronics", price: 999, qty: 2, date: "2024-01-15", region: "North" },
  { id: 2, product: "Desk Chair", category: "Furniture", price: 299, qty: 5, date: "2024-01-20", region: "South" },
  { id: 3, product: "Headphones", category: "Electronics", price: 79, qty: 10, date: "2024-02-01", region: "East" },
  { id: 4, product: "Bookshelf", category: "Furniture", price: 149, qty: 3, date: "2024-02-10", region: "North" },
  { id: 5, product: "Phone", category: "Electronics", price: 699, qty: 4, date: "2024-02-15", region: "West" },
  { id: 6, product: "Sofa", category: "Furniture", price: 599, qty: 2, date: "2024-03-01", region: "South" },
  { id: 7, product: "Tablet", category: "Electronics", price: 449, qty: 6, date: "2024-03-10", region: "East" },
  { id: 8, product: "Desk", category: "Furniture", price: 249, qty: 4, date: "2024-03-15", region: "North" },
  { id: 9, product: "Smartwatch", category: "Electronics", price: 299, qty: 8, date: "2024-03-20", region: "West" },
  { id: 10, product: "Lamp", category: "Furniture", price: 59, qty: 12, date: "2024-03-25", region: "East" }
];
sections.title: The Dataset
sections.explanation: This is your raw data. Each sale has an id, product name, category, unit price, quantity sold, date, and region.

sections.title: Complete the Analytics
sections.explanation: Tasks 1-4 are solved for you as examples. Tasks 5-7 are for you to complete using the techniques from this module.

sections.hint: Task 5: Same pattern as revenueByCategory but group by region. Task 6: filter for electronics, map to include revenue, filter revenue > 1000, map to strings.
sections.title: Complete the Dashboard
sections.expected_output:
🌍 Revenue by Region: { North: 3440, South: 2693, East: 3792, West: 5188 }
📱 Big Electronics: ["Laptop: $1998", "Phone: $2796", "Tablet: $2694", "Smartwatch: $2392"]
Any qty > 10? true
All prices > $50? true

questions.prompt: From the sales data, create an array of all unique categories (no duplicates). Hint: you can use reduce with an accumulator array and includes(), or use the Set trick.
Which method chain would give you the names of the 3 cheapest products?
questions.answer: [...sales].sort((a,b) => a.price - b.price).slice(0,3).map(s => s.product)
questions.explanation: We spread into a new array to avoid mutating the original, sort ascending by price, take the first 3, then extract just the product names. The first option mutates the original and has incorrect sort syntax. The second only filters by a threshold, not the top 3. The last sorts names alphabetically.

lessons.title: What is the DOM? The Bridge Between JS and the Web Page
objectives: Understand what the DOM is and why it exists
Visualize the DOM as a tree structure
Know the relationship between HTML and the DOM
Use the browser DevTools to explore the DOM
Understand the difference between the document, window, and DOM nodes

sections.content:
# What is the DOM?

Up until now, all our JavaScript has lived in isolation — we've logged things to the console, manipulated arrays, built objects, and written functions. But here's the thing: **JavaScript was invented to make web pages interactive**. It was literally born in a web browser.

So how does JavaScript actually *talk* to a web page? How does it know what's on the screen? How can it change text, add buttons, or make things disappear?

The answer is the **DOM** — the **Document Object Model**.

When your browser loads an HTML file, it doesn't just display the raw text. Instead, it **parses** the HTML and builds a live, in-memory representation of the page — a structured model that JavaScript can read and modify. That model is the DOM.

**Think of it this way:**
- Your HTML file is the **blueprint** (like architectural plans for a house)
- The DOM is the **actual house** built from that blueprint
- JavaScript is the **interior designer** who can rearrange furniture, repaint walls, and add new rooms — all while people are living in it

The DOM is not your HTML file. It's a **living, breathing object** that JavaScript can interact with in real time.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: The Family Tree
sections.content:
Imagine your family tree. At the top is your great-grandparent. Below them are their children, then grandchildren, then you. Each person is a "node" in the tree — they have a parent above them, possibly siblings beside them, and possibly children below them. The DOM works exactly the same way. The entire web page is a tree. The `` element is the root. Inside it are `` and `` — its children. Inside `` might be `
`, `

`, `

` — and those might have their own children. Every single element, text, and attribute on the page is a **node** in this tree.

sections.title: A Simple HTML Page
sections.code:
Hello World

This is a paragraph.

  • Item 1
  • Item 2
sections.explanation: This is plain HTML. When the browser reads this, it builds the DOM tree from it. Every opening and closing tag becomes a node. Even the text inside tags ('Hello World', 'Item 1') becomes its own text node.

sections.title: The DOM Tree for the HTML Above
sections.content: document | / \ | / | \ <h1> <p> <ul> | | | / \ "My Page" "Hello" "This.." <li> <li> World" | | "Item 1" "Item 2"

sections.definition: The DOM is a programming interface (API) that represents an HTML document as a tree of objects. Each HTML element becomes a JavaScript object (called a 'node') with properties you can read and methods you can call. It's the bridge that lets JavaScript interact with web page content.

sections.content:
## The Key Players: `document`, `window`, and Nodes When you're working with the DOM, you'll encounter three main concepts: ### 1. `window` — The Global Boss The `window` object represents the entire browser window/tab. It's the top-level object in the browser. When you write `console.log()`, you're actually calling `window.console.log()`. It contains everything — your variables, functions, the document, and more. ### 2. `document` — Your Entry Point to the DOM The `document` object is your gateway to the page content. It represents the entire HTML document and provides methods to find, create, and modify elements. Almost everything you do in DOM manipulation starts with `document`. ### 3. Nodes — The Building Blocks Every item in the DOM tree is a **node**. There are different types: - **Element nodes**: `

`, `
`, `

` — the HTML tags - **Text nodes**: The actual text content inside elements - **Attribute nodes**: `class="active"`, `id="main"` — the attributes - **Comment nodes**: `` — even HTML comments are nodes!

sections.title: Exploring the Document Object
sections.code:
// The document object is always available in the browser
console.log(document);           // The entire DOM
console.log(document.title);     // The page title (from <title> tag)
console.log(document.URL);       // The current page URL
console.log(document.body);      // The <body> element
console.log(document.head);      // The <head> element

// You can even change things immediately:
document.title = "I changed the title!"; // Look at the browser tab!
sections.explanation: The `document` object is always available in browser JavaScript — you don't need to import or create it. It has dozens of properties and methods. `document.title` gives you the page title. `document.body` gives you direct access to the <body> element. And yes — you can modify them! Changing `document.title` immediately updates the browser tab.

sections.title: DOM ≠ HTML Source Code
sections.content: A common misconception: the DOM is NOT the same as your HTML source code. The HTML is what the browser initially reads, but the DOM is what the browser actually builds and displays. JavaScript can modify the DOM without changing the HTML file at all. If you right-click a page and choose 'View Page Source', you see the original HTML. If you open DevTools (F12) and look at the Elements tab, you see the live DOM — which may be very different if JavaScript has modified it.

sections.title: How to Explore the DOM in Your Browser (Try This Now!)
steps: Open any web page (even this one!)
Press F12 (or Ctrl+Shift+I / Cmd+Option+I on Mac) to open DevTools
Click the 'Elements' tab — this shows the live DOM tree
Hover over elements in the tree — they highlight on the page!
Click the 'Console' tab
Type `document.title` and press Enter — you'll see the page title
Type `document.body.children` and press Enter — you'll see all direct children of <body>
Type `document.title = 'I am a hacker'` — watch the tab title change!

sections.description: A split-screen illustration. Left side shows HTML source code with tags highlighted. Right side shows the corresponding DOM tree with nodes as rounded rectangles connected by lines. Arrows point from HTML elements to their DOM node counterparts. The document node sits at the very top of the tree. Colors distinguish element nodes (blue), text nodes (green), and attribute nodes (orange).
sections.alt_text: HTML source code mapped to DOM tree visualization

sections.title: Where Does Your JavaScript Go?
sections.content:
To manipulate the DOM, your JavaScript needs to run in the browser. The most common way is to include a ` ``` Why at the bottom? Because the browser reads HTML top-to-bottom. If your script runs before the `

` is parsed, it won't find it in the DOM! Placing the script at the bottom ensures all elements exist before your JS runs.

## Why the DOM Matters — The Big Picture

Every interactive website you've ever used relies on DOM manipulation:

- **Google** — When you type in the search bar, the auto-suggestions that appear? DOM manipulation.
- **Twitter/X** — New tweets loading as you scroll? Elements being created and inserted into the DOM.
- **Shopping carts** — Adding an item and seeing the count update? The DOM being modified.
- **Form validation** — That red border and error message when you type an invalid email? DOM styles and elements being changed.
- **Dark mode toggle** — CSS classes being swapped on the DOM.

The DOM is what makes the web **dynamic**. Without it, web pages would be static documents — like printed flyers. The DOM is what turns them into interactive applications.

In this module, you'll learn to:
1. **Find** elements on the page (selecting)
2. **Read** their content, attributes, and styles
3. **Change** what's displayed
4. **Create** new elements from scratch
5. **Remove** elements from the page
6. **Navigate** between related elements

Let's begin! 🚀

questions.prompt: What is the DOM?
choices: The raw HTML file saved on a server
A tree-shaped programming interface that represents the web page as objects JavaScript can interact with
A CSS framework for styling web pages
A database that stores website data
questions.answer: A tree-shaped programming interface that represents the web page as objects JavaScript can interact with
questions.explanation: The DOM (Document Object Model) is a live, in-memory tree of objects built by the browser from your HTML. It's an API (programming interface) that JavaScript can use to read and modify page content. It's NOT the HTML file itself — it's what the browser builds FROM the HTML.

questions.prompt: Which object is the main entry point for accessing DOM elements in JavaScript?
questions.answer: document
questions.explanation: The `document` object is your gateway to the DOM. While `window` is the global browser object, `document` specifically represents the HTML document and provides all the methods for finding and manipulating elements — like `document.getElementById()`, `document.querySelector()`, etc.

questions.prompt:
What will appear in the console?
```javascript
console.log(typeof document);
```
questions.answer: object
questions.explanation: The `document` is an object — the Document Object Model is made of objects! Every element, text node, and attribute in the DOM is represented as a JavaScript object with properties and methods. `typeof document` returns 'object'.

questions.prompt: Why is it recommended to place `<script>` tags at the bottom of the `<body>`, right before `</body>`?
choices: It makes the JavaScript run faster
It's required by the HTML specification
It ensures all HTML elements are parsed and available in the DOM before the script runs
questions.answer: It ensures all HTML elements are parsed and available in the DOM before the script runs
questions.explanation: The browser reads HTML top-to-bottom. If your script tries to find an element that hasn't been parsed yet (because it appears later in the HTML), it will get `null`. Placing the `<script>` at the bottom ensures the browser has already built all the DOM nodes from the HTML above.

questions.prompt: In the DOM tree, what is the root element of an HTML page?
questions.answer: document
questions.explanation: The very root of the DOM tree is the `document` node itself. Below it is the `<html>` element (which is the root element of the HTML), and below that are `<head>` and `<body>`. So `document` → `<html>` → `<head>` + `<body>` → everything else.

lessons.title: Selecting Elements: Finding Things on the Page
objectives: Select elements using getElementById
Select elements using querySelector and querySelectorAll
Understand the difference between live and static NodeLists
Select elements by class name and tag name
Choose the right selector method for different situations

sections.content:
# Selecting Elements: Finding Things on the Page Before you can change anything on a web page, you first need to **find** it. Think of it like editing a document — before you can change a word, you need to find it first (Ctrl+F!). The DOM gives us several methods to select elements. These are your **search tools**, and each one is suited for different situations. Let's work with this HTML throughout the lesson: ```html

My Website

Home About Contact

First Card

This is the first card.

Second Card

This is the second card.

© 2025 My Website

```

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: Finding People in a Building
sections.content:
Imagine you're in a huge office building and need to find someone:

- **getElementById** → Finding someone by their unique employee badge number. There's only ONE person with that ID.
- **querySelector** → Using a description: 'Find me the first person wearing a red shirt in the marketing department.' It returns the FIRST match.
- **querySelectorAll** → Same description, but: 'Find me ALL people wearing red shirts in marketing.' Returns everyone who matches.
- **getElementsByClassName** → 'Find everyone in the Sales team.' Returns all people with that team/class.
- **getElementsByTagName** → 'Find every intern.' Returns all people with that role/tag.

## Method 1: `getElementById()` — The Fastest, Most Direct Way

If an element has an `id` attribute, you can grab it instantly. IDs must be **unique** on a page — only one element can have a given ID.

sections.title: getElementById — Selecting by ID
sections.explanation: getElementById takes a string — the ID name without the # symbol. It returns the element object if found, or `null` if no element has that ID. Since IDs are unique, it always returns a single element (or null). This is the fastest selection method because the browser indexes IDs internally.

sections.content:
## Method 2: `querySelector()` — The Swiss Army Knife

This is the **most flexible** selector. It uses **CSS selector syntax** — the same selectors you use in CSS stylesheets. It returns the **first** element that matches.

sections.title: querySelector — Select with CSS Selectors
sections.explanation: querySelector accepts any valid CSS selector string. It returns the FIRST matching element. This makes it incredibly powerful — you can use IDs (#), classes (.), tag names, attributes ([attr]), pseudo-classes, combinators (>, +, ~), and complex chains. If you know CSS selectors, you already know how to use this!

sections.content:
## Method 3: `querySelectorAll()` — Find ALL Matches

While `querySelector` returns the first match, `querySelectorAll` returns **all** matches as a **NodeList** (which is like an array, but not quite).

sections.title: querySelectorAll — Select Multiple Elements
sections.explanation: querySelectorAll returns a NodeList containing ALL matching elements. Key differences from querySelector: it always returns a NodeList (never null), even if empty. You can use .length, index access [0], and .forEach() on it. However, NodeList does NOT have all array methods like .map() or .filter() by default — we'll see how to handle that.

sections.title: Converting NodeList to Array

sections.content:
## Older Methods (Still Common in Legacy Code)

You'll encounter these in older tutorials and codebases. They still work, but `querySelector` / `querySelectorAll` are usually preferred for new code.

sections.title: getElementsByClassName and getElementsByTagName
sections.explanation: getElementsByClassName and getElementsByTagName return HTMLCollection objects, which are 'live' — they automatically update when the DOM changes. This can be useful but also confusing. Note: HTMLCollection does NOT have .forEach(), unlike NodeList from querySelectorAll. For modern code, querySelector/querySelectorAll is almost always the better choice.

sections.title: DOM Selection Methods Compared
rows: First match of any CSS selector
ALL matches of any CSS selector

sections.title: Which Method Should I Use?
sections.content:
**In modern JavaScript, stick with these three:**

1. **`getElementById()`** — When you need ONE element and it has an ID. Fastest.
2. **`querySelector()`** — When you need ONE element and want to use any CSS selector.
3. **`querySelectorAll()`** — When you need MULTIPLE elements.

You'll see `getElementsByClassName` and `getElementsByTagName` in older code, but there's rarely a reason to use them in new projects. `querySelector` and `querySelectorAll` can do everything they do — and more.

sections.title: Storing Selected Elements in Variables
sections.explanation: Always store your selected elements in descriptively-named `const` variables. This makes your code readable and avoids searching the DOM repeatedly (which is slower). The null check (`if (maybeElement)`) is important — if querySelector returns null and you try to access a property on it, you'll get a TypeError.

sections.title: Try It: Select Elements from This HTML
sections.expected_output:
App: <div id="app">
Button: <button id="cta-btn">
Intros: 2
Title: <h1 class="title">
sections.hint: Try selecting elements using different methods and see what you get back.

questions.prompt:
Given this HTML: ```html
A
B
C
``` What does this code return? ```javascript const result = document.querySelector(".box"); console.log(result.textContent); ```
questions.answer: A
questions.explanation: querySelector returns the FIRST matching element only. There are three .box elements, but querySelector stops at the first one and returns it. Its textContent is 'A'.

questions.answer:
NodeList []
0
questions.explanation: When querySelectorAll finds no matches, it returns an empty NodeList (NOT null). So `items` is `NodeList []` and `items.length` is 0. This is different from querySelector, which returns null when nothing is found.

questions.prompt: Which method would you use to select the FIRST element matching a complex CSS selector like `nav > ul > li.active`?
questions.answer: document.querySelector('nav > ul > li.active')
questions.explanation: querySelector is the only method that accepts full CSS selector syntax. The others only accept simple ID names, class names, or tag names. For complex selectors with combinators (>, +, ~) and chained classes/tags, querySelector is the right tool.

questions.answer: getElementsByClassName returns an HTMLCollection, which does NOT have a .forEach() method. Use Array.from(items).forEach(...) or use document.querySelectorAll('.item') instead.
questions.explanation: HTMLCollection (from getElementsByClassName and getElementsByTagName) does not support .forEach(). Only NodeList (from querySelectorAll) does. Fix it by converting to an array with `Array.from(items)` or `[...items]`, or better yet, use `document.querySelectorAll('.item')` which returns a NodeList that supports .forEach().

questions.prompt: Write code to select ALL <a> tags on a page and log the total number of links found.
questions.starter_code: // Select all anchor tags and log the count
questions.hint: Use querySelectorAll with the tag name 'a', then access .length

lessons.title: Changing Content: textContent, innerHTML, and value
objectives: Change text content with textContent
Understand innerHTML and its use cases
Know the security risks of innerHTML (XSS)
Read and set form input values with .value
Understand the difference between textContent, innerText, and innerHTML

sections.content:
# Changing Content: Making the Page Say What You Want

Now that you can *find* elements, it's time to **change** them. This is where DOM manipulation starts to feel like magic — you write some JavaScript, and the page updates right in front of the user's eyes.

There are three main ways to read and change content:

1. **`textContent`** — Read or set the plain text of an element
2. **`innerHTML`** — Read or set the HTML content of an element
3. **`value`** — Read or set the value of form inputs

Let's master each one.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: Editing a Sign
sections.content:
Imagine a sign on a store:

- **textContent** is like erasing the sign and writing new plain text. You can't make parts bold or add links — it's just text.
- **innerHTML** is like replacing the entire sign with a new one that can have fancy fonts, images, stickers — full formatting. But be careful: if a stranger hands you a sign to post (user input), it might have something dangerous on it.
- **value** is like the text written on a sticky note attached to the sign — it's what the customer wrote, not what the sign says.

sections.title: textContent — Reading and Setting Plain Text
sections.explanation: textContent is the safest way to read or change text. When reading, it gives you ALL the text inside the element (including nested elements), stripped of any HTML tags. When setting, it treats your string as plain text — HTML tags won't be parsed. This is important for security.

sections.title: innerHTML — Reading and Setting HTML Content
sections.explanation: innerHTML reads or sets the HTML content as a string. Unlike textContent, it PARSES HTML tags and creates real elements. This is powerful but comes with risks. Using += to append destroys and recreates all existing elements, losing any event listeners. For adding elements, we'll learn better methods later (createElement, insertAdjacentHTML).

sections.title: ⚠️ SECURITY WARNING: innerHTML and XSS Attacks
innerText vs textContent — The Subtle Difference
sections.explanation: textContent returns ALL text regardless of CSS visibility, and preserves whitespace exactly. innerText only returns visually rendered text — it skips hidden elements and collapses extra whitespace. textContent is faster because it doesn't need to check CSS styles. In most cases, textContent is what you want.

sections.content:
## Working with Form Inputs: The `.value` Property

Form elements like `<input>`, `<textarea>`, and `<select>` are special. Their content isn't their `textContent` — it's their **`.value`** property.

sections.title: .value — Reading and Setting Form Input Values
sections.explanation: For form elements (<input>, <textarea>, <select>), use .value to read what the user typed or selected. textContent and innerHTML don't work on inputs. .value gives you the current content as a string. You can also SET .value to programmatically fill in or clear form fields.

sections.title: textContent vs innerHTML vs innerText vs value
Practical Example: Dynamic Greeting
sections.explanation: This combines .value (reading the input) and textContent (setting the greeting). We use textContent (not innerHTML) because the name comes from user input — we never want to render user input as HTML. Notice the .trim() to handle whitespace-only input.

sections.title: Try It: Change Page Content Dynamically
sections.expected_output:
Headline: I Changed This with JavaScript! Description: DOM manipulation is amazing! Dynamic HTML:

✅ Content added dynamically!
sections.hint: textContent sets plain text, innerHTML can set HTML structure

questions.answer: Hello World
questions.explanation: textContent returns ALL the text inside the element and its descendants, but strips out the HTML tags. So the <strong> tags are ignored, and you get the combined text: 'Hello World'.

questions.answer: Hello World
questions.explanation: innerHTML returns the HTML content as a string, INCLUDING the HTML tags. So you get 'Hello World' with the tags preserved.

questions.prompt: A user types their name into an input field. You want to display it on the page. Which is the SAFEST approach?
questions.answer: outputElement.textContent = userInput.value;
questions.explanation: textContent is the safest because it treats everything as plain text — no HTML is parsed. If a malicious user typed '<script>alert("hacked")</script>', textContent would display it as text, not execute it. innerHTML would parse and execute it, creating a Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability.

questions.answer: "" (empty string)
questions.explanation: Input elements don't have text content — their content is stored in the .value property, not textContent. To get the input's content, use input.value, which would return 'test@email.com'.

questions.prompt: Write code that reads the text from a paragraph with id 'source', converts it to uppercase, and sets it as the text of a paragraph with id 'target'.
questions.hint: Select both elements, read .textContent from source, use .toUpperCase(), set .textContent on target

lessons.title: Changing Styles and CSS Classes
objectives: Change individual CSS properties using element.style
Add, remove, and toggle CSS classes with classList
Understand why classList is preferred over inline styles
Read computed styles with getComputedStyle
Build interactive style changes (dark mode, highlights, etc.)

sections.content:
# Changing Styles and CSS Classes

Making elements appear, disappear, change color, grow, shrink, animate — all of this involves **changing styles**. JavaScript gives you two main approaches:

1. **`element.style`** — Set CSS properties directly on the element (inline styles)
2. **`element.classList`** — Add, remove, or toggle CSS classes

Spoiler: **classList is almost always the better approach**. But you need to know both.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: Outfits vs Wardrobe
sections.content:
Think of styling an element like dressing a person:

- **element.style** (inline styles) = Manually picking each piece of clothing: 'Wear this red shirt, these blue jeans, and those white shoes.' You control every detail, but it's tedious and hard to change.

- **element.classList** (CSS classes) = Picking a pre-assembled outfit from the wardrobe: 'Wear the casual outfit' or 'Switch to the formal outfit.' The outfits are predefined in CSS, and you just swap them. Much cleaner and easier to maintain!

Just like in real life, having pre-assembled outfits (classes) is way more practical than choosing every item individually (inline styles).

## Approach 1: `element.style` — Direct Inline Styles

Every DOM element has a `.style` property that lets you read and set CSS properties directly. These become **inline styles** (like writing `style="..."` in HTML).

sections.title: Setting Styles with element.style
sections.explanation: element.style sets inline styles directly on the element. The key gotcha: CSS property names with hyphens become camelCase in JavaScript (background-color → backgroundColor). All values must be strings, including numbers with units ('48px', not 48). Setting a style to an empty string '' removes that inline style.

sections.title: element.style Only Reads INLINE Styles!
Reading Computed Styles with getComputedStyle()
sections.explanation: getComputedStyle() returns the final, computed values of all CSS properties for an element — after all stylesheets, inheritance, and calculations are applied. It's read-only; you can only use it to check what styles an element actually has. Colors are returned in rgb() format.

sections.content:
## Approach 2: `element.classList` — The Better Way ⭐

Instead of setting individual style properties, the **best practice** is to define CSS classes in your stylesheet and then use JavaScript to **add, remove, or toggle** those classes. This keeps your styles in CSS (where they belong) and your behavior logic in JavaScript.

sections.title: CSS Classes (in your stylesheet)
sections.explanation: Define your styling as CSS classes. Each class represents a visual state or modification. Then in JavaScript, you just add/remove these class names. This is much cleaner than setting 10 individual style properties.

sections.title: classList — add, remove, toggle, contains, replace
sections.explanation: classList is a powerful object with methods for manipulating CSS classes. add() and remove() can handle multiple classes at once. toggle() is perfect for on/off switches (like dark mode, showing/hiding menus). contains() lets you check if a class exists. replace() swaps one class for another. These are cleaner and more efficient than manipulating inline styles.

sections.title: Practical Example: Dark Mode Toggle
sections.explanation: This is the classic pattern: define visual states in CSS, then toggle classes with JavaScript. classList.toggle() returns a boolean telling you whether the class was added (true) or removed (false), making it perfect for if/else logic. The body element gets the class, and CSS handles cascading the dark theme to all children.

sections.title: When to Use style vs classList
sections.content:
**Use `classList` (classes) when:**
- The style represents a state (active, hidden, error, dark-mode)
- Multiple properties change together
- The same style is reused in multiple places
- You want clean, maintainable code

**Use `element.style` when:**
- The value is dynamic/calculated (e.g., `style.width = scrollPercent + '%'`)
- You're setting a single property based on user input (e.g., a color picker)
- You need to animate with JavaScript (though CSS transitions are usually better)

**90% of the time, classList is the right choice.**

sections.title: When element.style IS the Right Choice
sections.explanation: element.style shines when values are dynamic — calculated from user input, mouse position, scroll position, etc. You can't predefine a CSS class for every possible pixel position or percentage. CSS custom properties (variables) with setProperty are great for theming.

sections.title: Try It: Style a Card
sections.expected_output:
Is featured: true
Classes: card featured sold-out
sections.hint: classList.add() adds classes, .style.property sets inline styles

questions.answer: bold
questions.explanation: add('red', 'bold') adds both classes. Then remove('red') removes the 'red' class. Only 'bold' remains. The className property returns all classes as a space-separated string.

questions.answer:
false
false
questions.explanation: First, add('active') puts the class on. Then toggle('active') sees it's already there and REMOVES it, returning false (meaning 'was removed'). After toggling, 'active' is gone, so contains('active') returns false.

questions.prompt: Which approach is BEST for implementing a dark mode toggle?
choices: Setting element.style.backgroundColor and element.style.color on every element
Using innerHTML to rewrite the page with dark colors
Toggling a CSS class on the body element that cascades styles to children
Changing the <link> tag to load a different CSS file
questions.answer: Toggling a CSS class on the body element that cascades styles to children
questions.explanation: Toggling a class (like 'dark-mode') on the <body> is the cleanest approach. You define all dark mode styles in CSS using .dark-mode selectors, and JavaScript just does one simple toggle. All child elements inherit or are targeted by the CSS cascade. This keeps styling in CSS and logic in JavaScript.

questions.answer: CSS property names with hyphens must be converted to camelCase in JavaScript. Use `box.style.backgroundColor = 'blue';` instead of `box.style.background-color`.
questions.explanation: In JavaScript, the hyphen (-) is the subtraction operator, so `background-color` is interpreted as `background` minus `color`. All hyphenated CSS properties must use camelCase: backgroundColor, fontSize, borderRadius, marginTop, etc.

lessons.title: Creating and Removing Elements
objectives: Create new DOM elements with document.createElement()
Add text and attributes to newly created elements
Insert elements into the page with append, prepend, before, after
Remove elements from the DOM
Use insertAdjacentHTML for efficient HTML string insertion
Understand DocumentFragments for batch operations

sections.content:
# Creating and Removing Elements

So far we've selected existing elements and changed their content and styles. But what about creating **brand new** elements that didn't exist in the original HTML? Or removing elements from the page entirely?

This is where DOM manipulation gets really powerful. Think of:
- Adding a new item to a to-do list
- Creating a notification popup
- Building a table row from data
- Removing a deleted comment
- Adding a new message in a chat app

All of these require creating or removing DOM elements.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: Building with LEGO
sections.content:
Think of the DOM as a LEGO structure:

1. **createElement** = Manufacturing a new LEGO brick. It exists, but it's not attached to anything yet — it's sitting on your desk.
2. **Setting properties** = Painting the brick, adding stickers to it.
3. **append/prepend** = Snapping the brick onto the structure. NOW it's part of the visible model.
4. **remove** = Pulling a brick off the structure and putting it back on your desk.

Key insight: Creating an element does NOT automatically show it on the page. You must **attach it** to the DOM tree.
## Step 1: Create the Element

sections.title: document.createElement() — Building a New Element
sections.explanation: document.createElement() takes a tag name as a string and creates a brand new element of that type. The element is completely empty and disconnected from the DOM — it's not visible on the page yet. Think of it as building a component off-screen.

sections.content: ## Step 2: Configure the Element (Add Content, Classes, Attributes)

sections.title: Setting Up the New Element
sections.explanation: After creating an element, you configure it just like any other DOM element: set textContent, add classes, set attributes (href, src, alt, type, etc.), apply styles. You can use all the same properties and methods we learned in previous lessons. The element is fully functional — it's just not attached to the page yet.

sections.content: ## Step 3: Attach the Element to the Page

sections.title: Adding Elements to the DOM — append, prepend, before, after
sections.explanation:
There are four main methods for inserting elements:
- append(): Adds inside the parent, at the END
- prepend(): Adds inside the parent, at the START
- before(): Adds OUTSIDE the element, right before it (as a sibling)
- after(): Adds OUTSIDE the element, right after it (as a sibling)

These methods can accept both elements and plain text strings. You can even pass multiple items: container.append(el1, el2, 'text').

sections.title: Where append, prepend, before, after Insert Elements
sections.content:
┌── before(newEl) inserts HERE
                     │
                     ▼
    ┌────────────────────────────┐
    │  <parent>                 │
    │    ▲                      │
    │    │ prepend(newEl)        │
    │    │ inserts HERE          │
    │                           │
    │    <existing child 1>     │
    │    <existing child 2>     │
    │                           │
    │    │ append(newEl)         │
    │    ▼ inserts HERE          │
    │                           │
    └────────────────────────────┘
                     ▲
                     │
                     └── after(newEl) inserts HERE

sections.title: The Complete Create → Configure → Attach Pattern
sections.explanation: This Create → Configure → Attach pattern is the foundation of dynamic web development. You'll use it constantly — building UI from data, adding user-generated content, creating interactive components. Notice how in the card example we used innerHTML (which is OK because we control the string — it's not user input).

sections.content: ## Removing Elements

sections.title: Removing Elements from the DOM
sections.explanation: element.remove() is the simplest way to remove an element — just call it and the element disappears from the page. To remove all children, replaceChildren() is the cleanest modern method. replaceWith() lets you swap one element for another. Note: removed elements still exist in JavaScript memory if you have a variable reference — they're just not displayed on the page anymore.

sections.title: insertAdjacentHTML — A Powerful Shortcut
sections.explanation: insertAdjacentHTML is a powerful method that inserts an HTML string at one of four positions relative to the element. Unlike innerHTML +=, it doesn't destroy existing elements or their event listeners. It's great for quickly adding small pieces of HTML. The four positions correspond to: before the element, as first child, as last child, and after the element.

sections.title: DocumentFragment — Batch Insert for Performance
sections.explanation: A DocumentFragment is a lightweight, invisible container. You build your elements inside it without triggering browser repaints. When you append the fragment to the DOM, only its children are transferred — the fragment itself disappears. This is important for performance when adding many elements at once.

sections.title: Try It: Build a Card from Scratch
sections.expected_output:
JavaScript Course

Learn JS from zero to hero
sections.hint: Use createElement, set textContent, add classes, then append children to build a nested structure

questions.prompt: After `const el = document.createElement('div')`, where is this element?
choices: In JavaScript memory only — not yet visible on the page
questions.answer: In JavaScript memory only — not yet visible on the page
questions.explanation: createElement creates the element in memory but does NOT attach it to the DOM tree. It's invisible until you attach it with append(), prepend(), before(), after(), or similar methods. Think of it as building a LEGO brick on your desk — it exists but isn't part of the structure yet.

questions.answer: C, A, B
questions.explanation: prepend() adds the new element as the FIRST child. So 'C' goes before 'A' and 'B'. The order becomes: C, A, B. If we had used append(), the order would be A, B, C.

questions.prompt: What happens when you call `element.remove()`?
choices: The element is deleted from memory permanently
The element is removed from the page but still exists in memory if a variable references it
The element becomes invisible but stays in the DOM
questions.answer: The element is removed from the page but still exists in memory if a variable references it
questions.explanation: remove() detaches the element from the DOM tree — it's no longer displayed on the page. However, if you have a variable pointing to it, the element object still exists in JavaScript memory. You could even re-attach it later with append()! The garbage collector only deletes it when no references remain.

questions.prompt:
Write a function `createUserCard(name, email)` that creates a
with class 'user-card' containing an

with the name and a

with the email. Return the div element.
