questions.hint: Create a div, create h3 and p, set their textContent, append them to the div, return the div

questions.prompt: Why is `insertAdjacentHTML` preferred over `innerHTML +=` for adding content?
choices: insertAdjacentHTML is faster and doesn't destroy existing elements or event listeners
insertAdjacentHTML is more secure against XSS
innerHTML += doesn't work in modern browsers
questions.answer: insertAdjacentHTML is faster and doesn't destroy existing elements or event listeners
questions.explanation: innerHTML += converts ALL existing content to a string, appends the new HTML string, then re-parses EVERYTHING — destroying all existing DOM elements and their event listeners in the process. insertAdjacentHTML only parses and inserts the new HTML, leaving existing elements untouched. Both have the same XSS risks with user input.

lessons.title: Traversing the DOM: Navigating the Family Tree
objectives: Navigate from one element to its parent, children, and siblings
Understand the difference between Node and Element traversal properties
Use parentElement, children, nextElementSibling, previousElementSibling
Use closest() to find ancestor elements
Combine traversal with selection for powerful DOM navigation

sections.content:
# Traversing the DOM: Navigating Between Related Elements

Sometimes you don't want to search the entire document — you already have one element and want to get to a **related** element: its parent, its children, or its siblings.

This is called **DOM traversal** — walking the tree from one node to another.

Remember our family tree analogy? If you're looking at a person, you can navigate:
- **Up** → to their parent
- **Down** → to their children
- **Sideways** → to their siblings (same parent)

sections.title: Our Working HTML
sections.code:
My App

  • Alice
  • Bob
  • Charlie

Footer text
sections.explanation: We'll use this HTML structure to practice navigating between elements. Think of <div id='app'> as the grandparent, <main> and <header> as parents, and the <li> elements as children.

sections.title: Navigating UP — parentElement
sections.explanation: parentElement gives you the immediate parent of an element. You can chain it to go up multiple levels. When there's no parent (at the top of the tree), it returns null.

sections.title: Navigating DOWN — children, firstElementChild, lastElementChild
sections.explanation: children returns an HTMLCollection of direct child ELEMENTS only (no text nodes, no comments). firstElementChild and lastElementChild are shortcuts to the first and last child elements. These only go one level deep — they don't include grandchildren.

sections.title: Navigating SIDEWAYS — Siblings
sections.explanation: nextElementSibling and previousElementSibling move sideways to adjacent elements with the same parent. They return null when there's no next/previous sibling. You can loop through all siblings by starting at firstElementChild and following nextElementSibling until it's null.

sections.title: Element vs Node Traversal — IMPORTANT!
sections.content:
The DOM has TWO sets of traversal properties. Always use the **Element** versions:

| Element Properties (USE THESE ✅) | Node Properties (AVOID ❌) |
|---|---|
| `parentElement` | `parentNode` |
| `children` | `childNodes` |
| `firstElementChild` | `firstChild` |
| `lastElementChild` | `lastChild` |
| `nextElementSibling` | `nextSibling` |
| `previousElementSibling` | `previousSibling` |

The **Node** versions include text nodes (whitespace, newlines) and comment nodes — which are almost never what you want. For example, `childNodes` includes the whitespace between tags as text nodes!

```javascript
const list = document.getElementById('user-list');
console.log(list.childNodes.length);   // 7!? (includes text nodes)
console.log(list.children.length);     // 3 (just the <li> elements)
```

sections.title: closest() — Find the Nearest Ancestor Matching a Selector
sections.explanation: closest() is incredibly useful — it walks up the DOM tree from the current element, checking each ancestor against a CSS selector, and returns the first match. If the element itself matches, it returns itself. If no ancestor matches, it returns null. This is the opposite direction of querySelector (which searches DOWN/within).

sections.title: DOM Traversal Directions
sections.content:
parentElement
                         ▲
                         │  closest(selector)
                         │  keeps going up ↑
     previousElement ◄───┤───► nextElement
       Sibling           │      Sibling
                         │
              ┌──────────┼──────────┐
              │          │          │
              ▼          ▼          ▼
          children[0] children[1] children[2]
          (first       (middle)   (last
          ElementChild)           ElementChild)

sections.title: Practical Example: Highlight Active Navigation
sections.explanation: This shows a common real-world pattern: combining closest() (to navigate up) with querySelectorAll (to search down) to manage related elements. You'll use this pattern constantly for interactive UIs — navigation menus, tabs, accordions, and more.

sections.title: Try It: Navigate the DOM Tree
sections.expected_output:
First child: Introduction
Next section: main
Parent ID: wrapper
Intro children: 3
sections.hint: Use firstElementChild to go down, nextElementSibling to go sideways, parentElement to go up

questions.prompt:
```html
  • A
  • B
  • C
``` ```javascript const list = document.getElementById('list'); console.log(list.lastElementChild.previousElementSibling.textContent); ```
questions.answer: B
questions.explanation:
list.lastElementChild is the
  • C
    • . Then .previousElementSibling goes to the element before it, which is
  • B
    • . Its textContent is 'B'.

questions.prompt: What does `element.closest('.container')` do?
choices: Finds the nearest child element with class 'container'
Walks UP the DOM tree to find the nearest ancestor (or self) matching '.container'
Finds the closest sibling with class 'container'
Returns the distance to the nearest '.container' element
questions.answer: Walks UP the DOM tree to find the nearest ancestor (or self) matching '.container'
questions.explanation: closest() searches UPWARD from the current element. It checks the element itself first, then its parent, then its grandparent, and so on, returning the first element that matches the CSS selector. If no match is found, it returns null.

questions.prompt: Why should you use `children` instead of `childNodes`?
choices: children returns only element nodes, while childNodes includes text nodes and whitespace
children returns more elements than childNodes
questions.answer: children returns only element nodes, while childNodes includes text nodes and whitespace
questions.explanation: childNodes includes ALL node types — including whitespace text nodes (from newlines and indentation in HTML) and comment nodes. children filters to only element nodes (actual HTML tags), which is almost always what you want when traversing the DOM.

questions.prompt:
```html
Hello
``` ```javascript const span = document.querySelector('span'); console.log(span.nextElementSibling); ```
questions.answer: null
questions.explanation: The <span> is the only child element of #parent. It has no next sibling, so nextElementSibling returns null.

lessons.title: Working with Attributes and Data Attributes
objectives: Get, set, and remove HTML attributes with getAttribute/setAttribute/removeAttribute
Understand the difference between HTML attributes and DOM properties
Use data-* attributes to store custom data on elements
Access data attributes with the dataset property
Build dynamic UIs using data attributes

sections.content:
# Working with Attributes and Data Attributes

HTML elements have **attributes** — extra information attached to tags like `id`, `class`, `href`, `src`, `type`, `disabled`, `placeholder`, and many more. JavaScript can read, change, add, and remove any attribute.

But there's something even more powerful: **data attributes** (`data-*`). These are custom attributes YOU define to store any extra information on an element. They're the secret weapon for building dynamic, interactive UIs.

sections.title: getAttribute() and setAttribute() — The General-Purpose Methods
sections.explanation: getAttribute(), setAttribute(), removeAttribute(), and hasAttribute() are the universal methods for working with ANY HTML attribute. setAttribute takes two arguments: the attribute name and its value (both as strings). getAttribute returns the attribute value as a string, or null if the attribute doesn't exist.

sections.title: Attributes vs Properties — A Subtle Difference
Common DOM Properties (Shorthand for Standard Attributes)
sections.explanation: For standard HTML attributes, using the DOM property (dot notation) is cleaner and often behaves more intuitively, especially for boolean attributes like checked, disabled, and required. With properties, true/false works correctly. With setAttribute, any string value (even 'false') makes the attribute present and truthy.

sections.content:
## Data Attributes: Your Custom Data Storage ⭐

HTML5 introduced **data attributes** — custom attributes that start with `data-`. You can make up any name you want after `data-`, and store any string value. This lets you attach custom information to elements without abusing other attributes.

They're used everywhere: product IDs, user roles, state tracking, animation parameters, and more.

sections.title: Data Attributes in HTML
sections.code:
John Doe

Administrator

  • Headphones
  • Speaker
  • USB Cable
sections.explanation: Data attributes follow the pattern data-[your-name]='value'. You can name them whatever makes sense: data-user-id, data-price, data-action, data-category, etc. The values are always strings in HTML, but you can parse them as numbers or booleans in JavaScript.

sections.title: Accessing Data Attributes with .dataset
sections.explanation: The dataset property is a clean way to access all data-* attributes. Important rules: 1) Hyphenated names are converted to camelCase (data-user-id → dataset.userId). 2) All values are strings — convert with Number(), parseInt(), or JSON.parse() as needed. 3) You can set and delete properties on dataset to modify the HTML.

sections.title: Practical Example: Product Filter Using Data Attributes
sections.explanation: This shows the real power of data attributes: storing product metadata (category, price) directly on elements, then using that data for filtering and calculations. The data-filter on buttons tells us what filter to apply. The data-category on products tells us each product's category. The data-price lets us calculate totals. No separate data storage needed!

sections.definition: Custom HTML attributes prefixed with 'data-' that let you store extra information on any element. Accessed in JavaScript via element.dataset. Perfect for linking DOM elements to data (IDs, states, metadata) without using non-standard attributes or hidden inputs.

sections.title: Try It: Work with Data Attributes
sections.expected_output:
Name: Alice
Age: 25 number
Premium: true
Status: active
All data: {userName: 'Alice', age: '25', premium: 'true', status: 'active'}
sections.hint: Use dataset to access data attributes. Hyphenated names become camelCase.

questions.answer:
42
string
questions.explanation: data-product-id becomes dataset.productId (camelCase conversion). The value '42' is accessed. data-in-stock becomes dataset.inStock. Even though it looks like a boolean, ALL data attribute values are strings — typeof returns 'string'. You'd need to compare with === 'true' to use it as a boolean.

questions.prompt: How do you add a `data-color="blue"` attribute to an element using JavaScript?
questions.answer: element.dataset.color = 'blue'
questions.explanation: The dataset property provides access to all data-* attributes. Setting dataset.color = 'blue' adds/updates the data-color attribute on the HTML element. You could also use setAttribute('data-color', 'blue'), but dataset is cleaner.

questions.prompt: What's the correct way to uncheck a checkbox in JavaScript?
questions.answer: checkbox.checked = false
questions.explanation: For boolean HTML attributes (checked, disabled, required), always use the DOM property with true/false. setAttribute with 'false' would actually make the attribute present (any string value counts as truthy for HTML boolean attributes). checkbox.checked = false correctly unchecks the box.

questions.prompt: Write code to select all elements with a `data-price` attribute and log each product name and price.
questions.hint: Use querySelectorAll('[data-price]') to find elements with that data attribute, then forEach to iterate and log textContent and dataset.price

lessons.title: Mini Project: Building a Dynamic To-Do List
objectives: Combine all DOM manipulation skills into a working project
Handle user input from forms
Create, configure, and append new elements dynamically
Toggle element states with classList
Remove elements from the DOM
Use data attributes for element identification
Apply best practices for DOM manipulation

sections.content:
# Mini Project: Building a Dynamic To-Do List

Time to put everything together! We'll build a fully functional to-do list that lets users:

1. ✅ **Add** new tasks
2. ✅ **Mark tasks** as complete (toggle)
3. ✅ **Delete** tasks
4. ✅ **Count** remaining tasks
5. ✅ **Clear** all completed tasks

This project uses **every skill** from this module:
- Selecting elements (`querySelector`, `getElementById`)
- Changing content (`textContent`)
- Changing styles (`classList`)
- Creating elements (`createElement`)
- Removing elements (`remove()`)
- Traversing the DOM (`parentElement`, `closest`)
- Data attributes (`dataset`)

Let's build it step by step!

sections.title: Step 1: The HTML Structure
sections.code:
📝 To-Do List

0 tasks remaining
sections.explanation: This is the complete HTML and CSS for our to-do app. Notice: the <ul> is empty — all tasks will be created dynamically with JavaScript. The CSS includes styles for both normal and completed states (.completed class). The script tag is at the bottom of body so all elements exist when JS runs.

sections.content:
## Now let's build the JavaScript piece by piece.

We'll start by selecting all the elements we need, then build each feature.

sections.title: Step 2: Select Elements and Set Up Variables
sections.explanation: We grab references to every interactive element on the page and store them in const variables. This is always Step 1 in any DOM project — select your elements first, then work with them.

sections.title: Step 3: The addTask Function — Creating New To-Do Items
sections.explanation: This function follows the Create → Configure → Attach pattern. We create three elements (li, span, button), configure them with classes, text content, and data attributes, then assemble them (span and button go inside li) and finally attach the li to the todo list. The data-task-id attribute gives each task a unique identifier.

sections.title: Step 4: Toggle Complete and Delete Functions
sections.explanation: toggleTask uses classList.toggle('completed') — one line to switch between complete/incomplete states. The CSS handles the visual change (opacity, strikethrough). deleteTask calls remove() to take the element out of the DOM. updateTaskCount uses querySelectorAll with the :not(.completed) CSS pseudo-class to count only incomplete tasks.

sections.title: Step 5: Wire Up Event Listeners
sections.explanation: This is where everything comes together! We use addEventListener (which we'll cover deeply in Module 11) to handle user interactions. Key technique: Event Delegation — instead of adding click listeners to every task (which would be impractical since tasks are created dynamically), we add ONE listener to the parent <ul> and use event.target + closest() to figure out what was clicked. This is a professional pattern you'll use constantly.

sections.title: Key Pattern: Event Delegation with closest()
Step 6: The Complete app.js
sections.explanation: Here's the complete, clean JavaScript file. In about 55 lines, we have a fully functional to-do application. Notice how every DOM skill from this module is used: selection (getElementById, querySelector, querySelectorAll), content (textContent), classes (classList.add, toggle, contains), creation (createElement), removal (remove()), traversal (closest()), and data attributes (dataset.taskId).

sections.title: DOM Best Practices Summary
sections.content:
Looking back at this project, here are the best practices we followed:

1. **Cache your selections** — Store elements in variables at the top, don't re-query the DOM repeatedly
2. **Use textContent for user data** — Never innerHTML with user input (XSS protection)
3. **Prefer classList over inline styles** — Keeps styling in CSS, logic in JS
4. **Use data attributes for metadata** — Don't abuse class names or IDs for data storage
5. **Event delegation** — Listen on parents, not individual dynamic children
6. **Separate concerns** — Functions do one thing each (addTask, deleteTask, toggleTask)
7. **Always update the UI** — After any data change, update what the user sees (task count)

sections.title: 🏆 Challenge: Extend the To-Do List!
steps: Add an 'Edit' button that lets users rename a task (replace span with an input, then back to span on Enter)
Add a 'due date' feature using data-due-date and highlight overdue tasks
Add priority levels (high/medium/low) using data-priority and color-code them
Add a filter bar: 'All', 'Active', 'Completed' — show/hide tasks accordingly
Save tasks to localStorage so they survive page refreshes (research localStorage)
Add drag-and-drop reordering (advanced — research the drag events API)

questions.prompt: Write a function `createListItem(text)` that creates an <li> element with the given text and a delete button (a <button> with text '✕' and class 'delete-btn'). Return the <li> element.
questions.hint: Create an li, create a span for text, create a button for delete, append both to li, return li

questions.prompt: In the to-do list project, why do we add the click event listener to the <ul> instead of to each <li>?
choices: Because <li> elements can't have event listeners
Because <ul> events fire faster than <li> events
Because new <li> elements are created dynamically and wouldn't have the listener — delegating to the parent handles all current and future items
Because the HTML spec requires events on list containers
questions.answer: Because new <li> elements are created dynamically and wouldn't have the listener — delegating to the parent handles all current and future items
questions.explanation: Event delegation! When tasks are created with createElement and append, they don't automatically get event listeners. Instead of adding a listener every time we create a new <li>, we put ONE listener on the parent <ul>. Click events bubble UP from the <li> to the <ul>, where our listener catches them. This works for all items — including ones created after the listener was added.

questions.answer: Create → Configure → Attach
questions.explanation: This is the classic Create → Configure → Attach pattern. Step 1: createElement creates the element in memory. Step 2: Setting textContent and adding classes configures it. Step 3: append attaches it to the DOM, making it visible on the page. This is the fundamental pattern for dynamic element creation.

After removing all items, list.children.length is 0 — no children remain in the DOM. However, items.length is still 3! querySelectorAll returns a STATIC NodeList — it's a snapshot taken at the time of the query. The NodeList still contains references to the 3 removed elements (they exist in memory, just not in the DOM). This is important to understand about static vs live collections.

questions.prompt: Write a function `clearAndReplaceContent(elementId, newHTML)` that empties an element's contents and inserts new HTML. Use the safest efficient method.
questions.hint: Select by ID, use replaceChildren() or innerHTML = '' to clear, then insertAdjacentHTML or innerHTML to add new content

lessons.title: What Are Events? (The Doorbell Analogy)
objectives: Understand what browser events are and why they matter
Know the most common types of events
Use addEventListener to respond to events
Grasp the concept of event-driven programming
Remove event listeners when they're no longer needed

sections.content:
# Events — The Heartbeat of Interactive Web Pages

In Module 10, you learned to change the page — modify text, add elements, toggle classes. But all those changes happened when YOUR code ran. The user couldn't trigger any of it.

**Events** change everything. They let the user drive the action. A click, a keypress, a scroll, a form submission, the page finishing loading — these are all events. Your JavaScript can **listen** for specific events and **respond** to them.

This is called **event-driven programming**, and it's how every interactive website on the planet works. Without events, the web would be a collection of static documents. With events, it becomes an application platform.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: The Doorbell 🔔
sections.content:
Think of events like a doorbell system:

🔔 **The doorbell ring** = The event (something happened — someone pressed the button)
👂 **The listener** = The mechanism waiting for the bell to ring (`addEventListener`)
🏃 **Your reaction** = The callback function (you go open the door)

You don't stand at the door 24/7 staring at it. Instead, you **install a listener** (the doorbell) and go about your business. When the event fires (button pressed), your response function runs (you answer the door).

You can have **multiple doorbells**: one at the front door (click event), one at the back door (keypress event), one for deliveries (form submit). Each one has its own response.

This is exactly how JavaScript events work!

sections.title: Your First Event Listener
sections.explanation: addEventListener takes two arguments: 1) The event type as a string ('click', 'keydown', etc.), and 2) A callback function that runs when the event occurs. The most critical mistake is calling the function with parentheses — handleClick() executes immediately and passes undefined as the callback.

sections.title: Common Event Types
rows: An input loses focus after its value changed

sections.title: Multiple Listeners & Multiple Events
sections.explanation: You can attach multiple listeners to the same element, even for the same event type. They all run in the order they were added. Different event types let you respond to different interactions on a single element — hover, click, keyboard, etc.

sections.title: Don't Use onclick= in HTML (Usually)
sections.content:
You might see inline event handlers in old code:
```html
<button onclick="alert('Clicked!')">Click</button>
```

Or the older JavaScript property approach:
```javascript
button.onclick = function() { alert('Clicked!'); };
```

**Problems with these approaches:**
- Inline HTML mixes structure and behavior — hard to maintain
- The `.onclick` property can only have ONE handler per event (setting a second overwrites the first)
- addEventListener allows multiple handlers AND can be removed later

**Always use `addEventListener`** — it's the modern, professional approach.

sections.title: Removing Event Listeners
sections.explanation: To remove a listener, you need the exact same function reference — anonymous functions can't be removed. The { once: true } option is the cleanest way for one-time events. AbortController lets you batch-remove multiple listeners at once — very useful for cleanup.

sections.title: Try It: Click Counter with Reset
sections.expected_output: Clicking 'Count' increments the number and changes color. Clicking 'Reset' returns to 0.
sections.hint: Track count in a variable. Update the display's textContent on each click.

questions.prompt: What are the two arguments that addEventListener expects?
choices: A string (event type) and a function (callback)
questions.answer: A string (event type) and a function (callback)
questions.explanation: addEventListener takes: 1) A string naming the event type (like 'click', 'keydown', 'submit'), and 2) A callback function that executes when that event occurs. Optionally, a third argument can provide options like { once: true }.

questions.prompt:
What happens when you write:
```javascript
button.addEventListener('click', sayHello());
```
where sayHello is a function that logs 'Hello!'?
questions.answer: sayHello() runs IMMEDIATELY (when this line executes, not on click), and its return value (undefined) is passed as the callback. The click event won't do anything useful.
questions.explanation: The parentheses () after sayHello CALL the function immediately. You want to PASS the function reference, not call it. Use sayHello (without parentheses) to pass the function itself. This is one of the most common beginner mistakes with event listeners!

questions.prompt: Why can't you remove an anonymous arrow function listener?
choices: Arrow functions can't be event listeners
removeEventListener needs the EXACT same function reference, and you can't reference an anonymous function
questions.answer: removeEventListener needs the EXACT same function reference, and you can't reference an anonymous function
questions.explanation: Each () => {} creates a NEW function object in memory. Even if two arrow functions have identical code, they're different objects. removeEventListener compares by reference, not by code. To remove listeners, store the function in a variable first, or use { once: true } / AbortController.

lessons.title: The Event Object — What Just Happened?
objectives: Access the event object in event handler callbacks
Use event.target to identify what was clicked
Use event.preventDefault() to stop default browser behavior
Extract useful information from different event types
Know the difference between event.target and event.currentTarget

sections.content:
# The Event Object — The Crime Scene Report

When an event occurs, the browser doesn't just say "something happened" — it creates a detailed **event object** packed with information about *exactly* what happened. Where was the click? What key was pressed? Was Shift held down? Was it the left or right mouse button?

The event object is automatically passed to your callback function. You just need to name the parameter.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: The Security Camera Report 📹
sections.content:
When something happens in a building, the security system creates a detailed report:

📍 **Where** it happened → `event.target` (the exact element)
⏰ **When** it happened → `event.timeStamp`
🔍 **What type** of event → `event.type` ('click', 'keydown', etc.)
📐 **Coordinates** → `event.clientX`, `event.clientY`
⌨️ **What key** was pressed → `event.key`
🔘 **Which button** → `event.button`
🔑 **Modifier keys** → `event.shiftKey`, `event.ctrlKey`, `event.altKey`

The event object IS this detailed report — it tells you everything about the event.

sections.title: Accessing the Event Object
sections.explanation: The browser automatically passes the event object as the first argument to your callback function. You just need to name the parameter (commonly 'event', 'e', or 'evt'). This object contains everything you'd want to know about the event.

sections.definition: The `target` property of the event object refers to the **actual DOM element that the event originated from** — the exact element the user interacted with. If you click on bold text inside a button, event.target is the <strong> element, not the button. This is incredibly useful for event delegation and identifying what was clicked.

sections.title: event.target in Action
sections.explanation: Instead of adding separate click listeners to each color swatch, we add ONE listener to the parent. event.target tells us exactly which child was clicked. We check if it has the 'color' class before processing. This pattern is called 'event delegation' — we'll explore it deeply in Lesson 4.

sections.title: event.preventDefault() — Taking Control from the Browser
sections.explanation: preventDefault is essential for forms — without it, submitting a form reloads the page, losing all your JavaScript state. By preventing the default, you handle form data entirely with JavaScript (and later send it to a server with fetch). It also lets you create custom link behavior, custom keyboard input restrictions, and navigation confirmations.

sections.title: event.target vs event.currentTarget
sections.explanation: target is the actual element that was clicked/interacted with. currentTarget is the element that has the event listener attached. These are the same when you click the exact element, but different when a child triggers the event and it bubbles up to the parent's listener. This distinction is critical for event delegation.

sections.title: Try It: Mouse Position Tracker
sections.expected_output: The text updates with the mouse coordinates as you move the mouse around the page.
sections.hint: Use event.clientX and event.clientY for viewport-relative coordinates.

questions.prompt: What does `event.target` refer to?
choices: The element that the event listener is attached to
The actual element that triggered/originated the event
The parent element of the clicked element
questions.answer: The actual element that triggered/originated the event
questions.explanation: event.target is the specific element the user interacted with. If you click a <span> inside a <button>, event.target is the <span>. The element with the listener attached is event.currentTarget. This distinction matters when using event delegation.

questions.prompt: Why would you call `event.preventDefault()` on a form's submit event?
choices: To stop the browser from refreshing the page, so you can handle the form data with JavaScript
To prevent the user from typing
questions.answer: To stop the browser from refreshing the page, so you can handle the form data with JavaScript
questions.explanation: By default, submitting a form causes the browser to reload the page (or navigate to the form's action URL). preventDefault stops this, allowing JavaScript to capture and process the form data — essential for modern web apps that handle everything client-side.

questions.answer: 'Enter' — e.key returns the logical key name as a string.
questions.explanation: e.key returns the logical key name. For Enter, it returns the string 'Enter'. Other examples: 'a' for the A key, 'Shift' for Shift, 'ArrowUp' for the up arrow, ' ' (space) for the spacebar.

lessons.title: Mouse & Keyboard Events Deep Dive
objectives: Master mouse events: click, dblclick, mousemove, mouseenter, mouseleave
Master keyboard events: keydown, keyup, and the event.key property
Build interactive features like drag visualization and keyboard shortcuts
Understand mouse coordinate systems (clientX, pageX, offsetX)

sections.content:
# Mouse and Keyboard Events — Complete Mastery

Let's go deep on the two most common event categories. Understanding the nuances of mouse coordinates, modifier keys, and keyboard shortcuts will let you build polished, professional interactions.

sections.title: Mouse Event Types & Coordinates
sections.explanation: Mouse events provide different coordinate systems. clientX/Y is relative to the browser viewport. pageX/Y includes scrolling. offsetX/Y is relative to the element — ideal for drawing on canvases and positioning within an element. The button property tells you which mouse button was pressed.

sections.title: mouseenter/mouseleave vs mouseover/mouseout
rows: Yes — fires when entering/leaving children too
Yes — fires repeatedly over nested elements
Delegated hover events on child elements

sections.title: Keyboard Events Deep Dive
sections.explanation: e.key gives the logical key ('a', 'Enter') while e.code gives the physical position ('KeyA', 'Enter'). The keyboard shortcut system builds a string from modifier keys + the pressed key, then looks it up in a shortcuts object. preventDefault stops the browser's built-in shortcuts from interfering.

sections.title: Practical: Drawing App (Mouse Events Combined)
sections.explanation: This drawing app demonstrates coordinating multiple mouse events: mousedown starts drawing, mousemove draws while the button is held, and mouseup/mouseleave stops. The isDrawing flag tracks state across events. offsetX/offsetY give coordinates relative to the canvas element. This state-tracking pattern is fundamental to drag-and-drop, sliders, and interactive graphics.

questions.prompt: What's the difference between event.clientX and event.offsetX?
choices: clientX is relative to the viewport; offsetX is relative to the target element
clientX includes scroll position; offsetX doesn't
offsetX is for keyboard events; clientX is for mouse events
questions.answer: clientX is relative to the viewport; offsetX is relative to the target element
questions.explanation: clientX/Y measures from the top-left of the browser viewport. offsetX/Y measures from the top-left corner of the element that received the event. offsetX is best for drawing, positioning within elements, and anything relative to a specific element.

questions.answer: e.key will be 'A' (uppercase), and e.shiftKey will be true
questions.explanation: When Shift is held, e.key returns the uppercase character 'A'. The e.shiftKey property is a boolean that tells you whether the Shift key was held during the event. e.code would still be 'KeyA' regardless of Shift.

questions.prompt: Which mouse event pair is best for simple hover effects on a single element?
questions.answer: mouseenter and mouseleave
questions.explanation: mouseenter/mouseleave fire only once for the target element and don't fire again when entering/leaving child elements. mouseover/mouseout fire on every child element too, which can cause flickering effects. Use mouseenter/mouseleave for simple hover effects.

lessons.title: Event Bubbling, Capturing & Delegation
objectives: Understand event propagation: bubbling and capturing phases
Use event.stopPropagation() when needed
Master event delegation for efficient event handling
Use closest() and matches() for robust delegation
Know when and why to use delegation over direct listeners

sections.content:
# Event Bubbling & Delegation — The Most Important Event Pattern

Here's something surprising: when you click a button, the click event doesn't ONLY fire on that button. It **bubbles up** through every ancestor element, all the way to the document root. This behavior is called **event bubbling**, and understanding it unlocks the most powerful pattern in DOM programming: **event delegation**.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: The Ripple Effect 🌊
sections.content:
Imagine dropping a pebble into a pond:

💧 The **splash point** = The clicked element (event.target)
🌊 The **ripples spreading outward** = Event bubbling up through ancestors

The ripple starts at the exact point where the pebble hit (the button) and spreads outward in concentric circles (parent → grandparent → body → html → document).

Anyone standing at the edge of the pond (an ancestor with a listener) can notice the ripple. That's event delegation — listening at a higher level and catching events from descendants.

sections.title: Watching Events Bubble
sections.explanation: When the button is clicked, the event fires on the button first (target phase), then bubbles up to the parent div, then to the grandparent div, then to body, html, and document. Each ancestor with a click listener gets triggered in order. This is how a parent can 'hear' clicks on its children.

sections.title: Event Propagation Phases
sections.content:
When you click the <button>:

Phase 1: CAPTURING (rarely used)    Phase 3: BUBBLING (default)
top → down                          bottom → up

  document  ───────►                 ◄─────── document
    html    ───────►                 ◄───────   html
    body    ───────►                 ◄───────   body
 #grandparent ────►                 ◄──── #grandparent
   #parent  ───────►                 ◄───────  #parent
   #child   ═══════ TARGET PHASE ═══════  #child
                   (event fires here)

By default, listeners run during the BUBBLING phase.
To use capturing: addEventListener('click', fn, true)
sections.description: Diagram showing the three phases of event propagation: capturing (top-down), target (at the element), and bubbling (bottom-up).

sections.title: stopPropagation() — Stopping the Bubble
sections.explanation: stopPropagation prevents the event from traveling further up the tree. Use it when clicking a child should NOT trigger the parent's handler. But use it sparingly — it can break event delegation patterns. Often, checking e.target in the parent handler is a cleaner solution.

sections.definition: A pattern where you attach a SINGLE event listener to a **parent** element instead of individual listeners on each child. When a child fires an event, it bubbles up to the parent where you handle it. You use `event.target` (and `closest()` / `matches()`) to identify which child was actually interacted with. **Benefits:** Fewer listeners (better performance), works with dynamically added elements, less code to manage.

sections.title: Event Delegation — The Professional Pattern
sections.explanation: Event delegation puts ONE listener on the parent. When anything inside is clicked, we use closest() to figure out what was clicked and respond appropriately. This handles ALL current AND future child elements. It's fewer listeners, less memory, less code, and automatically works with dynamically added elements.

sections.title: matches() and closest() — Delegation Helpers
sections.explanation: matches() checks if an element fits a selector. closest() walks up to find an ancestor. Together, they make delegation robust. The icon-inside-button problem is very common: users click the icon, but you need to find the button. closest() handles this perfectly.

sections.title: How Twitter/X Uses Event Delegation
sections.company: Twitter/X, Facebook, Instagram, YouTube, Reddit — all major platforms

questions.prompt: What is event bubbling?
choices: Events only fire on the exact element you clicked
Events fire on the target element and then propagate UP through every ancestor
Events fire on all elements at the same time
Events only fire on parent elements, not children
questions.answer: Events fire on the target element and then propagate UP through every ancestor
questions.explanation: Event bubbling means the event starts at the target (where it originated) and propagates upward through each ancestor: parent → grandparent → ... → body → html → document. Each ancestor with a listener for that event type gets triggered.

questions.prompt: What is the main advantage of event delegation?
choices: One listener on a parent handles all children — including dynamically added ones
It only works with click events
questions.answer: One listener on a parent handles all children — including dynamically added ones
questions.explanation: Event delegation uses bubbling to your advantage. One listener on a parent can handle events from all children. Huge benefits: fewer listeners (better performance), automatic handling of dynamically added elements, and centralized event logic.

questions.answer: Only 'Child' is logged. 'Parent' is NOT logged because stopPropagation prevents bubbling.
questions.explanation: stopPropagation() stops the event from bubbling further up the DOM tree. The child's handler fires and logs 'Child', then propagation stops. The parent's handler never receives the event, so 'Parent' is never logged.

questions.prompt: Why do we use `e.target.closest('.delete-btn')` instead of just `e.target.matches('.delete-btn')` in delegation?
choices: If the button contains an icon or span, e.target might be the child element. closest() walks up to find the actual button.
questions.answer: If the button contains an icon or span, e.target might be the child element. closest() walks up to find the actual button.
questions.explanation: Buttons often contain icons: . If the user clicks the icon, e.target is the , not the button. e.target.matches('.delete-btn') would be false! closest('.delete-btn') walks up from the and finds the button — much more robust.

lessons.title: Form Events & Input Handling
objectives: Handle form submission with the submit event and preventDefault
Track input changes with input, change, focus, and blur events
Build real-time validation with visual feedback
Extract form data using FormData API
Know how to access values from every input type

sections.content:
# Form Events — Capturing User Input Like a Pro

Forms are how users communicate with your web app: login credentials, search queries, registration info, comments, orders. Handling forms properly is one of the most important and most common tasks in web development.

sections.title: The submit Event — Handling Form Submission
sections.explanation: Always call e.preventDefault() on form submit — otherwise the page refreshes and you lose everything. The FormData API is the modern, clean way to extract all form values at once. Object.fromEntries(formData) converts it to a regular object. form.reset() clears all fields after successful submission.

sections.title: Input Events Compared
rows: Real-time validation, live search, character counters
When the field loses focus AND value changed
When a field gains focus (clicked/tabbed into)
Showing help text, highlighting the field
When a field loses focus (clicked/tabbed away)
When the form is submitted (button click or Enter)

sections.title: Real-Time Validation with input and blur
sections.explanation: Use 'input' for instant feedback while typing (username length/format). Use 'blur' for validation when the user moves on (email format). Use 'focus' to clear messages when they return to edit. This gives a smooth, professional user experience.

sections.title: Accessing Values from Different Input Types
sections.explanation: Every input type has its own way of accessing the value. Most use .value (returns a string). Checkboxes use .checked (boolean). Number/date inputs have .valueAsNumber/.valueAsDate for typed values. Radio buttons need the :checked pseudo-selector. File inputs use .files. Know these and you can handle any form.

sections.title: Practical: Password Strength Meter
sections.explanation: A password strength meter provides instant feedback as the user types. It checks for length, uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and special characters. The colored bar and label update on every keystroke using the 'input' event. This is a feature users expect on modern signup forms.

sections.title: The FormData Cheat Sheet

questions.prompt: Which event fires on EVERY keystroke in an input field?
questions.answer: input
questions.explanation: The 'input' event fires every time the value changes — on every keystroke, paste, or other input method. 'change' only fires after the field loses focus. 'blur' fires when the field loses focus regardless of changes. 'submit' fires when the whole form is submitted.

questions.prompt: What does `document.querySelector('input[type="checkbox"]').value` return for a checkbox?
questions.answer: It returns the value attribute string (like 'on' by default), NOT whether it's checked. Use .checked (boolean) to check if a checkbox is checked.
questions.explanation: Checkboxes are special: .value gives the value attribute (often 'on'), while .checked gives a boolean indicating whether the box is checked. This trips up many beginners who expect .value to tell them the checkbox state.

questions.prompt: When is the best time to validate an email field — on 'input', 'blur', or 'submit'?
choices: On 'blur' — validate when the user leaves the field (they might not be done typing yet)
On 'submit' — only validate when the form is submitted
questions.answer: On 'blur' — validate when the user leaves the field (they might not be done typing yet)
questions.explanation: Validating email on every keystroke is annoying — 'a' is invalid, 'al' is invalid, 'ali' is invalid... The user isn't done yet! Blur fires when they move to the next field, meaning they've finished typing. That's the right time to check. Submit validation is also good as a final check.

lessons.title: Building Interactive Components
objectives: Build a functional modal/popup component
Create an accordion (collapsible sections)
Build a tab navigation system
Combine DOM manipulation and events into polished UI patterns

sections.content:
# Building Real UI Components

Now let's combine everything — DOM selection, content manipulation, styles, events, and delegation — to build real interactive components you see on actual websites. These are the building blocks of modern web interfaces.

Each component follows the same pattern:
1. **HTML structure** (the skeleton)
2. **CSS for states** (visible/hidden, active/inactive)
3. **JavaScript for behavior** (toggling classes, handling clicks)

sections.title: Component 1: Modal (Popup Dialog)
sections.explanation: A professional modal includes: 1) Opening trigger, 2) Close via button, overlay click, AND Escape key, 3) Scroll lock on the background, 4) Focus management for accessibility. We check e.target === overlay to distinguish clicking the dark background from clicking the modal content itself.

sections.title: Component 2: Tab Navigation
sections.explanation: Tabs use event delegation (one listener on the container) and data attributes (data-tab links buttons to content panels). The deactivate-all-then-activate-one pattern is the standard approach. Keyboard navigation with arrow keys makes it accessible — the modulo operator creates a circular wrap-around.

sections.title: Component 3: Accordion (Collapsible Sections)
sections.explanation: The accordion uses CSS for animation (max-height transition) and JavaScript only for toggling the 'open' class. Event delegation on the accordion container handles all headers. The 'close all then open clicked' pattern creates a single-open accordion. For multi-open, just use classList.toggle instead.

sections.title: The Component Pattern Summary
sections.content:
Every interactive component follows this pattern:

1. **Structure (HTML):** Semantic elements with meaningful classes and data attributes
2. **Visual States (CSS):** `.hidden`, `.active`, `.open`, `.selected` — CSS handles all visual changes and transitions
3. **Behavior (JS):** JavaScript ONLY toggles classes and manages state. Let CSS do the visual work.
4. **Events:** Use delegation where possible. Support keyboard navigation for accessibility.
5. **Accessibility:** `aria-*` attributes, focus management, keyboard support.

This separation makes components easy to style, maintain, and reuse.

questions.prompt: In the modal component, why do we check `e.target === overlay` when handling overlay clicks?
choices: To check if the overlay is visible
To close the modal only when the dark background is clicked, NOT when the modal content is clicked
questions.answer: To close the modal only when the dark background is clicked, NOT when the modal content is clicked
questions.explanation: The modal content is INSIDE the overlay. Without the check, clicking any part of the modal (text, buttons, form fields) would also trigger the close because the click bubbles up to the overlay. e.target === overlay ensures we only close when clicking the dark background itself.

questions.prompt: Why should JavaScript only toggle CSS classes instead of directly manipulating styles for component states?
choices: CSS handles visual presentation and transitions better, while JavaScript handles behavior. This separation makes code cleaner and more maintainable.
Toggling classes is faster to type
Inline styles don't work on all browsers
questions.answer: CSS handles visual presentation and transitions better, while JavaScript handles behavior. This separation makes code cleaner and more maintainable.
questions.explanation: When JavaScript toggles a class like 'open', CSS handles the animation (transition: max-height 0.3s). If you want to change how the accordion looks, you only change CSS — no JavaScript changes needed. This separation of concerns is a core principle of web development.

lessons.title: Module 11 Challenge: Build an Interactive Quiz App
objectives: Apply all event concepts in a complete project
Handle clicks, keyboard input, form submission, and timer events
Use event delegation for dynamically created elements
Manage application state with events driving the UI

sections.content:
# 🧠 Module 11 Challenge: Interactive Quiz App

Build a complete quiz application that tests JavaScript knowledge! This project uses every event concept from this module:

- **Click events** — Selecting answers, navigating questions
- **Keyboard events** — Number keys to select answers, Enter to continue
- **Timer events** — Countdown timer per question using setInterval
- **Event delegation** — Handling dynamically created answer buttons
- **preventDefault** — Preventing unintended form submissions
- **DOM manipulation** — Rendering questions, showing results

sections.title: Part 1: Quiz Data & State Management
sections.explanation: We define quiz data as an array of question objects, each with the question text, four options, the index of the correct answer, and an explanation. The state object tracks the current question, score, user's answers, and timer — everything the app needs to function.

sections.title: Part 2: Rendering & Timer
sections.explanation: renderQuestion builds the entire question UI with a progress bar, timer, and answer buttons. startTimer uses setInterval to count down from 15 seconds. The timer gets visual urgency classes at 5s and 3s. If time runs out, handleTimeout records a missed answer and shows the correct one.

sections.title: Part 3: Answer Handling with Events
sections.explanation: Two input methods: clicking answer buttons (event delegation on the container) and pressing number keys 1-4 (keydown on document). After answering, all buttons are disabled, correct answer is highlighted green, wrong answer red. An explanation and next button appear. The next button is also keyboard-accessible (Enter/Space). Every dynamic element is handled via delegation.

sections.title: Part 4: Results Screen & Restart
sections.explanation: The results screen shows score, percentage, grade, and a detailed breakdown of each question with the user's answer vs the correct one. Timeout answers are shown separately. The restart button resets all state and re-renders the first question. Everything uses event delegation for dynamically created elements.

sections.title: 🧠 Event Concepts Used in This Challenge
sections.content:
Every event concept from this module:

✅ **addEventListener** — Click handlers, keyboard handlers, timer setup
✅ **event.target** — Identifying which answer button was clicked
✅ **event.preventDefault()** — Preventing Space key from scrolling
✅ **Event delegation** — One listener on the container handles all dynamic buttons
✅ **closest()** — Finding the answer button from a clicked child (icon, text)
✅ **Keyboard events** — Number keys 1-4 to answer, Enter/Space for next
✅ **e.key** — Checking which key was pressed
✅ **setInterval / clearInterval** — Countdown timer
✅ **DOM manipulation** — innerHTML, insertAdjacentHTML, classList
✅ **Dynamic elements** — Answer buttons, feedback, next button created at runtime
✅ **Disabling elements** — btn.disabled = true after answering
✅ **Focus management** — Focusing next button for keyboard accessibility

**Bonus challenges:**
🌟 Add a difficulty selector (easy/medium/hard) with different timer lengths
🌟 Randomize question order on each attempt
🌟 Add a high score system using localStorage
🌟 Add sound effects for correct/wrong answers
🌟 Add a "50/50" lifeline that removes two wrong answers

questions.prompt: Why do we use event delegation for the answer buttons instead of adding listeners directly?
choices: Direct listeners are not supported in JavaScript
The answer buttons are created dynamically with innerHTML — they don't exist when the page loads
Direct listeners don't support click events
questions.answer: The answer buttons are created dynamically with innerHTML — they don't exist when the page loads
questions.explanation: The answer buttons are rendered by JavaScript when each question loads — they don't exist in the original HTML. Direct listeners added at page load would have nothing to attach to. Delegation on the parent container catches clicks from ALL buttons, including ones created later.

questions.prompt: Why do we call `clearInterval(state.timerId)` when an answer is selected?
choices: To make the timer count faster
To stop the countdown — the question has been answered so the timer is no longer needed
clearInterval is required before using setInterval again
questions.answer: To stop the countdown — the question has been answered so the timer is no longer needed
questions.explanation: Once the user answers, the countdown should stop. Without clearInterval, the timer would keep counting down to 0 and trigger handleTimeout even though the user already answered. Always clean up intervals when they're no longer needed.

questions.prompt: What happens if you click an answer button after the question is already answered (buttons are disabled)?
questions.answer: Nothing — the buttons are disabled (btn.disabled = true) AND the code checks for the 'answered' class on the container, so the click handler returns early without doing anything.
questions.explanation: Two layers of protection: 1) HTML disabled attribute prevents the browser from firing click events on the buttons, and 2) the delegation handler checks if the container has the 'answered' class and returns early. Belt AND suspenders — important for preventing double-submissions.

lessons.title: HTML Forms Meet JavaScript — The Gateway to User Data
objectives: Understand why forms are the backbone of web interactivity
Learn the default behavior of HTML forms and why JavaScript overrides it
Access form elements using JavaScript
Read and write input values programmatically

sections.content:
# HTML Forms Meet JavaScript

Every time you log into Instagram, search on Google, sign up for Netflix, or post a tweet — you're interacting with an **HTML form**. Forms are the #1 way websites collect data from users.

Up until now, you've been manipulating the DOM and responding to events like clicks. But forms take interactivity to a whole new level. They let users **input data**, and JavaScript lets you **capture, validate, and process** that data — all without leaving the page.

In the old days (early 2000s), forms would send data directly to a server, the entire page would reload, and you'd wait. Today, JavaScript intercepts the form, validates data instantly, and can send it in the background. This is the foundation of every modern web app.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy
sections.content: Think of an HTML form like a **paper application form** at a government office. The form itself has blank fields (name, email, phone). But the paper can't check if you filled it out correctly — that's the job of the **clerk** (JavaScript). The clerk reviews your form before it's submitted to the back office (server). Without the clerk, incorrect forms would clog up the system.

## The Anatomy of an HTML Form

Before we touch JavaScript, let's understand what we're working with. Here's a basic HTML form:

```html
<form id="signup-form">
  <label for="username">Username:</label>
  <input type="text" id="username" name="username" placeholder="Enter username">

  <label for="email">Email:</label>
  <input type="email" id="email" name="email" placeholder="you@example.com">

  <label for="password">Password:</label>
  <input type="password" id="password" name="password">

  <button type="submit">Sign Up</button>
</form>
```

Key things to notice:
- **`id`** — Used by JavaScript to find specific elements
- **`name`** — Used when sending data to a server (we'll use it later)
- **`type`** — Determines input behavior (`text`, `email`, `password`, `number`, `checkbox`, etc.)
- **`placeholder`** — Ghost text that hints what to type
- **`<button type="submit">`** — Triggers form submission

sections.definition: When a form is submitted (by clicking the submit button or pressing Enter), the browser's DEFAULT behavior is to collect all the input values, encode them, and send them to the URL specified in the form's `action` attribute. This causes a full page reload. In modern JavaScript apps, we PREVENT this default behavior using `event.preventDefault()` and handle the data ourselves.

sections.title: Accessing a Form and Its Inputs
sections.explanation: The `.value` property is the star of form handling. Every `<input>`, `<textarea>`, and `<select>` element has a `.value` property. Reading it gives you whatever the user typed. Setting it changes what appears in the field. This is how autofill, clearing forms, and pre-populating data works.

sections.description: A diagram showing an HTML form with three input fields (username, email, password). Arrows point from each field to JavaScript code showing `document.getElementById('...').value`. A second arrow shows the flow: User Types → .value updates → JavaScript reads .value → Data is available in your code. The diagram should emphasize that .value is the bridge between what users type and what JavaScript can access.
sections.alt_text: Diagram showing how JavaScript reads form input values through the .value property

sections.title: Accessing Inputs Through the Form Element
sections.explanation: The `form.elements` property gives you a collection of all controls (inputs, buttons, selects, textareas) inside that form. You can access them by their `name` attribute or by index. This is often cleaner than using `getElementById` for every single field, especially when forms have many inputs.

sections.title: Ways to Access Form Inputs
rows: When you need a specific element anywhere on the page
When working within a specific form — cleanest approach
When you need to reference the form itself

sections.title: Working with Different Input Types
sections.explanation: This is a critical lesson: `.value` always returns a **string**, even for `<input type='number'>`. You must convert it yourself using `Number()`, `parseInt()`, or use the `.valueAsNumber` property. For checkboxes, use `.checked` (boolean), not `.value`. For radio buttons, query for the `:checked` one. These are common beginner traps!

sections.title: The #1 Form Trap for Beginners
sections.content:
```javascript
const price = document.getElementById('price').value; // "10"
const quantity = document.getElementById('quantity').value; // "3"
console.log(price + quantity); // "103" 😱 — string concatenation!
console.log(Number(price) + Number(quantity)); // 13 ✅
```
ALWAYS convert `.value` to a number before doing math. The `.value` property returns a string, even from `<input type='number'>`.

sections.title: Try It: Read Form Values
sections.expected_output:
Name: Jane Doe
Age: 28, Type: string
Newsletter: true
In 10 years: 38
sections.hint: Remember that .value always returns a string, even from number inputs

sections.title: Pro Tip: Clearing a Form
sections.content:
You can reset all inputs in a form to their default values with:
```javascript
document.getElementById('signup-form').reset();
```
Or clear individual fields:
```javascript
document.getElementById('username').value = '';
```
The `.reset()` method is especially useful after successfully processing form data.

questions.prompt: What does `document.getElementById('email').value` return if the user typed `hello@test.com` in the email input?
questions.answer: "hello@test.com" (a string)
questions.explanation: The `.value` property returns the current text content of an input field as a **string**. Whatever the user has typed is returned as a string value.

Even though the input type is 'number', `.value` always returns a **string**. So `"5" + 2` triggers string concatenation, resulting in `"52"`. To get `7`, you'd need `Number(qty) + 2` or use `valueAsNumber`.

questions.prompt: How do you check if a checkbox is checked in JavaScript?
questions.answer: checkbox.checked
questions.explanation: For checkboxes, use the `.checked` property which returns `true` or `false`. The `.value` property on a checkbox typically returns `"on"` regardless of whether it's checked, so it's useless for determining state.

questions.prompt: Given a form with inputs named 'item' (text) and 'price' (number), write code that reads both values and logs: 'Item: [item], Price: $[price]'. Make sure price is treated as a number and displayed with 2 decimal places.
questions.starter_code:
const form = document.getElementById('order-form');
// Your code here
test_cases: Should use form.elements or getElementById to access inputs
Should convert price to a number
questions.hint: Use form.elements.item.value for text, Number(form.elements.price.value).toFixed(2) for the price

questions.prompt: What does the `form.reset()` method do?
choices: Deletes the form from the DOM
Submits the form to the server
Resets all form inputs to their default/initial values
questions.answer: Resets all form inputs to their default/initial values
questions.explanation: The `.reset()` method restores all form controls to their initial values (the values they had when the page loaded or as specified in the HTML `value` attributes). It's commonly used after successfully processing a form submission.

lessons.title: Form Events — Submit, Input, Change & More
objectives: Handle the submit event and prevent default page reload
Use the input event for real-time tracking
Understand the difference between input, change, focus, and blur events
Build a live character counter

sections.content:
# Form Events — The Pulse of User Interaction

In the last lesson, you learned how to *read* form values. But *when* do you read them? That's where **form events** come in.

Forms fire a rich set of events at different moments of user interaction. Mastering these events is what separates a clunky form from a smooth, responsive one.

Think about Google Search — as you type, suggestions appear *instantly*. That's the `input` event at work. When you submit a form on Amazon and it doesn't reload the page? That's `submit` with `preventDefault()`. These are the tools you're about to learn.

sections.definition: The `submit` event fires on a `<form>` element when the user submits the form (by clicking a submit button or pressing Enter inside an input). It fires on the FORM, not on the button. This is the single most important form event — it's where you intercept the form data before it goes anywhere.

sections.title: Handling Form Submission — The Right Way
sections.explanation:
This is the **#1 pattern** you'll use in every form you ever build:

1. **Listen for `submit`** on the form element (NOT the button)
2. **Call `event.preventDefault()`** — This stops the page from reloading
3. **Read the values** from `form.elements`
4. **Process the data** — validate, display, send to server, etc.

Without `preventDefault()`, the browser would reload the page, and all your JavaScript variables would be lost. This single line is what enables modern single-page web apps.

sections.title: Common Mistake: Listening on the Button

sections.content:
## The `input` Event — Real-Time Tracking

The `input` event fires **every single time** the value of an input changes. Every keystroke, every paste, every deletion — it fires. This is what powers:
- Live search suggestions
- Character counters
- Real-time validation
- Password strength meters
- Live previews

sections.title: The input Event in Action
sections.explanation: The `input` event is incredibly responsive — it fires for every character typed, deleted, or pasted. The `event.target.value` gives you the current complete value of the input at that moment. This is different from `event.key` (from keyboard events) which only tells you which key was pressed.

sections.title: Form Events Compared
rows: Form is submitted (button click or Enter)
Live search, character counters, real-time validation
Value changes AND element loses focus
Dropdown selections, checkbox toggles, after-edit validation
Element receives focus (clicked or tabbed into)
Element loses focus (clicked away or tabbed out)
Similar to keydown but fires after

sections.title: input vs change — Understanding the Difference
sections.explanation: The key difference: `input` fires *during* typing (every keystroke). `change` fires *after* the user finishes and moves away. Use `input` for live feedback, use `change` for when you want to validate or react after the user is done editing. Note: for `<select>` dropdowns and checkboxes, `change` is actually the preferred event because it fires immediately when the selection changes.

sections.title: Focus and Blur — Field Entry and Exit
sections.explanation: `focus` fires when a user clicks into (or tabs to) a field. `blur` fires when they click away (or tab out). This pair is perfect for showing contextual help when a field is active and validating when they leave. The keyword `this` inside the event handler refers to the element that fired the event.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: Restaurant Ordering
sections.content:
Think of form events like ordering at a restaurant:
- **focus** = The waiter walks over to your table (attention begins)
- **input** = You're speaking your order word by word (each word is tracked)
- **change** = You've finished saying your order (final value)
- **blur** = The waiter walks away from your table (attention ends)
- **submit** = You say "That's everything" and the waiter goes to the kitchen (form is sent)

sections.title: Practical Example: Live Character Counter
sections.explanation: This is a Twitter/X-style character counter. Every time the user types (`input` event), we calculate remaining characters and update the display. We also change the color to give visual feedback: green (plenty of room), orange (running low), red (over limit). This pattern is used on virtually every social media platform.

sections.title: Try It: Build a Live Preview
sections.expected_output: Preview updates in real-time as user types
sections.hint: Use this.value or event.target.value to get the current text. Use the || operator for fallback: preview.textContent = this.value || 'Your title here...'

sections.definition: A method on the event object that stops the browser's default behavior for that event. For forms, the default is to reload the page and send data to the server. Calling `event.preventDefault()` in the submit handler keeps the user on the page and lets your JavaScript handle everything. Without it, your carefully crafted JavaScript would be wiped out by a page reload.

questions.prompt: Which event should you listen for to handle form submission properly?
choices: The 'click' event on the submit button
questions.answer: The 'submit' event on the <form> element
questions.explanation: The `submit` event on the `<form>` element catches both button clicks AND Enter key presses. Listening on the button's click event would miss Enter key submissions. Always use `form.addEventListener('submit', handler)`.

questions.prompt: A user types 'Hi' into a text input that has both `input` and `change` event listeners. They then click somewhere else on the page. How many total events fire?
questions.answer: 3 events — two 'input' events (one for 'H', one for 'i') and one 'change' event (when focus leaves)
questions.explanation: The `input` event fires for each keystroke: once for 'H' and once for 'i' (2 times). The `change` event fires once when the input loses focus (blur) because the value changed. Total: 3 events.

questions.prompt: What happens if you forget `event.preventDefault()` in a submit handler?
choices: The page reloads and all JavaScript state is lost
The form data is automatically saved
questions.answer: The page reloads and all JavaScript state is lost
questions.explanation: Without `preventDefault()`, the browser performs its default action: it reloads the page (sending form data to the URL in the `action` attribute, or the current page if none is specified). All your JavaScript variables, DOM changes, and app state are wiped clean.

questions.prompt: Write a submit handler for a form with id 'login-form'. It should prevent the default behavior, read the values from inputs named 'user' and 'pass', and log 'Login: [user]' to the console. If either field is empty, log 'Please fill in all fields' instead.
questions.starter_code:
const loginForm = document.getElementById('login-form');

// Your code here
questions.hint: Use form.elements.user.value to read the username. Check if either value === '' or .trim() === '' for empty check.

questions.prompt: Which event would you use to build a real-time search feature that shows results as the user types?
choices: change — it fires when value changes
submit — it fires when form is submitted
input — it fires on every keystroke
blur — it fires when user leaves the field
questions.answer: input — it fires on every keystroke
questions.explanation: The `input` event fires on every single change to the input's value (each keystroke, paste, deletion). This makes it perfect for real-time search features, live previews, and character counters where you need instant feedback.

lessons.title: Input Validation — Guarding the Gates
objectives: Understand why client-side validation matters
Implement required field checks
Validate string length, number ranges, and format
Display user-friendly error messages
Build a complete validated form

sections.content:
# Input Validation — Guarding the Gates

**Validation** is the process of checking whether user input meets your requirements *before* processing it. This is one of the most important skills in web development.

Here's a scary truth: **users will enter anything**. Empty fields, numbers where names should be, SQL injection attacks, emojis in phone number fields, and 50-character-long zip codes. Your job is to catch bad data before it causes problems.

Every major website you've ever used has validation:
- Twitter: 280-character limit
- Gmail: Valid email format required
- Banking sites: Strong password requirements
- E-commerce: Valid credit card numbers
- Instagram: Username character restrictions

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: The Nightclub Bouncer
sections.content:
Validation is like a **bouncer at a nightclub**. Before anyone gets in (before data is processed), the bouncer checks:
- Are you on the list? (required fields)
- Are you old enough? (range validation)
- Do you have proper ID? (format validation)
- Are you dressed appropriately? (pattern matching)

If any check fails, you don't get in. The bouncer doesn't let you in and *then* figure out there's a problem — they check at the door. That's what validation does for your forms.

sections.definition: **Client-side validation** happens in the browser using JavaScript BEFORE data is sent to the server. It provides instant feedback and a better user experience. **Server-side validation** happens on the server AFTER data is received. BOTH are necessary. Client-side validation can be bypassed (a user can disable JavaScript or use dev tools), so server-side validation is the true security gate. Think of client-side as 'courtesy checking' and server-side as 'security checking'.

sections.title: Basic Validation Pattern
sections.explanation:
This is the fundamental validation pattern:
1. **Read & clean** values (`.trim()` removes whitespace)
2. **Collect errors** in an array (don't stop at the first error — show all of them)
3. **Check each field** against your rules
4. **If errors exist**, show them. **If no errors**, process the form.

Using an array to collect errors is a best practice — it lets you show ALL problems at once instead of making the user fix them one at a time.

sections.title: Why .trim() Is Essential
sections.content:
```javascript
const input = '   ';  // Just spaces
console.log(input === '');        // false — it's NOT empty!
console.log(input.trim() === ''); // true — NOW it's empty

const name = '  Alice  ';
console.log(name.trim());  // 'Alice' — clean!
```
Always use `.trim()` before validating text inputs. Without it, a field full of spaces would pass a `!== ''` check, which is clearly not what you want.

sections.title: Complete Validation Library — Reusable Functions
sections.explanation: This is production-quality thinking! Instead of jamming all validation into one giant function, we create small, reusable validation functions. Each function checks ONE thing and returns a boolean. Then `validateSignupForm` uses them to build a list of errors. Notice the `else if` chains — we only check format AFTER confirming the field isn't empty. No point checking if an email is valid if there's no email at all!

sections.title: Displaying Errors in the DOM
sections.explanation:
Real validation doesn't just `console.log` errors — it shows them to the user IN the page. This code demonstrates:
1. **Error summary** — A box at the top listing all errors
2. **Field-level errors** — Red borders and error messages next to each invalid field
3. **Clearing errors** — Always clear previous errors before re-validating

Notice we use `.innerHTML` with template literals to build the error list dynamically. The `.map().join('')` pattern converts the errors array into HTML list items.

sections.title: The Cost of Bad Validation
sections.content: In 2020, a study found that 67% of online shoppers abandon their cart due to frustrating form experiences. Poor validation — like showing errors one at a time, unclear messages, or losing data on reload — costs businesses billions in lost sales annually. Good validation = good UX = more conversions. The skills you're learning here literally make money.

sections.title: Try It: Validate a Password
sections.expected_output: Various arrays of error messages depending on the password
sections.hint: Loop through each character and check: if (char >= '0' && char <= '9') hasNumber = true; Do similar for uppercase: if (char >= 'A' && char <= 'Z') hasUpper = true;

sections.title: Validation Best Practices Checklist
steps: Always `.trim()` text input values before checking
Check required fields FIRST — skip format checks if empty
Collect ALL errors — don't stop at the first one
Show clear, specific error messages (not just 'Invalid input')
Highlight the specific field that has the error
Clear previous errors before re-validating
Validate on submit AND on blur for the best UX
Never trust client-side validation alone — always validate on the server too

questions.answer: [] (empty array)
questions.explanation: The string `'  '` (two spaces) is NOT equal to `''` (empty string). The `if` check fails because spaces are characters! This is why you must use `.trim()`: `if (username.trim() === '')` would correctly catch this.

questions.prompt: Why do we collect errors in an array instead of stopping at the first error?
choices: Arrays are faster than individual variables
So we can show ALL errors at once — better user experience
JavaScript requires errors to be in arrays
It's just a coding style preference with no real benefit
questions.answer: So we can show ALL errors at once — better user experience
questions.explanation: Showing one error at a time is extremely frustrating for users — they fix one thing, submit, see another error, fix it, submit, see another... By collecting all errors and showing them together, users can fix everything in one pass. This is a key UX principle.

questions.prompt: Write a function `validateEmail(email)` that returns an error message string if the email is invalid, or `null` if it's valid. Check that: (1) it's not empty, (2) it contains an @ symbol, (3) it contains a dot after the @.
test_cases: validateEmail('hello') should return an error about @ symbol
questions.hint: Use .trim() first, then .indexOf('@') to check for @, then .indexOf('.', atIndex) to check for dot after @

questions.prompt: Is client-side (JavaScript) validation alone enough to secure a web application?
choices: No — users can bypass JavaScript, so server-side validation is also required
Yes — modern browsers enforce validation automatically
No — you should only use server-side validation
questions.answer: No — users can bypass JavaScript, so server-side validation is also required
questions.explanation: Client-side validation improves user experience with instant feedback, but it can be bypassed (disable JavaScript, use developer tools, or send requests directly to the server). Server-side validation is the real security gate. Best practice: use BOTH. Client-side for UX, server-side for security.

lessons.title: Regular Expressions — Pattern Matching Power
objectives: Understand what regular expressions are and why they exist
Use basic regex patterns for validation
Apply regex to email, phone, and password validation
Use the test() and match() methods
Know common regex patterns used in real-world apps

sections.content:
# Regular Expressions — Pattern Matching Power

In the last lesson, you wrote validation logic with loops, `indexOf()`, and character-by-character checking. It worked, but checking if a string matches a complex pattern (like a valid email or phone number) gets messy fast.

Enter **Regular Expressions** (regex) — a powerful mini-language for describing text patterns. Regex lets you express in one line what would take 10-20 lines of manual checking.

Regex is used in virtually every programming language, every text editor's "Find & Replace," and every production form validation. It's intimidating at first — regex looks like someone smashed their keyboard — but we'll break it down step by step.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: Metal Detector Template
sections.content:
Imagine a **metal template with cutouts** at airport security. You slide luggage through, and if it matches the shape of the cutout, it passes. If it doesn't fit the pattern, it's flagged.

A regex is like that template — you define a **pattern** (the shape), and strings either match or they don't:
- Pattern: `XXX-XXX-XXXX` → `555-123-4567` ✅ matches → `55-12-456` ❌ doesn't match
- Pattern: `something@something.something` → `a@b.com` ✅ → `notanemail` ❌

sections.title: Your First Regular Expression
sections.explanation: A regex is written between forward slashes `/pattern/`. The `.test()` method returns `true` if the pattern is found anywhere in the string, `false` otherwise. By default, regex is case-sensitive. Adding the `i` flag after the closing slash makes it case-insensitive.

sections.content:
## Regex Building Blocks

Regex uses special characters (called **metacharacters**) to describe patterns. Let's learn them one by one:

sections.title: Essential Regex Characters
rows: Between n and m of preceding

sections.title: Building Patterns Step by Step
sections.explanation:
The key to building regex patterns is **breaking down the format**:
1. Look at what a valid input looks like
2. Describe each part using regex characters
3. Use `^` at the start and `$` at the end to ensure the ENTIRE string matches (not just a substring)

Without `^` and `$`, the regex `/\d{3}/` would match `'abc123def'` because it finds 3 digits somewhere in the string. With `^` and `$`: `/^\d{3}$/` only matches strings that are EXACTLY 3 digits.

sections.title: Why ^ and $ Matter
sections.content:
```javascript
