```javascript
const loose = /\d{5}/;       // finds 5 digits ANYWHERE
const strict = /^\d{5}$/;    // string must be EXACTLY 5 digits

console.log(loose.test('abc12345xyz'));  // true 😬
console.log(strict.test('abc12345xyz')); // false ✅
console.log(strict.test('12345'));        // true ✅
```
For validation, ALWAYS use `^` and `$` to anchor your pattern. Without them, `'I have 12345 cats'` would pass a zip code check!

sections.title: Email Validation with Regex
sections.explanation: This is a widely-used email validation pattern. It's not perfect (no regex can fully validate emails per the RFC spec), but it catches 99% of invalid inputs. The key pieces: `[a-zA-Z0-9._%+-]+` allows common username characters, `\.` matches a literal dot (since `.` in regex means 'any character', you must escape it with `\`), and `{2,}` means 'two or more'.

sections.title: Password Strength Validation with Regex
sections.explanation: Instead of one giant regex, we use **multiple small regex tests** — each checking for one requirement. This approach is more readable and lets us give specific error messages for each failed requirement. Notice the `!` before each regex test: `!/[a-z]/.test(password)` means 'if there are NO lowercase letters'. We also return an object with `isValid`, `errors`, and `strength` — this is clean, structured data that the UI can easily use.

sections.definition:
**`regex.test(string)`** — Returns `true` or `false`. Use this for validation when you just need to know if a pattern exists.

**`string.match(regex)`** — Returns an array of matches or `null`. Use this when you need to extract data from a string.

```javascript
/\d+/.test('abc123');      // true (just checking)
'abc123'.match(/\d+/);     // ['123'] (extracting)
'abc123def456'.match(/\d+/g); // ['123', '456'] (all matches with g flag)
```

sections.title: Try It: Build a Username Validator
sections.expected_output:
true
true
false
false
false
false
sections.hint: The regex should be: /^[a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9_]{2,14}$/

sections.title: Don't Go Overboard with Regex
sections.content:
There's a famous quote: *'Some people, when confronted with a problem, think "I know, I'll use regular expressions." Now they have two problems.'* — Jamie Zawinski

Regex is powerful but can be hard to read and maintain. For complex validation:
- Use **multiple simple regex tests** instead of one monster pattern
- Add **comments** explaining what each pattern matches
- For truly complex parsing (like HTML), use a proper parser, not regex
- Simple checks like `string.includes('@')` or `string.length > 8` are perfectly fine when they do the job

questions.prompt: What does `/^\d{3}-\d{4}$/.test('555-1234')` return?
questions.answer: true
questions.explanation: `^\d{3}` matches exactly 3 digits at the start (555), `-` matches a literal hyphen, `\d{4}$` matches exactly 4 digits at the end (1234). The string '555-1234' fits this pattern exactly, so `.test()` returns `true`.

questions.prompt: What does the regex `/[aeiou]/i` match?
questions.answer: Both lowercase and uppercase vowels
questions.explanation: `[aeiou]` matches any single character that is a, e, i, o, or u. The `i` flag makes it case-insensitive, so it also matches A, E, I, O, U.

questions.prompt:
What will this return?
```javascript
/^\d+$/.test('12a34')
```
questions.answer: false
questions.explanation: `^\d+$` means 'start to end, only digits, one or more'. The string '12a34' contains the letter 'a', which is not a digit. So the entire string doesn't match the pattern, and `.test()` returns `false`.

questions.prompt: Write a regex pattern stored in a variable called `dateRegex` that matches dates in the format MM/DD/YYYY (e.g., 01/25/2024). Use \d and {n} for digit counts. Test it with .test() against '12/25/2023' (should be true) and '1/5/2023' (should be false — needs leading zeros).
questions.starter_code:
const dateRegex = // your regex here

console.log(dateRegex.test('12/25/2023')); // true
console.log(dateRegex.test('01/01/2000')); // true
console.log(dateRegex.test('1/5/2023'));   // false
console.log(dateRegex.test('13-25-2023')); // false (wrong separator)
test_cases: Should use \d{2} for month and day
Should use \/ for literal forward slashes
questions.hint: The pattern should be /^\d{2}\/\d{2}\/\d{4}$/ — use \/ to match a literal forward slash

questions.prompt: When should you use `string.match(regex)` instead of `regex.test(string)`?
choices: When you need to extract the matched text from the string
questions.answer: When you need to extract the matched text from the string
questions.explanation: `.test()` returns just `true/false` — perfect for validation. `.match()` returns the actual matched text (or `null` if no match) — use it when you need to extract data from a string, like pulling phone numbers from text or parsing dates.

lessons.title: Real-Time Validation & User Feedback
objectives: Implement validation that fires as the user types (real-time)
Implement validation on blur (when they leave a field)
Build a password strength meter
Apply proper CSS classes for valid/invalid states
Create an accessible, user-friendly validation experience

sections.content:
# Real-Time Validation & User Feedback

So far, our validation fires when the user clicks Submit. That works, but modern apps do better — they validate **in real-time**, giving feedback as the user types or immediately after they leave a field.

Think about how Gmail handles password creation:
- As you type, it shows "Too short" in red
- Then it turns yellow with "Fair"
- Then green with "Strong"
- All BEFORE you click any submit button

This immediate feedback is what separates amateur forms from professional ones. Users love it because they can fix mistakes in real-time instead of hunting for errors after submitting.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: GPS Navigation
sections.content:
Submit-only validation is like getting directions **after** you've completed a road trip — 'Hey, you made a wrong turn 200 miles ago.' Not helpful!

Real-time validation is like **GPS navigation** — it tells you IMMEDIATELY when you're going the wrong way: 'Recalculating... make a U-turn.' Much better! That's the experience we're building.

## When to Validate? The Three Strategies

1. **On Submit** — Validate everything when the form is submitted. Simple but delays feedback.
2. **On Blur** — Validate a field when the user leaves it. Good balance of timing.
3. **On Input** — Validate as the user types. Instant feedback but can be aggressive.

**The best approach combines all three:**
- Don't show errors while the user is actively typing in an untouched field (annoying)
- Show errors when they **leave** a field (blur) — they're done with it
- Once a field has been shown as invalid, update validation **as they type** (input) to show when it's fixed
- Do a **final check on submit** to catch anything missed

sections.title: The Smart Validation Pattern
sections.explanation:
This is the **gold standard** pattern for form validation UX:

1. We use a `Set` called `touchedFields` to track which fields the user has interacted with
2. On `blur`, we mark the field as touched and validate it
3. On `input`, we only re-validate if the field was already touched
4. Each validation function returns an error string (if invalid) or `null` (if valid)
5. `showFieldState` handles the CSS classes and error message display

This prevents the annoying pattern of showing 'Required!' errors the moment the page loads or as soon as the user starts typing their first character.

sections.title: CSS for Validation States
sections.explanation: Using CSS classes (`.valid` and `.invalid`) instead of inline styles is the professional approach. The `transition` property gives smooth color changes, the `box-shadow` creates a glow effect on focus, and the error messages are hidden by default and shown via JavaScript. This CSS gives you a polished, production-quality form appearance.

sections.title: Building a Password Strength Meter
sections.explanation:
This password strength meter uses an **array of rule objects** — each with a `test` function and a `label`. On every keystroke, it:
1. Checks which rules pass
2. Updates the visual checklist (✅ or ⬜ for each rule)
3. Calculates the strength percentage
4. Updates the colored progress bar
5. Shows the strength label (Weak/Fair/Good/Strong)

The rules array makes this easily extensible — just add another object to add a new requirement. This is the exact pattern used by GitHub, Google, and most password creation forms.

sections.description: A mockup of a form field with four states shown side by side: (1) Default/untouched - gray border, no message. (2) Focused - blue border with subtle blue glow. (3) Invalid (after blur) - red border, red error message below reading 'Username must be at least 3 characters', small red X icon. (4) Valid - green border, small green checkmark icon. Below the four states, show a password field with a colored progress bar underneath: red segment labeled 'Weak', yellow segment labeled 'Fair', green segment labeled 'Strong'.
sections.alt_text: Form validation visual states: default, focused, invalid with error, and valid with checkmark

sections.title: Putting It All Together: Complete Validated Form
sections.explanation:
This is a **production-quality form validation system** in about 60 lines. Key architectural decisions:

1. **Validators object** — All validation rules in one place, easy to modify
2. **Dynamic setup** — `Object.keys(validators).forEach(...)` sets up listeners for all fields automatically
3. **classList.toggle** — Cleanly adds/removes CSS classes based on a condition
4. **Three-layer validation**: blur (first touch), input (real-time updates), submit (final gate)
5. **On submit**, we mark ALL fields as touched and validate everything — catching any fields the user skipped

This exact pattern scales to forms with 50+ fields.

sections.title: Accessibility Matters

questions.prompt: Why shouldn't you show validation errors while the user is still typing in a field for the first time?
choices: It's annoying — they haven't finished typing yet
Errors can only be shown on submit
questions.answer: It's annoying — they haven't finished typing yet
questions.explanation: If a user clicks into the username field and types 'A', immediately showing 'Too short!' is aggressive and frustrating — they weren't done! The best UX is to wait until they LEAVE the field (blur) before showing the first error. After that, update in real-time (input) so they can see when they've fixed it.

questions.prompt: What does `element.classList.toggle('invalid', true)` do?
questions.answer: It adds the 'invalid' class to the element (because the second argument is true)
questions.explanation: `classList.toggle(className, force)` with a second boolean argument: if `true`, it adds the class; if `false`, it removes it. So `toggle('invalid', true)` is equivalent to `classList.add('invalid')`, and `toggle('invalid', false)` is equivalent to `classList.remove('invalid')`. This is a handy shortcut!

questions.prompt: In the smart validation pattern, why do we validate ALL fields on submit even though we've been validating on blur?
choices: Because blur validation might have bugs
To catch fields the user never clicked into (never blurred)
Because submit events reset all validation
It's redundant — we don't actually need to
questions.answer: To catch fields the user never clicked into (never blurred)
questions.explanation: A user might tab past a field or simply never click into it. Those fields were never 'touched' (never blurred), so their validation never ran. On submit, we mark ALL fields as touched and validate them all — catching any empty required fields that were completely skipped.

questions.prompt: Write a validation function for a 'confirm password' field. It takes two parameters: `password` and `confirmPassword`. Return an error string if confirmPassword is empty OR doesn't match password. Return null if valid.
test_cases: Mismatched passwords return 'Passwords do not match'
questions.hint: Check empty first with trim(), then compare the two values with strict equality (===)

lessons.title: Building Dynamic Lists — The Todo List Pattern
objectives: Dynamically add items to a list from form input
Generate unique IDs for list items
Render data from an array into the DOM
Understand the data-first approach (array → DOM)
Build a working 'Add Item' feature from scratch

sections.content:
# Building Dynamic Lists — The Todo List Pattern

You've mastered forms and validation. Now it's time to combine everything into one of the most fundamental patterns in web development: **dynamically adding items to a list**.

This pattern appears everywhere:
- **Todo apps** — Add, check off, and delete tasks
- **Shopping carts** — Add products, change quantities
- **Chat messages** — New messages appear at the bottom
- **Social media feeds** — Posts are added dynamically
- **Comment sections** — Users add comments in real-time
- **Email inboxes** — Messages appear as they arrive

Mastering this pattern unlocks all of these features. This is where everything you've learned comes together: forms (input), validation (quality control), events (triggers), DOM manipulation (display), arrays (data storage), and objects (data structure).

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: Restaurant Order Board
sections.content:
Imagine a restaurant kitchen with an **order board**:
1. A waiter takes an order (form input)
2. They check it's valid — no imaginary dishes (validation)
3. They write it on a ticket and stick it on the board (add to array + render to DOM)
4. The cooks can see all current orders (the list display)
5. When an order is done, the ticket is removed (delete from list)

Our code works the same way: data goes into an array (the ticket system), and the DOM (the order board) always reflects what's in the array.

sections.definition: In the 'Data-First' approach, your JavaScript **array is the single source of truth**. The DOM is just a visual representation of that data. When something changes, you update the array FIRST, then re-render the DOM from the array. Never modify the DOM directly without updating the underlying data. This makes your app predictable, debuggable, and scalable. This is the same principle behind React, Vue, and Angular.

sections.content:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              DATA-FIRST FLOW                        │
│                                                     │
│   ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────────┐  │
│   │  User    │    │  Array   │    │    DOM       │  │
│   │  Action  │───▶│  (Data)  │───▶│  (Display)   │  │
│   └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────────┘  │
│                                                     │
│   Examples:                                         │
│   Add item  ──▶  items.push()  ──▶  renderList()   │
│   Delete    ──▶  items.splice() ──▶  renderList()  │
│   Toggle    ──▶  item.done = !  ──▶  renderList()  │
│   Edit      ──▶  item.text = x  ──▶  renderList()  │
│                                                     │
│   ❌ WRONG: Modify DOM directly without updating    │
│      the array — data and display get out of sync   │
│   ✅ RIGHT: Update array first, then render from it │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

sections.title: The HTML Structure
sections.code: 📝 My Todo List
sections.explanation:
Clean, semantic HTML with an empty `
    ` that JavaScript will populate. Notice the `

    ` for validation errors and `

    ` for showing counts. The form uses the submit event pattern we learned. Styles are inline for simplicity but would normally be in a separate CSS file.

sections.title: The Complete Todo List — Step by Step
sections.explanation:
Let's break down the architecture:

**1. Data Layer** — `todos` array holds all the data. `nextId` generates unique IDs.

**2. Add Flow** — Submit → Validate → Create object → Push to array → Re-render DOM → Clear input.

**3. Render Function** — The heart of the system. It clears the entire list, then loops through the `todos` array and creates DOM elements for each item. This 'destroy and rebuild' approach is simple and reliable.

**4. `escapeHTML()`** — CRITICAL security function! If a user types `<script>alert('hacked')</script>` as a task, we must NOT insert that raw HTML. `escapeHTML` converts dangerous characters to their safe equivalents.

**5. `dataset.id`** — We store each todo's ID on its DOM element using `data-id`. This lets us find which todo to modify when the user clicks a button.

sections.title: Security Alert: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
Alternative: Building DOM Without innerHTML
sections.explanation: This approach uses `createElement` and `textContent` instead of `innerHTML`. It's inherently XSS-safe because `textContent` never parses HTML. The tradeoff: it's more verbose but more secure. Both approaches are valid — use `innerHTML` with `escapeHTML()` for simpler code, or `createElement` with `textContent` for maximum security.

sections.title: Try It: Add Items to a Shopping List
sections.expected_output:
Shopping List:
⬜ Milk (x2)
⬜ Bread (x1)
⬜ Eggs (x12)
sections.hint: Create an object like: { id: idCounter++, name: name, quantity: quantity, purchased: false } and push it to items

sections.title: The 'Todo List' as a Rite of Passage
sections.content: Building a todo list is considered a **rite of passage** for every web developer. It's so popular that there's a website called **TodoMVC** (todomvc.com) that showcases the same todo app built in dozens of frameworks — React, Vue, Angular, Svelte, and more. The skills you're learning here are the exact same foundations used in all of them. Once you build a vanilla JS todo list, learning any framework becomes dramatically easier.

questions.prompt: In the data-first approach, what is the 'single source of truth'?
choices: The HTML elements in the DOM
The JavaScript array holding the data
questions.answer: The JavaScript array holding the data
questions.explanation: In a data-first architecture, the JavaScript array (e.g., `todos`) is the single source of truth. All changes happen to the array first, and then the DOM is re-rendered to reflect the array's current state. The DOM is a 'mirror' of the data, never the other way around.

questions.answer: todos.length is 3, nextId is 4
questions.explanation: Three objects are pushed into the array, so its length is 3. `nextId++` uses post-increment — it uses the current value (1, 2, 3) and then increments. After three calls, `nextId` is 4 (ready for the next item).

questions.prompt: Why is `escapeHTML()` important when displaying user-generated content?
choices: It makes the text look prettier
It prevents Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks by neutralizing HTML tags
It's required by the browser or the code won't work
questions.answer: It prevents Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks by neutralizing HTML tags
questions.explanation: If a user enters `<script>stealCookies()</script>` as a todo item and you insert it with `innerHTML` unescaped, the browser would execute that script! `escapeHTML()` converts `<` to `<` and `>` to `>`, so the browser displays the text instead of executing it.

questions.prompt: Write a `renderList` function that takes an array of `{name, price}` objects and renders them as list items inside a `<ul id='products'>`. Each item should show: 'name — $price'. Clear the list before rendering.
test_cases: Should clear the list first with innerHTML = ''
Should display name and formatted price
Should use textContent or escapeHTML for security
questions.hint: Clear with ul.innerHTML = '', then loop with forEach, create li with createElement, set textContent, and appendChild to ul.

lessons.title: Editing, Deleting & Toggling List Items
objectives: Delete items from a dynamic list using event delegation
Toggle item completion status
Edit items inline
Use data attributes to identify which item was acted on
Handle events on dynamically created elements

sections.content:
# Editing, Deleting & Toggling List Items

Adding items to a list is just the beginning. A real app needs to let users **delete** items they don't need, **toggle** items as complete/incomplete, and **edit** items they made mistakes on.

This lesson covers a crucial technique: **event delegation** — how to handle clicks on elements that didn't exist when the page loaded. This is one of the most important DOM patterns to master.

sections.definition: Event delegation is a pattern where you attach ONE event listener to a PARENT element instead of individual listeners to each child. When a child is clicked, the event 'bubbles up' to the parent, where your listener catches it. You use `event.target` to figure out which child was actually clicked. This is essential for dynamically created elements — you can't attach a listener to an element that doesn't exist yet, but you CAN attach one to its parent.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: Office Reception Desk
sections.content:
Imagine a company with 100 employees. You could give each employee their own mailbox at the front door (100 event listeners), OR you could have ONE reception desk (parent listener) that receives all mail and routes it to the right person (event.target).

The reception desk approach (event delegation) is:
- **More efficient** — one listener instead of 100
- **Handles new employees** — new hires don't need a new mailbox, the reception handles them automatically
- **Easier to manage** — one point of contact

sections.title: Why Direct Listeners Don't Work on Dynamic Elements
sections.explanation:
This is a common 'gotcha' for beginners. When the page loads, `querySelectorAll('.delete-btn')` finds zero elements because no list items exist yet. Later, when JavaScript creates list items with delete buttons, those new buttons have NO event listeners.

**Event delegation** solves this elegantly: we listen on the `<ul>` element (which exists from page load), and when any click happens inside it, the event bubbles up to the `<ul>`. We check `event.target` to see if the clicked element is a delete button.

sections.title: Deleting Items — Array + DOM
sections.explanation:
The key pattern here:

1. **`event.target.closest('.todo-item')`** — Starting from whatever was clicked, find the nearest ancestor with class `todo-item`. This is the `<li>` containing the clicked button.

2. **`li.dataset.id`** — We stored the todo's ID on the `<li>` as a `data-id` attribute during rendering. Now we retrieve it to find the corresponding item in our array.

3. **Delete** — Use `.filter()` to create a new array without the deleted item. This is the functional/immutable approach.

4. **Toggle** — Use `.find()` to locate the item, then flip `todo.done`. Since objects are by reference, this modifies the original in the array.

5. **Re-render** — After each change, call `renderList()` to update the display.

sections.title: Understanding .closest()
Inline Editing — Making Items Editable
sections.explanation:
Inline editing is a three-phase process:

**1. Start** (double-click) — Replace the `<span>` with an `<input>`, pre-filled with the current text. Focus the input and select all text for easy replacement.

**2. Edit** — User modifies the text freely.

**3. Finish** (Enter key or clicking away) — Read the new value, update the data array, and re-render. If the user pressed Escape, cancel by re-rendering without changes. If they emptied the field, delete the todo.

**Key insight**: `span.replaceWith(editInput)` is a clean way to swap elements in the DOM.

sections.title: Complete Event Handler with All Actions
sections.explanation:
This shows the full event delegation pattern with all CRUD operations (Create, Read, Update, Delete) handled through just TWO event listeners on the parent element.

Notice `event.target.matches('.delete-btn')` — this is similar to `classList.contains()` but uses a CSS selector string, making it more flexible. And `confirm('Delete this task?')` shows a browser dialog asking the user to confirm before deleting — a good UX practice for destructive actions.

The filter functions demonstrate how easy it is to show subsets of data when you use the data-first approach — just `.filter()` the array and render the result!

sections.description: A diagram showing event bubbling in a todo list. At the bottom is a DELETE button (event.target). An arrow labeled 'Event bubbles up' goes from the button → to the <li> parent → to the <ul> parent (which has the event listener). At the <ul> level, a speech bubble says: 'event.target.closest(.todo-item)' finds the <li>, '.dataset.id' gets the ID, then we search the array and update data. Three action paths branch out: Delete (filter), Toggle (find + flip), Edit (find + update).
sections.alt_text: Event delegation diagram showing how clicks bubble from child elements to the parent ul where they are handled

sections.title: Try It: Delete from an Array by ID
sections.expected_output:
Before: 4
After delete: 3
Dune removed? true
Hobbit read? true
sections.hint: deleteBook: books = books.filter(book => book.id !== id); toggleRead: const book = books.find(b => b.id === id); if (book) book.read = !book.read;

questions.prompt: Why is event delegation better than adding individual event listeners to dynamically created elements?
choices: It makes the code run faster because events don't bubble
It works for elements that don't exist yet when the listener is attached
It prevents all click events from firing
questions.answer: It works for elements that don't exist yet when the listener is attached
questions.explanation: Event delegation attaches ONE listener to a parent element that already exists. When new child elements are created later, clicks on them still bubble up to the parent where they're handled. You can't attach a listener to an element that doesn't exist yet, but you CAN listen on its parent.

`.filter(t => t.id !== 2)` creates a new array containing only items whose `id` is NOT 2. So the item with text 'B' (id: 2) is excluded. The result is [{id: 1, text: 'A'}, {id: 3, text: 'C'}], and mapping their text properties gives ['A', 'C'].

questions.prompt: What does `event.target.closest('.todo-item')` do?
choices: Finds the nearest child element with class 'todo-item'
Finds the nearest ancestor element (including self) with class 'todo-item'
Finds all elements with class 'todo-item' on the page
Creates a new element with class 'todo-item'
questions.answer: Finds the nearest ancestor element (including self) with class 'todo-item'
questions.explanation: `.closest()` starts from the element and traverses UP the DOM tree, returning the first ancestor (including itself) that matches the CSS selector. If no match is found, it returns `null`. It's the opposite of `.querySelector()` which searches DOWN the tree.

questions.prompt: Write an event delegation handler on an element with id 'task-list'. When a button with class 'complete-btn' is clicked inside a list item, it should find the closest <li>, get the task ID from its `data-id` attribute, and log 'Completing task: [id]'.
questions.starter_code:
const taskList = document.getElementById('task-list');

// Your code here
test_cases: Should use event.target.closest('li') to find the parent li
Should read li.dataset.id for the task ID
questions.hint: Listen for 'click' on taskList. Inside: check if event.target has class 'complete-btn'. If so, find closest('li'), get dataset.id.

lessons.title: Local Storage — Persisting Data Across Sessions
objectives: Understand what localStorage is and why it's useful
Save and retrieve data from localStorage
Use JSON.stringify and JSON.parse for complex data
Persist a todo list so it survives page reloads
Know the limitations and gotchas of localStorage

sections.content:
# Local Storage — Data That Survives a Refresh

Right now, our todo list has a fatal flaw: **reload the page, and everything disappears**. Every JavaScript variable is wiped clean when the page refreshes. For users, this is unacceptable — they need their data to persist.

Enter **localStorage** — a simple browser-based storage system that lets you save data on the user's computer. Data in localStorage survives page reloads, browser restarts, and even computer restarts. It's like a tiny, persistent database built into every browser.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: Sticky Notes on Your Fridge
sections.content:
JavaScript variables are like writing on a whiteboard — one swipe (page reload) and everything's gone.

localStorage is like **sticky notes on your fridge** — they stay there until YOU decide to remove them, no matter how many times you walk in and out of the kitchen (reload the page). The notes are specific to YOUR fridge (your browser, your domain) — guests at another house can't see them.

sections.title: localStorage Basics — The Four Essential Methods
sections.explanation:
localStorage is incredibly simple — just 4 methods:
- **`setItem(key, value)`** — Save a value under a key name
- **`getItem(key)`** — Retrieve a value by key (returns `null` if not found)
- **`removeItem(key)`** — Delete one specific item
- **`clear()`** — Delete ALL stored data

Critical rule: localStorage can ONLY store **strings**. If you save a number, it becomes a string. If you save an object, it becomes `[object Object]`. That's why we need JSON.

sections.title: localStorage Only Stores Strings!
JSON.stringify and JSON.parse — Saving Complex Data
sections.explanation:
**JSON.stringify(data)** — Converts JavaScript objects/arrays into a JSON string that localStorage can store.

**JSON.parse(string)** — Converts a JSON string back into a JavaScript object/array.

This stringify→store→retrieve→parse cycle is the standard way to persist complex data in the browser.

The `loadFromStorage` helper function is a production-quality pattern: it handles `null` (key doesn't exist) and catches parsing errors (corrupted data) gracefully, returning a default value in both cases.

sections.definition: JSON is a text format for representing structured data. It looks almost identical to JavaScript objects/arrays but with stricter rules: all keys must be in double quotes, no trailing commas, no functions, no `undefined`. It's the universal data exchange format of the web — used by APIs, databases, config files, and localStorage. `JSON.stringify()` converts JS → JSON text. `JSON.parse()` converts JSON text → JS.

sections.title: Adding Persistence to Our Todo App
sections.explanation:
Adding persistence requires just THREE changes to our existing code:

1. **On page load** — Call `loadTodos()` to load saved data from localStorage (instead of starting with an empty array)
2. **After every change** — Call `saveTodos()` after add, delete, toggle, and edit operations
3. **nextId calculation** — Use `Math.max(...)` to find the highest existing ID and start from there, avoiding ID collisions

Now when users refresh the page, their todos are restored! The data survives until they clear their browser data or we call `localStorage.clear()`.

sections.title: localStorage vs Other Storage Options
rows: User preferences, cached data, app state

sections.title: localStorage Limitations
sections.content:
1. **Strings only** — Must use JSON.stringify/parse for complex data
2. **~5-10 MB limit** — Not for large datasets (images, videos)
3. **Synchronous** — Large reads/writes can freeze the UI momentarily
4. **No expiration** — Data stays forever until manually deleted
5. **Same origin** — Only accessible from the same domain (good for security)
6. **Not secure** — Never store passwords, tokens, or sensitive data
7. **Can be cleared** — Users can delete it from browser settings

localStorage is perfect for: user preferences (dark mode), form drafts, small app data (todo lists), and caching non-sensitive data.

sections.title: Try It: Save and Load High Scores
sections.expected_output:
🏆 HIGH SCORES 🏆
1. Bob — 2200 pts
2. Charlie — 1800 pts
3. Alice — 1500 pts
sections.hint: The code is complete — run it and see! Try adding more scores and notice only top 5 are kept.

questions.prompt: What does `localStorage.getItem('nonexistent')` return?
questions.answer: null
questions.explanation: `getItem()` returns `null` when the key doesn't exist in localStorage. This is different from `undefined` — it specifically returns `null`. Always check for `null` before trying to parse the result with `JSON.parse()`.

questions.prompt: What happens when you store a JavaScript object directly in localStorage without JSON.stringify?
choices: It stores the string '[object Object]' — data is lost!
The object is automatically converted to JSON
questions.answer: It stores the string '[object Object]' — data is lost!
questions.explanation: localStorage calls `.toString()` on the value. For objects, `{}.toString()` returns `'[object Object]'` — a useless string. Your actual data (properties, values) is completely lost. Always use `JSON.stringify()` before saving objects/arrays.

questions.answer: "510" and "string"
questions.explanation: localStorage stores everything as strings. So `5` becomes `'5'`. When retrieved, `count` is the string `'5'`. `'5' + 10` triggers string concatenation, giving `'510'`. And `typeof count` is `'string'`. To get `15`, you'd need `Number(count) + 10`.

questions.prompt: Write a function `saveSettings(settings)` that saves a settings object to localStorage under the key 'app-settings', and a function `loadSettings()` that loads them back (returning a default object `{theme: 'light', fontSize: 16}` if nothing is stored).
test_cases: loadSettings should return defaults if nothing in storage
questions.hint: saveSettings: localStorage.setItem('app-settings', JSON.stringify(settings)); loadSettings: const stored = localStorage.getItem('app-settings'); return stored ? JSON.parse(stored) : {theme: 'light', fontSize: 16};

lessons.title: Mini Project: Task Manager App
objectives: Combine forms, validation, dynamic lists, and localStorage into one app
Build a complete CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) application
Implement filtering and search functionality
Practice the data-first architecture pattern
Gain confidence building real interactive web apps

sections.content:
# 🚀 Mini Project: Task Manager App

This is the capstone project for Module 12! You'll combine **everything** you've learned — forms, validation, dynamic lists, editing, deleting, and localStorage — into one complete application.

By the end, you'll have a fully functional Task Manager that:
- ✅ Lets users add tasks with titles, priorities, and due dates
- ✅ Validates all input before adding
- ✅ Shows tasks in a dynamic, filterable list
- ✅ Supports editing, completing, and deleting tasks
- ✅ Persists data in localStorage across sessions
- ✅ Filters tasks by status (all, active, completed)
- ✅ Searches tasks by keyword

This is a **real-world CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) application** — the same pattern that powers apps like Todoist, Trello, and Jira.

sections.title: Architecture: Data-First Pattern
sections.content:
We'll use the **data-first architecture** that professional developers use:

1. **Data** — An array of task objects is the single source of truth
2. **Functions** — Pure functions that modify the data array (add, delete, toggle, edit)
3. **Render** — One function renders the entire UI from the data
4. **Events** — Event handlers call data functions, then re-render

This pattern keeps your code organized and debuggable. The UI is always a reflection of the data, never the other way around.

sections.title: The HTML Structure
sections.code: 📋 Task Manager
sections.explanation: Clean, semantic HTML with form for input, controls for filtering, and a list for dynamic rendering.

sections.title: The Complete Task Manager
sections.explanation: Complete CRUD Task Manager using data-first architecture. The data layer manages the tasks array and localStorage persistence. The render layer builds the entire UI from the data array. Event handlers use delegation for dynamically created elements, and input validation prevents bad data.

sections.title: Key Patterns Used
sections.content:
✅ **Form handling** — submit event, preventDefault()
✅ **Input validation** — validateTask() checks before adding
✅ **Dynamic list rendering** — renderTasks() builds from array
✅ **Event delegation** — single listener handles all task actions
✅ **localStorage** — data persists across sessions
✅ **XSS protection** — escapeHtml() prevents injection
✅ **Data-first architecture** — array is source of truth
✅ **Filtering & search** — getFilteredTasks() returns subset

steps.title: Start with Data
steps.description: Create the tasks array and addTask/deleteTask functions. Test in console.

steps.title: Build the Render Function
steps.description: Write renderTasks() that creates HTML from the array.

steps.title: Wire Up the Form
steps.description: Add submit listener. Call addTask(), then renderTasks().

steps.title: Add Validation
steps.description: Create validateTask() and show errors before adding.

steps.title: Add Delete & Toggle
steps.description: Use event delegation for delete and checkbox handlers.

steps.title: Add localStorage
steps.description: Save after every change, load on page start.

steps.title: Add Filtering & Search
steps.description: Implement getFilteredTasks() and wire up controls.

questions.prompt: In data-first architecture, what is the 'single source of truth'?
choices: The HTML currently displayed in the browser
The JavaScript array containing all the data
The localStorage copy of the data
questions.answer: The JavaScript array containing all the data
questions.explanation: The JavaScript array is the single source of truth. The DOM is rebuilt from this data after every change. localStorage is a persistence layer, not the source of truth.

questions.prompt: Why do we use event delegation on the task list?
choices: Dynamically created elements won't have listeners — delegation handles current AND future elements
JavaScript doesn't allow multiple event listeners
It uses less memory but is slower
questions.answer: Dynamically created elements won't have listeners — delegation handles current AND future elements
questions.explanation: When we re-render with innerHTML, all old elements are destroyed. Event delegation solves this by listening on a parent that never gets destroyed.

questions.prompt: Why is escapeHtml() important?
choices: It prevents XSS attacks where malicious scripts could be injected
It compresses text to save memory
questions.answer: It prevents XSS attacks where malicious scripts could be injected
questions.explanation: Without escaping, a user could type <script>alert('hacked')</script> and it would execute when rendered with innerHTML.

questions.prompt: Write a function getTaskStats(tasks) that returns an object with total, active, and completed counts.
questions.hint: Use .filter() to count completed tasks. Active = total - completed.

modules.title: Module 13: Browser Storage, Error Handling & Putting It All Together

lessons.title: Understanding Errors in JavaScript
objectives: Understand why errors happen and that they are normal
Read and interpret JavaScript error messages
Identify the 7 built-in error types in JavaScript
Use error messages to locate and fix bugs

sections.content:
# Understanding Errors in JavaScript

If you've been coding along with this course, you've **already** encountered errors. Maybe you misspelled a variable name, forgot a closing parenthesis, or tried to call a method on `undefined`. And if you're like most beginners, seeing a big red error message in the console probably made your stomach drop.

Here's the truth: **errors are not failures — they are feedback.**

Every professional developer encounters errors every single day. The difference between a beginner and a pro isn't that the pro makes fewer mistakes — it's that the pro **reads the error message, understands it, and fixes the problem quickly.**

In this lesson, you're going to learn to love error messages. They are your GPS when you're lost in your own code.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: The Check Engine Light
sections.content: Think of JavaScript errors like your car's dashboard warning lights. When the "check engine" light comes on, your car isn't broken beyond repair — it's **telling you something specific** needs attention. You wouldn't ignore the light and keep driving (well, you shouldn't). Similarly, error messages tell you exactly what went wrong and where. They're your code's way of saying, "Hey, I tried to do what you asked, but something doesn't make sense."

## Anatomy of an Error Message

Every JavaScript error message has **three key parts** that tell you everything you need to know:

1. **Error Type** — What *kind* of error occurred
2. **Error Message** — A human-readable description of what went wrong
3. **Stack Trace** — The exact file, line number, and chain of function calls that led to the error

Let's break down a real error:

sections.title: Reading an Error Message
sections.explanation:
Let's decode this error:

**ReferenceError** → This is the ERROR TYPE. It tells you JavaScript tried to access a variable or function that doesn't exist.

**usrName is not defined** → This is the ERROR MESSAGE. It tells you specifically that `usrName` (your misspelling) was never declared.

**at script.js:3:13** → This is the STACK TRACE. It tells you the error is in file `script.js`, on line 3, at character 13.

With this information, you can go directly to line 3, see the typo (`usrName` instead of `userName`), and fix it instantly.

sections.description: A diagram showing a JavaScript error message broken into three labeled parts. The top shows 'ReferenceError' with an arrow labeled 'Error Type (WHAT kind of error)'. The middle shows 'usrName is not defined' with an arrow labeled 'Error Message (WHAT went wrong)'. The bottom shows 'at script.js:3:13' with an arrow labeled 'Stack Trace (WHERE it happened)'. Each part is color-coded differently — red for type, orange for message, blue for location.
sections.alt_text: Anatomy of a JavaScript error message with three labeled parts

sections.content:
## The 7 Built-In Error Types

JavaScript has **seven** built-in error types. You don't need to memorize them all right now, but knowing the most common ones will save you hours of debugging time. Think of them as **categories** — each one narrows down what went wrong.

sections.title: JavaScript Error Types
rows: Using a value the wrong way
Calling non-function, accessing prop of undefined

sections.content:
The **Big Three** you'll encounter 95% of the time are:

1. **SyntaxError** — Your code has a grammar mistake and JavaScript can't even read it
2. **ReferenceError** — You referenced a variable or function that doesn't exist
3. **TypeError** — You used a value in a way that doesn't make sense for its type

sections.title: The Big Three Error Types in Action
sections.explanation:
Notice the pattern: each error type gives you a specific clue.

- SyntaxError = Look for typos in the code structure itself (missing quotes, brackets, etc.)
- ReferenceError = Look for misspelled names or variables used before declaration
- TypeError = Look for wrong data types or null/undefined values being used as objects

sections.title: Pro Debugging Workflow
sections.content:
When you see an error, follow this 3-step process:

1. **Read the error type** — This tells you the *category* of the problem
2. **Read the error message** — This tells you the *specific* problem
3. **Go to the line number** — The stack trace points you to the *exact location*

Don't just stare at the error. Don't randomly change code. Read. Understand. Navigate. Fix.

sections.title: Understanding the Stack Trace
sections.explanation:
The stack trace reads from **top to bottom** = most recent call first.

1. The error occurred at line 6 in `formatGreeting` — `name` is undefined, so `.toUpperCase()` fails
2. `formatGreeting` was called from line 2 in `greetUser` — it passed `user.name` which was undefined
3. `greetUser` was called from line 9 — we passed `{}` (an object with no `name` property)

The stack trace shows you the entire chain of events, like rewinding a security camera to see what happened.

sections.definition: A stack trace is a report of the sequence of function calls that were active when an error occurred. It's like a trail of breadcrumbs showing the exact path your code took before it crashed. Read it from top (where the error happened) to bottom (where the chain started).

sections.title: Runtime Errors vs Syntax Errors
sections.explanation:
There's a crucial difference:

**Syntax Errors** happen at 'parse time' — before ANY code runs. JavaScript reads your whole file first, and if the grammar is broken, it refuses to run anything.

**Runtime Errors** happen during execution. Some code may have already run successfully before the error is hit. This is why you sometimes see partial output in the console before a crash.

sections.title: Bugs: A True Story
sections.content: The term 'bug' in computing has a legendary origin. In 1947, engineers working on the Harvard Mark II computer found an actual moth trapped in a relay, causing a malfunction. They taped the moth into their logbook and wrote 'First actual case of bug being found.' While the term 'bug' was used informally before that, this incident cemented it in computing lore. Grace Hopper, who popularized the story, is one of the most important figures in computer science history.

sections.title: Error Detective Challenge
sections.expected_output: All errors fixed! 🎉
sections.hint: Fix 1: Missing closing quote. Fix 2: Misspelled variable — should match what was declared. Fix 3: .split() is a String method, not a Number method. Try .toString().split('') instead.

questions.prompt: What are the three parts of a JavaScript error message?
choices: File name, error code, fix suggestion
Error type, error message, stack trace
Line number, column number, file name
questions.answer: Error type, error message, stack trace
questions.explanation: Every JavaScript error has three parts: the Error Type (e.g., TypeError, ReferenceError), the Error Message (a description of what went wrong), and the Stack Trace (showing the file, line number, and chain of function calls).

questions.answer: ReferenceError
questions.explanation: JavaScript is case-sensitive. `score` (lowercase) and `Score` (uppercase S) are two completely different identifiers. Since `Score` was never declared, JavaScript throws a ReferenceError saying 'Score is not defined'.

questions.answer: TypeError: Cannot read properties of null (reading 'email')
questions.explanation: You're trying to access the `.email` property on `null`. Since `null` is not an object, JavaScript throws a TypeError. This is one of the most common errors — always check if a value is null or undefined before accessing its properties.

questions.prompt: What is the key difference between a SyntaxError and a RuntimeError?
choices: SyntaxErrors are more serious than RuntimeErrors
SyntaxErrors prevent ANY code from running; RuntimeErrors happen during execution
RuntimeErrors only happen in loops; SyntaxErrors happen everywhere
There is no difference — they are the same thing
questions.answer: SyntaxErrors prevent ANY code from running; RuntimeErrors happen during execution
questions.explanation: SyntaxErrors are caught during the parsing phase — JavaScript can't understand the code at all, so nothing executes. RuntimeErrors occur during execution, meaning some code may have already run successfully before the error was hit.

questions.prompt: This code has 3 bugs. Find and fix all of them so it prints 'HELLO, ALICE!' to the console.
questions.hint: Bug 1: Mixed quote types in string concatenation. Bug 2: `frstName` is misspelled. Bug 3: `.toUppercase()` has wrong capitalization — JavaScript methods are case-sensitive.

lessons.title: Try, Catch, and Finally — Handling Errors Gracefully
objectives: Use try...catch to prevent errors from crashing your program
Understand the flow of execution in try/catch/finally blocks
Access and use the error object inside catch blocks
Know when to use finally and why it always runs

sections.content:
# Try, Catch, and Finally

In the last lesson, you learned to **read** error messages. That's essential for debugging. But here's the thing — sometimes errors aren't bugs. Sometimes errors are **expected possibilities**.

What if a user enters invalid data? What if a network request fails? What if a file doesn't exist? These aren't programming mistakes — they're real-world situations your code needs to handle gracefully.

That's where `try...catch` comes in. Instead of letting your program crash, you can **catch** the error, handle it, and keep running.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: The Safety Net
sections.content:
Imagine a trapeze artist performing high above the ground. They're skilled, but sometimes things go wrong — they slip, lose their grip, or misjudge a swing. Without a safety net, one mistake means a disaster. With a safety net, they fall safely, get back up, and continue the show.

`try...catch` is the safety net for your code. The `try` block is the trapeze act — you attempt something risky. The `catch` block is the safety net — if something goes wrong, you land safely and decide what to do next.

sections.title: Your First try...catch
sections.explanation:
The magic of `try...catch`:

1. JavaScript tries to execute the code inside the `try` block
2. If an error occurs, execution immediately jumps to the `catch` block
3. The `catch` block receives an `error` object with details about what went wrong
4. After the `catch` block finishes, the program continues normally
5. The code AFTER the try...catch still runs — no crash!

Without try...catch, the error would crash the entire script. With it, we handle the problem and keep going.

sections.title: The Error Object — Your Detective Kit
sections.explanation:
The `error` parameter in `catch(error)` is an object with three main properties:

- **error.name** — The type of error (TypeError, ReferenceError, etc.)
- **error.message** — A human-readable description of what went wrong
- **error.stack** — The full stack trace showing where the error occurred

You can name this parameter anything (`catch(err)`, `catch(e)`, `catch(oops)`), but `error` or `err` are the conventions.

sections.content:
## No Error? Catch Is Skipped

An important detail: if the code inside `try` runs successfully with **no errors**, the `catch` block is **completely skipped**. It's only activated when something goes wrong.

sections.title: When try Succeeds — catch Is Skipped
sections.explanation: Since `name.toUpperCase()` works perfectly fine (it's a string, and strings have that method), no error is thrown. The `catch` block is ignored entirely, and execution continues after the try...catch.

sections.content:
## The `finally` Block — Always Runs, No Matter What

Sometimes you have cleanup code that **must run** regardless of whether an error occurred or not. That's what `finally` is for.

Common use cases:
- Closing a database connection
- Hiding a loading spinner
- Releasing a file lock
- Resetting a form state

sections.title: try...catch...finally in Action
sections.explanation:
Notice that '🔄 Loading complete' prints in BOTH cases — whether the operation succeeds or fails. The `finally` block is guaranteed to execute.

This is perfect for cleanup tasks. Imagine you show a loading spinner when fetching data — you want to hide it whether the fetch succeeds or fails. `finally` ensures that happens.

Important: `finally` even runs if there's a `return` statement in try or catch! That's how powerful it is.

sections.title: finally Runs Even After return!
sections.content:
This catches many people off guard: if you have a `return` statement inside `try` or `catch`, the `finally` block STILL executes before the function actually returns. This is by design — `finally` is literally unstoppable (except by a full system crash or infinite loop).

However, if `finally` also has a `return` statement, it will override the return value from `try` or `catch`. Avoid returning from `finally` to prevent confusion.

sections.title: Practical Example: Safe JSON Parsing
sections.explanation:
This is one of the most practical uses of try...catch. When you receive data from an API, a form, or localStorage, you can't always guarantee it's valid JSON. Wrapping `JSON.parse()` in try...catch lets you handle the error gracefully instead of crashing.

Returning `null` (or a default value) from the catch block means the calling code can check: `if (user === null) { /* handle the problem */ }`

sections.title: Practical Example: Safe DOM Access
sections.explanation: Here we're combining try...catch with DOM manipulation. Instead of letting your app crash when an element doesn't exist, you handle it gracefully and return a safe default value. The `throw` keyword (which we'll explore in the next lesson) creates a new error when the element is null.

sections.definition: A control structure for handling runtime errors. The `try` block contains code that might fail. The `catch` block runs only if an error occurs, receiving an error object with details. The optional `finally` block runs regardless of the outcome — error or not — and is used for cleanup tasks.

sections.title: When Should You Use try...catch?
sections.content:
Use try...catch when:
✅ Parsing data (JSON.parse, parseInt on unknown input)
✅ Accessing APIs or external resources
✅ Working with user input that could be unpredictable
✅ Calling functions that might throw errors by design
✅ Accessing deeply nested object properties

DON'T use try...catch for:
❌ Flow control (use if/else instead)
❌ Catching bugs that should be fixed in code
❌ Wrapping every single line of code (overkill)

Rule of thumb: Use try...catch for things **outside your control** (user input, network, external data). Fix actual bugs instead of catching them.

sections.title: Build a Safe Calculator
sections.expected_output:
5
null
null
sections.hint: Use typeof to check if inputs are numbers. Use throw new Error('message') to create errors. Wrap everything in try...catch.

questions.answer:
A
C
D
E
questions.explanation:
Step by step:
1. 'A' prints from the try block
2. `x.name` throws a TypeError (x is undefined)
3. 'B' is SKIPPED — error jumps to catch
4. 'C' prints from the catch block
5. 'D' prints from finally — it ALWAYS runs
6. 'E' prints — code after try/catch/finally continues normally

questions.answer:
Start
End
Done
questions.explanation: There's no error in the try block, so ALL lines in try execute. The catch block is completely skipped (no error to catch). The finally block runs regardless, printing 'Done'. Note that 'Error!' never prints because catch was skipped.

questions.prompt: Which property of the error object gives you a human-readable description of what went wrong?
questions.answer: error.message
questions.explanation: The `error.message` property contains a human-readable string describing the error. `error.name` gives the error type (like 'TypeError'). `error.stack` gives the full stack trace. There is no standard `error.description` or `error.text` property.

questions.prompt: Write a function `safeArrayAccess(arr, index)` that uses try...catch. It should return the element at the given index. If `arr` is not an array, it should catch the error and return the string 'Invalid array'. If the index is out of bounds, return 'Index out of bounds'.
test_cases: safeArrayAccess([10, 20], 5) should return 'Index out of bounds'
questions.hint: Use Array.isArray() to check if arr is an array. Check if index >= arr.length or index < 0 for out-of-bounds.

lessons.title: Throwing Errors & Custom Error Types
objectives: Use the throw keyword to create intentional errors
Understand why throwing errors is better than silent failures
Create custom Error objects with meaningful messages
Build validation functions that throw descriptive errors

sections.content:
# Throwing Errors — Taking Control

So far, we've seen errors that JavaScript **generates automatically** when something goes wrong (like accessing `.name` on `undefined`). But you can also **create your own errors on purpose.**

Why would you *want* to create an error? Because sometimes your code is technically valid JavaScript, but it doesn't make sense for your application. JavaScript won't complain about a negative age or an empty username — but **your app should.**

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: The Referee
sections.content:
Think of yourself as a referee in a sports game. The laws of physics (like JavaScript's engine) won't stop a player from picking up the ball in soccer — physics allows it just fine. But as a referee, you know the **rules of the game** and you blow the whistle when something violates those rules.

`throw` is your whistle. JavaScript may not see a problem, but you know your application's rules, and you enforce them by throwing errors when those rules are broken.

sections.title: The throw Keyword
sections.explanation:
`throw new Error("message")` does two things:

1. Creates a new Error object with your custom message
2. Immediately stops the current function's execution

The error propagates up the call stack until it's either caught by a `try...catch` block or crashes the program. This is called 'error propagation' — errors bubble up like a hot potato until someone handles them.

sections.definition: The `throw` keyword creates and fires an error intentionally. When used with `new Error('message')`, it creates an Error object with a custom message and immediately stops the current execution, propagating up the call stack until caught by a try...catch or crashing the program.

sections.title: Throwing and Catching — Working Together
sections.explanation:
This demonstrates the **separation of concerns** pattern:

- `createUser` is responsible for VALIDATION — it knows the rules and throws errors when they're broken
- The calling code is responsible for HANDLING — it wraps the call in try...catch and decides what to do with the error

The function doesn't know or care how the error will be handled. It just reports the problem. This makes the function reusable — different parts of your app can catch and handle the error differently.

sections.content:
## Why Throw Instead of Returning null or false?

You might be thinking: "Why not just return `null` or `false` when something is wrong?" Great question. Let's compare both approaches.

sections.title: Silent Failure vs Throwing Errors
sections.explanation:
Silent failures are dangerous because:
1. The caller might forget to check for null
2. The error appears later in a confusing location
3. The error message doesn't explain the real problem

Throwing errors is better because:
1. The problem is impossible to ignore
2. The error message describes the exact issue
3. The error appears at the point of failure
4. The caller is forced to handle it with try...catch

sections.content:
## Creating Custom Error Types

The built-in `Error` object is great, but sometimes you want to distinguish between different kinds of errors. You can create custom error types using classes (a preview of Module 14!).

sections.title: Custom Error Types
sections.explanation:
Custom error types let you:

1. **Categorize** errors — distinguish validation errors from not-found errors from network errors
2. **Add custom properties** — like `field`, `resource`, `statusCode`, etc.
3. **Handle differently** — use `instanceof` to check the error type and respond appropriately

Don't worry if the `class` and `extends` syntax is unfamiliar — you'll learn all about classes in Module 14. For now, just understand the pattern: different error types for different situations.

sections.title: Error Handling Best Practices
sections.content:
1. **Be specific** — `throw new Error('Email format invalid: missing @')` is better than `throw new Error('Bad input')`

2. **Throw early** — Check inputs at the start of functions, before doing any work

3. **Include context** — Include the problematic value in the message: `Invalid age: ${age}` helps debugging

4. **Don't catch errors you can't handle** — If you don't know what to do with an error, let it propagate up

5. **Never use empty catch blocks** — `catch(e) {}` swallows errors silently. At minimum, log it.

sections.title: Error Handling in Form Validation (Bringing It All Together)
sections.explanation: This pattern collects ALL validation errors before throwing, rather than stopping at the first one. This is a much better user experience — the user sees all problems at once and can fix them all, rather than playing whack-a-mole with one error at a time.

sections.title: Build a Validation Function with throw
sections.expected_output:
true
Age cannot be negative
Age must be a number
sections.hint: Use typeof age !== 'number' to check if it's a number. Use throw new Error('your message') to create errors.

questions.prompt: What happens when a `throw` statement is executed inside a function?
choices: The function pauses and resumes later
The function immediately stops and the error propagates up the call stack
Nothing — throw is just a console warning
questions.answer: The function immediately stops and the error propagates up the call stack
questions.explanation: When `throw` executes, the current function stops immediately. Any code after the throw statement is skipped. The error travels up the call stack looking for a `catch` block. If none is found, the program crashes.

questions.answer:
10
Negative!
questions.explanation:
1. `check(5)` returns 10 — prints 10
2. `check(-3)` throws an error — execution immediately jumps to catch
3. `check(10)` is NEVER reached because the error from step 2 jumped to catch
4. The catch block prints 'Negative!'

Key insight: once an error is thrown inside a try block, ALL remaining code in that try block is skipped.

questions.prompt: Why is throwing an error generally better than returning `null` for invalid inputs?
choices: Throwing errors is faster for performance
Thrown errors are impossible to ignore and include descriptive messages
null is deprecated in modern JavaScript
questions.answer: Thrown errors are impossible to ignore and include descriptive messages
questions.explanation: When you return null, the caller might forget to check for it, leading to confusing errors later (like 'Cannot read properties of null'). Thrown errors force the caller to handle the problem, and they include clear messages describing exactly what went wrong.

questions.prompt: Write a function `withdraw(balance, amount)` that throws an Error with the message 'Insufficient funds' if amount > balance, throws 'Invalid amount' if amount <= 0, and returns the new balance otherwise.
questions.hint: Check if amount <= 0 first (throw 'Invalid amount'). Then check if amount > balance (throw 'Insufficient funds'). Otherwise, return balance - amount.

lessons.title: Defensive Programming & Debugging Techniques
objectives: Write defensive code that prevents errors before they happen
Use optional chaining (?.) and nullish coalescing (??) operators
Master console methods for debugging (log, table, group, time)
Use the browser debugger with breakpoints

sections.content:
# Defensive Programming & Debugging

So far you've learned to **read** errors and **handle** them with try...catch. But the best error handling strategy is **preventing errors in the first place.**

Defensive programming is the practice of writing code that anticipates problems and guards against them. It's like driving defensively — you don't just follow the rules, you also watch out for other drivers who might not.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: Defensive Driving
sections.content:
A defensive driver doesn't just trust that other drivers will stop at red lights. They look both ways, keep a safe distance, check blind spots, and plan escape routes. They hope for the best but prepare for the worst.

Defensive programming is the same: don't trust that data will always be valid, that DOM elements will always exist, or that users will always enter correct information. Write code that handles the unexpected gracefully.

## Guard Clauses: Check First, Act Second

A **guard clause** is an early `return` (or `throw`) that validates inputs at the start of a function before doing any real work. Think of it as a bouncer at a club — checking IDs at the door so the party inside isn't disrupted.

sections.title: Guard Clauses Pattern
sections.explanation:
Guard clauses eliminate the 'pyramid of doom' (deeply nested if statements). Each guard checks one condition and exits early if it fails. If execution reaches the main logic, ALL validations have passed.

This pattern makes code:
- Easier to read (flat structure)
- Easier to maintain (add new checks easily)
- Self-documenting (each guard clause explains a requirement)

sections.content:
## Optional Chaining (?.) — The Safe Navigator

One of the most common errors in JavaScript is the dreaded **"Cannot read properties of undefined."** This happens when you try to access a property on something that's `undefined` or `null`.

ES2020 introduced **optional chaining** (`?.`) to solve this elegantly.

sections.title: Optional Chaining (?.) Operator
sections.explanation:
The `?.` operator is like a careful explorer. At each step, it checks: 'Does this thing exist?' If yes, it continues. If no, it returns `undefined` immediately instead of crashing.

Think of it as a chain of doors. Normal dot notation (`user.address.city`) kicks open every door and crashes if one is locked. Optional chaining (`user?.address?.city`) gently checks each door — if one is locked, it says 'undefined' and walks away peacefully.

Read `user?.address?.city` as: 'If user exists, AND user.address exists, then give me city. Otherwise, undefined.'

sections.definition: The optional chaining operator `?.` allows you to safely access deeply nested object properties without checking each level for null/undefined. If any link in the chain is null or undefined, the expression short-circuits and returns `undefined` instead of throwing a TypeError.

sections.content:
## Nullish Coalescing (??) — Smart Defaults

Optional chaining returns `undefined` when something doesn't exist. But often you want a **default value** instead. That's where the **nullish coalescing operator** (`??`) comes in.

sections.title: Nullish Coalescing (??) Operator
sections.explanation:
The difference between `||` and `??`:

`||` replaces ANY falsy value: `false`, `0`, `""`, `null`, `undefined`, `NaN`
`??` replaces ONLY `null` and `undefined`

Use `??` when `0`, `false`, or `""` are valid values that shouldn't be replaced by defaults. This is almost always the safer choice for default values.

sections.title: Combining ?. and ?? — The Power Combo
sections.explanation:
The `?.` + `??` combination is incredibly powerful:

1. `?.` safely navigates the property chain (no crashes)
2. `??` provides a sensible default if the result is null/undefined

This one-line pattern replaces 5-6 lines of manual checking code. It's one of the most practical features in modern JavaScript.

sections.content:
## Debugging with Console Methods

You probably already use `console.log()`. But the console object has many more powerful methods that most beginners don't know about.

sections.title: Advanced Console Methods
sections.explanation:
These tools are incredibly useful for debugging:

- `console.table()` makes arrays of objects easy to scan visually
- `console.group()` organizes complex logging
- `console.time()` measures performance
- `console.warn()` / `console.error()` stand out with colors
- `console.count()` tracks how many times code runs
- `console.assert()` only logs when something is wrong

Using the right console method saves significant debugging time.

sections.title: The Browser Debugger
sections.content:
While console.log is great, the browser's built-in debugger is even more powerful:

1. **Open DevTools** (F12 or Ctrl+Shift+I)
2. Go to the **Sources** tab
3. Find your JavaScript file
4. **Click on a line number** to set a breakpoint
5. Refresh the page — execution pauses at your breakpoint
6. You can now:
   - Hover over variables to see their values
   - Step through code line by line (F10)
   - Step into function calls (F11)
   - See the call stack and scope

You can also add `debugger;` directly in your code — it acts like a breakpoint.

```javascript
function calculateTotal(items) {
  debugger;  // Execution will pause here when DevTools is open
  return items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + item.price, 0);
}
```

sections.title: Defensive Programming Checklist in Practice
sections.explanation:
This function demonstrates every defensive technique:

1. **Guard clause** — Returns early if input is missing
2. **try...catch** — Wraps risky operations
3. **Optional chaining** — Safely accesses nested properties
4. **Nullish coalescing** — Provides safe defaults
5. **Input validation** — Checks if user exists
6. **Graceful degradation** — Shows a friendly message instead of crashing

This is what professional code looks like. No crashes, no blank screens, no cryptic errors for the user.

sections.title: Practice Defensive Programming
sections.expected_output:
PARIS
UNKNOWN
UNKNOWN
UNKNOWN
UNKNOWN
sections.hint: Use optional chaining (?.) to safely navigate user?.address?.city. Use nullish coalescing (??) to default to 'UNKNOWN'. Then call .toUpperCase() on the result.

questions.answer: Unknown
questions.explanation: `user?.address` evaluates to `undefined` because `user` has no `address` property. The `?.` prevents a crash and returns `undefined`. Then `undefined?.city` also returns `undefined`. Finally, `undefined ?? "Unknown"` gives us `"Unknown"` because `??` replaces null/undefined with the default.

questions.prompt: What is the key difference between `||` and `??` when providing default values?
choices: They are identical — there is no difference
`||` replaces any falsy value; `??` replaces only null and undefined
`||` only works with strings; `??` works with all types
questions.answer: `||` replaces any falsy value; `??` replaces only null and undefined
questions.explanation: The `||` operator treats `false`, `0`, `""`, `null`, `undefined`, and `NaN` as falsy and replaces them all with the default. The `??` operator only replaces `null` and `undefined`. This means `0 ?? 42` gives `0` (keeping the value), while `0 || 42` gives `42` (replacing it).

`count` is `0`, which is falsy but not null/undefined.
- `0 || 10` → 0 is falsy, so `||` returns `10`
- `0 ?? 10` → 0 is NOT null/undefined, so `??` keeps `0`

This is why `??` is usually the safer choice for default values — it doesn't accidentally replace valid values like `0`, `false`, or `""`.

questions.prompt: What is a 'guard clause'?
choices: A try...catch block around the entire function
An early return or throw at the start of a function to validate inputs
A special JavaScript keyword for security
A type of loop that prevents infinite execution
questions.answer: An early return or throw at the start of a function to validate inputs
questions.explanation: A guard clause checks for invalid conditions at the very beginning of a function and returns or throws immediately, preventing the rest of the function from running with bad data. This keeps code flat and readable, avoiding deep nesting.

lessons.title: Introduction to Browser Storage
objectives: Understand why we need browser storage
Know the difference between localStorage, sessionStorage, and cookies
Understand data persistence and the concept of client-side storage
Choose the right storage mechanism for different use cases

sections.content:
# Introduction to Browser Storage

Here's a frustrating user experience: you fill out a long form, accidentally close the tab, and **everything is gone.** Or you set your preferred theme to dark mode, refresh the page, and it's back to blinding white.

So far, everything in your JavaScript programs disappears the moment the page refreshes. Variables live only in memory — they're erased when the page reloads. This is like writing in sand on the beach — one wave (refresh) and it's all gone.

Browser storage solves this. It lets you **save data on the user's computer** so it survives page refreshes, tab closures, and even browser restarts.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: Filing Cabinets
sections.content:
Think of your JavaScript variables as notes on a whiteboard — visible and useful right now, but wiped clean at the end of the day.

Browser storage is like a filing cabinet in the room. You can write information on cards and store them in the cabinet. When you come back tomorrow (refresh the page), the whiteboard is clean, but the filing cabinet still has your cards.

Different storage types are like different kinds of filing cabinets:
- **localStorage** — A permanent filing cabinet. Papers stay until you remove them.
- **sessionStorage** — A temporary desk tray. Papers stay as long as you're sitting at the desk (browser tab is open). When you leave (close the tab), the tray is emptied.
- **Cookies** — Tiny sticky notes that get attached to every letter you send (every network request).

sections.title: Browser Storage Options Compared
rows: Same origin, sent with HTTP requests

sections.description: A diagram showing three storage types side by side. On the left, a tall permanent filing cabinet labeled 'localStorage' with notes '✅ Survives refresh', '✅ Survives tab close', '✅ Survives browser restart'. In the middle, a desk tray labeled 'sessionStorage' with notes '✅ Survives refresh', '❌ Cleared on tab close'. On the right, a tiny sticky note pad labeled 'Cookies (4KB max)' with notes '✅ Has expiration', '⚠️ Sent with every request', '🔒 Used for auth'. All three are inside a large box labeled 'The Browser' with a note at the top: 'All data is stored per origin (domain)'.
sections.alt_text: Visual comparison of localStorage, sessionStorage, and cookies showing their persistence levels and characteristics

sections.content:
## The Same-Origin Policy

All browser storage is sandboxed by **origin** (protocol + domain + port). This means:

- `https://mysite.com` can only access its OWN stored data
- `https://evil.com` CANNOT read `mysite.com`'s localStorage
- Even `http://mysite.com` and `https://mysite.com` are different origins!

This is a critical security feature. Without it, any website could read your saved passwords, preferences, and authentication tokens from other sites.

sections.definition: A security mechanism that restricts how documents or scripts loaded from one origin can interact with resources from another origin. For browser storage, this means data saved by one website (origin) cannot be accessed by a different website. An origin is defined by the combination of protocol (http/https), domain, and port.

sections.content:
## Which Storage Should You Use?

Here's a practical decision guide:

**Use localStorage when:**
- Saving user preferences (dark mode, language)
- Caching data that doesn't change often
- Storing the user's shopping cart
- Saving progress in a game or tutorial

**Use sessionStorage when:**
- Storing data for a multi-step form (wizard)
- Keeping scroll position in a single-page app
- Temporary data that should reset when the user closes the tab

**Use cookies when:**
- Authentication tokens (the server needs to read them)
- Tracking/analytics
- Data that must be sent with every server request
- Data that needs a specific expiration date

sections.title: Quick Preview: localStorage in 30 Seconds
sections.explanation: localStorage has four simple methods: setItem(), getItem(), removeItem(), and clear(). It stores everything as strings (we'll learn how to handle objects and arrays in a later lesson). The data persists until you explicitly delete it or the user clears their browser data.

sections.title: Important Security Note
sections.content:
NEVER store sensitive data like passwords, credit card numbers, or personal identification numbers in localStorage or sessionStorage. This data is:

1. **Not encrypted** — anyone with access to the browser can read it
2. **Vulnerable to XSS** — if an attacker injects JavaScript into your page, they can read all localStorage data
3. **Visible in DevTools** — open Application tab → Local Storage and see everything

For sensitive authentication data, use httpOnly cookies (which JavaScript can't access) and always use HTTPS.

sections.title: Explore Your Browser's Storage
sections.expected_output:
Items in localStorage: [varies]
...
Added item! Check Application tab in DevTools.
sections.hint: Open your browser's DevTools (F12), go to the Application tab, and click on 'Local Storage' in the left sidebar to see your stored items.

questions.prompt: What happens to data stored in `sessionStorage` when the user closes the browser tab?
choices: It is sent to the server
questions.answer: It is deleted automatically
questions.explanation: sessionStorage data is tied to the specific browser tab or window. When the tab is closed, all sessionStorage data for that tab is automatically deleted. This is in contrast to localStorage, which persists until explicitly removed.

questions.prompt: Which browser storage mechanism is sent to the server with every HTTP request?
questions.answer: Cookies
questions.explanation: Cookies are automatically included in every HTTP request to the same origin. This makes them useful for authentication tokens but also means they add overhead to every request. localStorage and sessionStorage data stays in the browser and is never sent to the server automatically.

questions.prompt: Why can't `https://evil.com` read localStorage data saved by `https://mysite.com`?
choices: Because of the Same-Origin Policy — each origin has its own isolated storage
questions.answer: Because of the Same-Origin Policy — each origin has its own isolated storage
questions.explanation: The Same-Origin Policy is a fundamental browser security feature. Each origin (protocol + domain + port) has its own isolated storage space. A page from `evil.com` cannot access data stored by `mysite.com` — the browser enforces complete separation.

questions.prompt: You're building a multi-step checkout wizard (3 pages). The user's cart data should be preserved while moving between steps but should NOT persist after checkout is complete. Which storage is most appropriate?
choices: sessionStorage — it lasts for the session and is easy to clear
Cookies — they get sent to the server
JavaScript variables — just use a global variable
questions.answer: sessionStorage — it lasts for the session and is easy to clear
questions.explanation: sessionStorage is ideal because: (1) Data survives page navigation between steps, (2) Data automatically clears when the tab is closed, (3) You can manually clear it after checkout with sessionStorage.clear(). localStorage would work but requires manual cleanup. Cookies add unnecessary overhead. Global variables would be lost on page navigation.

lessons.title: localStorage — Deep Dive
objectives: Master all localStorage methods: setItem, getItem, removeItem, clear
Understand that localStorage only stores strings
Use JSON.stringify() and JSON.parse() to store complex data
Build practical features using localStorage

sections.content:
# localStorage — Deep Dive

`localStorage` is the most commonly used browser storage mechanism. It's simple, persistent, and has a generous storage limit (~5-10MB depending on the browser). In this lesson, you'll master every aspect of it.
