sections.explanation: Callback hell is what happens when you need to perform multiple async operations in sequence, each depending on the previous result. The code nests deeper and deeper, forming a pyramid shape. Error handling is duplicated at every level. This is one of the main reasons Promises were invented.

sections.title: Callback Hell Visualization
sections.description: Visual comparison of deeply nested callback hell (pyramid shape) vs flat Promise chain.

sections.title: When You'll Still See Callbacks
sections.content:
Even though Promises and async/await are now preferred, you'll still encounter callbacks in:

1. **setTimeout / setInterval** — These always use callbacks
2. **Event listeners** — `addEventListener('click', callback)`
3. **Array methods** — `forEach`, `map`, `filter` all take callbacks
4. **Legacy Node.js code** — Older Node.js libraries use error-first callbacks
5. **Some browser APIs** — `geolocation.getCurrentPosition(successCallback, errorCallback)`

Callbacks aren't bad — they're the wrong tool for chaining multiple async operations. For simple, one-off async operations, callbacks are perfectly fine!

sections.title: Mitigating Callback Hell (Before Promises)
sections.explanation: Named functions flatten the pyramid but scatter the logic across multiple functions. You lose the ability to see the full flow in one place. This was the best practice before Promises, but it's still not great. Promises and async/await give us the best of both worlds.

questions.prompt: What is 'callback hell'?
choices: When you have too many event listeners
Deeply nested callbacks that form a pyramid shape, making code hard to read and maintain
When callbacks run out of order
questions.answer: Deeply nested callbacks that form a pyramid shape, making code hard to read and maintain
questions.explanation: Callback hell occurs when multiple async operations must run in sequence, each nested inside the previous callback. The code shifts right with each level of nesting, forming a pyramid. Error handling is repeated at every level, and the code becomes very hard to read, debug, and modify.

questions.prompt: In the error-first callback convention, what does `callback(null, data)` mean?
choices: An error occurred and data is null
The operation succeeded — null means no error, data is the result
Both error and data are being passed
questions.answer: The operation succeeded — null means no error, data is the result
questions.explanation: In the error-first convention, the first argument is always the error. If it's null or undefined, the operation succeeded. The second argument is the result data. callback(new Error('failed'), null) means an error occurred. callback(null, result) means success.

questions.prompt: Write a function `delayedGreet(name, callback)` that waits 1 second, then calls the callback with the string 'Hello, [name]!'. Then call it and log the greeting.

questions.answer: A, C, E, B, D: data
questions.explanation: fetchData is called: logs 'A', schedules setTimeout (async), logs 'C'. Returns to caller, logs 'E'. All synchronous code is done. Event loop picks up setTimeout callback: logs 'B', calls callback which logs 'D: data'. Even with 0ms delay, setTimeout is always async.

lessons.title: Promises — The Modern Solution
objectives: Understand what a Promise is and its three states
Create Promises with the Promise constructor
Use .then() to handle resolved values
Use .catch() to handle errors
Convert callback-based functions to Promise-based functions

sections.content:
# Promises — An IOU for Future Data

A **Promise** is JavaScript's modern way to handle async operations. It represents a value that doesn't exist YET but will (or won't) exist in the future.

Promises were added to JavaScript in ES6 (2015) to solve the problems of callback hell — and they're now the foundation of ALL modern async JavaScript, including async/await and fetch.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: The Order Receipt 🧾
sections.content:
When you order food at a fast-food restaurant:

1. You place your order and pay
2. You get a **receipt with a number** (this is the Promise!)
3. The receipt is a PROMISE that you will get food... eventually
4. You sit down and do other things (non-blocking!)
5. Your number is called — two possible outcomes:
   - ✅ **Fulfilled/Resolved:** You get your food! (.then)
   - ❌ **Rejected:** They're out of chicken — sorry! (.catch)

The receipt IS the Promise. It's not the food itself — it's a placeholder that represents food that will arrive in the future. You can hold the receipt and plan what to do when your food arrives (.then) or if there's a problem (.catch).

sections.definition:
A Promise has exactly three possible states:

1. ⏳ **Pending** — The async operation is still running. Neither fulfilled nor rejected yet.
2. ✅ **Fulfilled (Resolved)** — The operation completed successfully. The Promise has a result value.
3. ❌ **Rejected** — The operation failed. The Promise has an error reason.

A Promise can only transition ONCE: from pending → fulfilled OR from pending → rejected. Once settled, it never changes again.

sections.title: Promise Lifecycle
sections.content:
┌──────────────────────┐
                    │                      │
                    │   PENDING ⏳         │
                    │   (initial state)    │
                    │                      │
                    └──────────┬───────────┘
                               │
                    Async operation completes
                               │
                ┌──────────────┴──────────────┐
                │                             │
       ┌────────▼─────────┐         ┌─────────▼────────┐
       │                  │         │                  │
       │  FULFILLED ✅    │         │  REJECTED ❌     │
       │  (resolved)      │         │  (error)         │
       │                  │         │                  │
       │  Has a VALUE     │         │  Has a REASON    │
       │  ↓               │         │  ↓               │
       │  .then(value)    │         │  .catch(error)   │
       │                  │         │                  │
       └──────────────────┘         └──────────────────┘

       Once settled (fulfilled or rejected), a Promise
       NEVER changes state again. It's immutable.
sections.description: Diagram showing the three states of a Promise and the transitions between them.

sections.title: Creating a Promise
sections.explanation: The Promise constructor takes an 'executor' function that runs immediately. Inside it, you do your async work. Call resolve(value) when it succeeds, or reject(error) when it fails. Outside the constructor, .then() handles success, .catch() handles errors. Notice the code AFTER the promise creation runs immediately — it doesn't wait.

sections.title: Practical Promise Examples
sections.explanation: These are real-world patterns you'll use constantly. The wait() function wraps setTimeout in a Promise for clean delay handling. The fetchUser() function simulates an API call, returning a Promise that resolves with user data or rejects with an error.

sections.title: Converting Callbacks to Promises (Promisification)
sections.explanation: Promisification is wrapping a callback-based function in a Promise. This lets you use .then/.catch instead of error-first callbacks. The generic promisify utility works with any function that follows the error-first callback convention. Node.js has a built-in util.promisify() that does exactly this.

sections.title: Quick Promise Shortcuts

questions.prompt: What are the three states of a Promise?
questions.answer: Pending, Fulfilled, Rejected
questions.explanation: A Promise is Pending while the async operation is running. It becomes Fulfilled (resolved) if the operation succeeds, or Rejected if it fails. Once it transitions from Pending to either Fulfilled or Rejected, it stays in that state forever.

questions.answer: 'first' — The .then handler runs with 'first'. The second resolve and the reject are ignored.
questions.explanation: A Promise can only settle ONCE. The first resolve('first') changes the state to Fulfilled. After that, the second resolve('second') and reject('error') are completely ignored — the Promise is already settled. Only the .then runs with 'first'.

questions.prompt: Write a function `coinFlip()` that returns a Promise. After 1 second, it should randomly either resolve with 'Heads!' or reject with new Error('Tails!'). Use Math.random() > 0.5 for the coin flip.
Which method handles a rejected Promise?
questions.answer: .catch()
questions.explanation: .catch() handles rejected Promises (errors). .then() handles fulfilled Promises (success). .finally() runs regardless of success or failure (cleanup). .reject() is not a method on Promise instances — it's Promise.reject() which creates a rejected Promise.

lessons.title: Promise Chaining and Error Handling
objectives: Chain multiple .then() calls for sequential async operations
Understand how values pass through a Promise chain
Use .catch() to handle errors anywhere in the chain
Use .finally() for cleanup code that always runs

sections.content:
# Promise Chaining — The Callback Hell Killer

Remember callback hell? Deeply nested callbacks forming a pyramid of doom? Promise chaining is the solution. Instead of nesting, you chain `.then()` calls in a flat, readable sequence.

The magic: **every `.then()` returns a NEW Promise**, which means you can chain another `.then()` onto it. The return value of one `.then()` becomes the input of the next.

sections.title: Promise Chaining — Flat and Beautiful
sections.explanation: Each .then() returns the next Promise in the chain. The key is the RETURN statement — if you return a Promise from .then(), the next .then() waits for it to resolve. If you return a regular value, the next .then() receives it immediately. One .catch() at the end handles errors from ANY step in the chain.

sections.definition:
In a Promise chain, what you RETURN from `.then()` determines what the NEXT `.then()` receives:

- **Return a value** → next `.then` gets that value immediately
- **Return a Promise** → next `.then` waits for it to resolve, then gets its value
- **Throw an error** → skips to `.catch()`
- **Return nothing** → next `.then` gets `undefined`

sections.title: Passing Values Through the Chain
sections.explanation: Always return values from .then() callbacks! Without a return statement, the next .then() receives undefined. If you return a Promise, the chain automatically waits for it to resolve. This is what makes Promise chains so powerful — they handle both sync and async steps seamlessly.

sections.title: Error Handling in Promise Chains
sections.explanation: When an error occurs anywhere in the chain (rejected Promise or thrown Error), it skips all subsequent .then() calls and jumps to the nearest .catch(). After .catch() handles the error, the chain can continue normally if .catch() returns a value. This is similar to try/catch in synchronous code.

sections.title: .finally() — Cleanup Code That Always Runs
sections.explanation: .finally() is called after the Promise settles, regardless of whether it was fulfilled or rejected. It doesn't receive any arguments and doesn't change the settled value. It's perfect for cleanup tasks that must run in all cases: hiding loading indicators, closing connections, clearing timers.

sections.title: Common Promise Mistakes

questions.prompt:
What does this output?
javascript
Promise.resolve(5)
  .then(x => x * 2)
  .then(x => x + 3)
  .then(x => console.log(x));
questions.explanation: The chain starts with 5. First .then: 5 * 2 = 10. Second .then: 10 + 3 = 13. Third .then: console.log(13). Each .then receives the return value of the previous .then.

questions.prompt: What happens when an error occurs in the middle of a Promise chain?
choices: All subsequent .then() calls are skipped, and execution jumps to the nearest .catch()
questions.answer: All subsequent .then() calls are skipped, and execution jumps to the nearest .catch()
questions.explanation: When a Promise is rejected or an error is thrown inside .then(), the error propagates down the chain, skipping all .then() handlers, until it reaches a .catch(). This is similar to how a thrown exception skips code until it hits a try/catch block.

questions.prompt: Chain three .then() calls onto Promise.resolve(10). The first should double the value, the second should subtract 5, the third should log the result. Add a .catch() at the end.
What does .finally() do?
choices: It runs only when the Promise is fulfilled
It runs only when the Promise is rejected
It runs after the Promise settles, regardless of success or failure
questions.answer: It runs after the Promise settles, regardless of success or failure
questions.explanation: .finally() runs no matter what — whether the Promise was fulfilled or rejected. It's used for cleanup code that must happen in all cases, like hiding a loading spinner or closing a database connection. It doesn't receive the resolved value or error, and it doesn't change the chain's result.

lessons.title: async/await — The Elegant Way
objectives: Use the async keyword to declare async functions
Use the await keyword to pause execution until a Promise resolves
Handle errors with try/catch in async functions
Understand that async/await is syntactic sugar over Promises

sections.content:
# async/await — Write Async Code That Looks Synchronous

Promise chains were a huge improvement over callbacks, but they can still be hard to read with complex logic. ES2017 introduced `async/await` — a way to write asynchronous code that LOOKS and READS like synchronous code.

**async/await is NOT a new async mechanism** — it's syntactic sugar on top of Promises. Under the hood, it's still Promises all the way. But the code is SO much cleaner that async/await is now the standard way to write async JavaScript.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: Reading a Recipe
sections.content:
Promise chains read like a recipe with arrows:
"Get flour → THEN mix with eggs → THEN pour in pan → THEN bake → IF ERROR start over"

async/await reads like a normal recipe:
"1. Get flour
2. Mix with eggs
3. Pour in pan
4. Bake
(If anything goes wrong, start over)"

Same steps, same result, but async/await reads like natural instructions — no .then() or .catch() everywhere.

sections.title: Your First async/await
sections.explanation: The async keyword marks a function as asynchronous. Inside an async function, you can use await to PAUSE execution until a Promise resolves. The resolved value is returned directly — no .then() needed. Errors are caught with regular try/catch — no .catch() needed.

sections.definition:
**async** function:
- Declared with `async function name()` or `const name = async () => {}`
- ALWAYS returns a Promise (even if you return a plain value)
- Allows you to use `await` inside it

**await** expression:
- Can ONLY be used inside an `async` function (or at the top level of a module)
- Pauses the async function until the Promise settles
- Returns the resolved value of the Promise
- If the Promise rejects, it throws the error (catchable with try/catch)

sections.title: Rewriting the Full Chain with async/await
sections.explanation: Look at how clean this is compared to callback hell AND Promise chains! It reads top-to-bottom like synchronous code. Variables are in scope (no need to pass them through .then chains). try/catch/finally works just like in synchronous code. This is why async/await took over the JavaScript world.

sections.title: async/await with Conditional Logic and Loops
sections.explanation: async/await makes complex control flow simple. You can use regular for loops, if/else statements, and try/catch blocks with async operations. Try/catch inside a loop lets you handle errors for individual items without stopping the entire process. This is nearly impossible to do cleanly with Promise chains.

sections.title: Important: async Functions ALWAYS Return a Promise
sections.explanation: async functions ALWAYS return a Promise. If you return a value, it's automatically wrapped in Promise.resolve(). If you throw an error, it's wrapped in Promise.reject(). This means any async function can be used with .then()/.catch() OR with await.

sections.title: Common async/await Mistakes

questions.prompt: What does the `await` keyword do?
choices: Pauses the async function until the Promise resolves, then returns the resolved value
questions.answer: Pauses the async function until the Promise resolves, then returns the resolved value
questions.explanation: await pauses the execution of the async function (without blocking the main thread!) until the given Promise settles. If it resolves, await returns the value. If it rejects, await throws the error (which you can catch with try/catch). Other code outside the async function continues to run normally.

questions.answer: Promise { 'Hello!' } — because async functions ALWAYS return a Promise, even if you return a plain value.
questions.explanation: async functions wrap their return value in a Promise. Even though we wrote `return 'Hello!'`, the function actually returns `Promise.resolve('Hello!')`. To get the actual string, you'd need `greet().then(v => console.log(v))` or `await greet()` inside another async function.

questions.prompt:
Rewrite this Promise chain as an async function using async/await and try/catch:
javascript
fetchUser(1)
  .then(user => fetchOrders(user.id))
  .then(orders => console.log(orders))
  .catch(err => console.error(err.message));
Why doesn't await work inside forEach?
choices: forEach fires all callbacks immediately without waiting for each to complete — it doesn't understand Promises
await only works with for loops
questions.answer: forEach fires all callbacks immediately without waiting for each to complete — it doesn't understand Promises
questions.explanation: forEach ignores the return value of its callback. Even if the callback is async (returns a Promise), forEach doesn't await it — it just moves to the next iteration. Use `for...of` when you need sequential async operations, or `Promise.all(array.map(...))` for parallel operations.

questions.answer: D, A, E, B, C
questions.explanation: D is sync — runs first. demo() is called: A runs immediately (sync part of async function). Then await pauses demo — control returns to the caller. E runs (sync). Then the event loop resumes demo: B is logged (the resolved value), then C. The key: everything AFTER the first await runs asynchronously.

lessons.title: Parallel Promises — Promise.all, Promise.race, and More
objectives: Run multiple async operations in parallel with Promise.all
Use Promise.race to respond to the fastest result
Use Promise.allSettled when you need all results regardless of errors
Use Promise.any for the first successful result
Know when to use sequential vs parallel execution

sections.content:
# Parallel Promises — Speed Up Your Async Code

So far, we've run async operations **sequentially** — one after another. But what if you need to fetch a user's profile, their posts, AND their notifications? These three requests are **independent** — none depends on the result of another.

Running them sequentially wastes time:
- Sequential: 1s + 1s + 1s = **3 seconds** ⏳
- Parallel: max(1s, 1s, 1s) = **1 second** ⚡

Promise.all lets you fire multiple Promises at once and wait for ALL of them to complete.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: Ordering at a Food Court 🍕🍔🌮
sections.content:
**Sequential (slow):**
You go to the pizza place, wait for your pizza. Then go to the burger place, wait for your burger. Then go to the taco place, wait for your taco.
Total: 10 + 5 + 3 = 18 minutes.

**Parallel with Promise.all (fast):**
You send a friend to each place at the SAME time. You wait until ALL friends return.
Total: max(10, 5, 3) = 10 minutes. Nearly 2x faster!

**Promise.race:**
You go to whoever finishes FIRST. "First one back wins!"
Total: min(10, 5, 3) = 3 minutes.

**Promise.any:**
Like race, but if the first one fails (taco place is closed), wait for the next successful one.

sections.title: Promise.all — Wait for Everything
sections.explanation: Promise.all fires all three requests simultaneously. It waits until ALL are complete, then returns an array with all results in order. The total time is the duration of the SLOWEST request (1.2s), not the sum of all requests (3s). Use destructuring to name each result cleanly.

sections.title: Promise.all — Error Handling (All or Nothing!)
sections.explanation: Promise.all follows an 'all or nothing' strategy. If even ONE Promise rejects, the entire Promise.all rejects and you lose all results — even from the successful ones. This is fine when all operations must succeed (like a bank transfer). For optional operations, use Promise.allSettled instead.

sections.title: Promise.allSettled — Get ALL Results, Success or Failure
sections.explanation: Promise.allSettled is perfect when you want ALL results and can tolerate individual failures. Each result has a status ('fulfilled' or 'rejected') and either a value (success) or reason (error). Use it for loading dashboard widgets where some might fail independently.

sections.title: Promise.race — First One Wins
sections.explanation: Promise.race resolves or rejects with the result of whichever Promise settles first. The most practical use is implementing timeouts — race a network request against a timer. If the timer wins, you know the request took too long.

sections.title: Promise.any — First SUCCESS Wins
sections.explanation: Promise.any ignores rejections and resolves with the first SUCCESSFUL result. It only rejects if ALL Promises reject (with an AggregateError containing all errors). Great for redundancy patterns — try multiple sources and use whichever succeeds first.

rows: First to settle is a rejection
sections.title: Promise Combinators Cheat Sheet

sections.hint: Independent operations should run in parallel with Promise.all. Only use sequential await when each step depends on the previous one's result.
sections.title: Sequential vs Parallel Performance
sections.expected_output:
Sequential: ~3300ms
Parallel: ~1500ms — more than 2x faster!

questions.prompt: What happens if one Promise in Promise.all rejects?
choices: It waits for the others and returns partial results
The entire Promise.all rejects immediately, losing all results
It replaces the failed result with null
questions.answer: The entire Promise.all rejects immediately, losing all results
questions.explanation: Promise.all is 'all or nothing.' If ANY Promise rejects, the entire Promise.all rejects with that error. Even if the other Promises succeeded, their results are lost. Use Promise.allSettled if you need all results regardless of individual failures.

questions.prompt: When should you use sequential await vs Promise.all?
choices: Sequential when each step depends on the previous result; Promise.all when operations are independent
It doesn't matter — they produce the same result
questions.answer: Sequential when each step depends on the previous result; Promise.all when operations are independent
questions.explanation: If step 2 needs the result of step 1, you MUST await sequentially. But if the operations are independent (fetch profile AND posts AND notifications), use Promise.all to run them in parallel — it's much faster because they overlap in time.

questions.prompt: Use Promise.all to fetch three things in parallel: fetchUser(1), fetchPosts(1), fetchComments(1). Destructure the results into user, posts, and comments variables. Log all three. Add error handling.
Which Promise combinator should you use to implement a request timeout? (Race a fetch against a timer)
questions.answer: Promise.race — it resolves/rejects with whichever Promise settles first. If the timer finishes before the fetch, you get a timeout error.
questions.explanation: Promise.race settles with the first Promise to complete. By racing a network request against a setTimeout that rejects after N milliseconds, you get a timeout mechanism. If the fetch wins, you get data. If the timer wins, you get a timeout error.

lessons.title: Module 15 Challenge: Build an Async Task Runner
objectives: Apply all async concepts in a single project
Build a task queue that processes async operations
Handle errors gracefully in real-world scenarios
Combine sequential and parallel execution strategies

sections.content:
# 🚀 Module 15 Challenge: Async Task Runner & Data Dashboard

Time to combine EVERYTHING from this module into a practical project. You'll build a system that simulates fetching data from multiple APIs, processes the results, and handles errors gracefully.

This challenge tests:
- **setTimeout** for simulating async operations
- **Promises** for wrapping async work
- **async/await** for clean async code
- **Promise.all** for parallel operations
- **Promise.allSettled** for fault-tolerant operations
- **Error handling** with try/catch
- **The event loop** understanding

sections.title: Part 1: API Simulator — Building Our Fake Backend
sections.explanation: This API simulator mimics real-world conditions: variable response times and random failures. Your task runner must handle both success and failure cases gracefully. Real applications always deal with unreliable networks — your code should be resilient.

sections.title: Part 2: Task Runner — Sequential Execution with Retry
sections.explanation: The withRetry function is a real-world pattern used in production applications. It wraps any async operation and retries it up to N times with exponential backoff (waiting longer between each retry: 500ms, 1000ms, 2000ms). This handles temporary network failures gracefully.

sections.title: Part 3: Dashboard Loader — Parallel + Fault Tolerant
sections.explanation: This is a realistic dashboard loader! The user fetch is CRITICAL (fail if it fails), so we await it with retry. Posts, stats, and weather are OPTIONAL — we use Promise.allSettled to get whatever succeeds. Each piece is handled independently: if stats fail, we still show posts and weather. This is how real production apps work.

sections.hint: The simplest approach is to chunk the tasks array into batches of size `concurrency`, then process each batch with Promise.allSettled before moving to the next. For a more advanced approach, use a pool pattern where a new task starts as soon as one finishes.
sections.title: Your Challenge: Build a Task Queue
sections.expected_output: Tasks run in batches of 2, with each batch waiting for the previous to complete before starting the next.

sections.title: Async Concepts Recap — Everything in This Module
sections.content:
Let's count all the async concepts used in this challenge:

⏳ **Synchronous vs Async** — Understanding what blocks and what doesn't
🔄 **Event Loop** — Knowing the execution order of callbacks, Promises, and macrotasks
⏰ **setTimeout** — Simulating delays and implementing exponential backoff
📞 **Callbacks** — Still used in setTimeout and event listeners
🤝 **Promises** — The foundation of modern async JS
🔗 **Promise Chaining** — Sequential async operations
⏸️ **async/await** — Clean, readable async code
🏎️ **Promise.all** — Parallel execution for independent operations
✅ **Promise.allSettled** — Fault-tolerant parallel execution
🏁 **Promise.race** — Timeouts and performance racing
🔁 **Retry pattern** — Handling transient failures
🎯 **Error handling** — try/catch with async/await

You now have the tools to handle ANY async scenario in JavaScript! 🎉

questions.prompt: Write an async function `fetchWithTimeout(promiseFn, timeoutMs)` that races a Promise (returned by calling promiseFn()) against a timeout. If the Promise doesn't resolve within timeoutMs, reject with 'Request timed out'. Otherwise return the resolved value.
In the dashboard loader, why do we use Promise.allSettled for posts/stats/weather instead of Promise.all?
choices: Because posts/stats/weather are optional — if one fails, we still want to show the others. Promise.all would lose ALL results if one fails.
questions.answer: Because posts/stats/weather are optional — if one fails, we still want to show the others. Promise.all would lose ALL results if one fails.
questions.explanation: Promise.all is 'all or nothing' — one failure means zero results. For optional data like dashboard widgets, we use Promise.allSettled which gives us results from every operation regardless of individual failures. If stats fail, we still display posts and weather.

questions.prompt: What is exponential backoff in the context of retries?
choices: Making each retry faster than the previous one
Doubling the wait time between each retry (500ms → 1000ms → 2000ms) to avoid overwhelming a failing server
Retrying an infinite number of times
questions.answer: Doubling the wait time between each retry (500ms → 1000ms → 2000ms) to avoid overwhelming a failing server
questions.explanation: Exponential backoff increases the delay between retries exponentially (e.g., 500ms, 1000ms, 2000ms, 4000ms). This prevents overwhelming a server that's having problems — if it's struggling, hammering it with rapid retries makes things worse. The increasing delay gives the server time to recover.

questions.prompt:
If 5 independent API calls each take 1 second, how long does it take to run them all with:
a) Sequential await
b) Promise.all
questions.answer:
a) Sequential: ~5 seconds (1s × 5, one after another)
b) Promise.all: ~1 second (all 5 run simultaneously, total = slowest one)
questions.explanation: Sequential await runs each operation one at a time: 1+1+1+1+1 = 5 seconds. Promise.all starts all 5 simultaneously and waits for the slowest: max(1,1,1,1,1) = 1 second. For independent operations, Promise.all is always faster.

questions.prompt: Write an async function `loadMultipleUsers(ids)` that fetches all users in PARALLEL using Promise.allSettled and returns only the successful results (an array of user objects). Use APISimulator.fetchUser.

modules.title: Module 16: Fetch API & Working with Real Data

lessons.title: What Are APIs? (The Waiter Analogy)
objectives: Understand what an API is and why they exist
Know the difference between a web page and a web API
Understand the client-server model
Read basic API documentation
Know what REST means at a high level

sections.content:
# APIs — How Apps Talk to Each Other

You've built interactive web pages with DOM manipulation and events. You've mastered asynchronous JavaScript with Promises and async/await. Now comes the moment everything clicks together: **talking to the outside world**.

Every time you check the weather on your phone, scroll through tweets, search on Google, or see your bank balance in an app — your device is **talking to a server through an API**.

**API** stands for **Application Programming Interface**. It's a set of rules that lets one piece of software talk to another. Specifically, a **Web API** is a server that accepts requests over the internet and sends back data (usually in JSON format).

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: The Restaurant Waiter 🍽️
sections.content:
Imagine you're at a restaurant:

👤 **You (the client/browser)** — You're hungry and want food. You can't go into the kitchen and make it yourself.

🍳 **The Kitchen (the server/database)** — Has all the food and ingredients. Does all the heavy work. You're not allowed in.

🤵 **The Waiter (the API)** — Takes your order (request) to the kitchen, and brings food back to you (response).

📋 **The Menu (API documentation)** — Tells you what you can order. You can't ask for anything that isn't on the menu.

**The flow:**
1. You look at the **menu** (API docs) to see what's available
2. You tell the **waiter** (API) what you want: "I'd like the chicken, please" (HTTP request)
3. The waiter goes to the **kitchen** (server), which prepares your food
4. The waiter brings back your **food** (response with data) — or tells you there's a problem (error)

You never interact with the kitchen directly. The API is the controlled, well-defined interface between you and the data.

sections.title: The Client-Server Model
sections.content:
YOUR BROWSER (Client)                          SERVER (Backend)
  ══════════════════════                     ═══════════════════════
  ┌──────────────────┐                       ┌─────────────────────┐
  │                  │    HTTP Request        │                     │
  │   JavaScript     │  ════════════════►     │   API Endpoint      │
  │                  │  GET /api/users/42     │                     │
  │   fetch()        │                       │   Process Request   │
  │                  │                       │   Query Database    │
  │                  │    HTTP Response       │   Format Response   │
  │   Update DOM     │  ◄════════════════     │                     │
  │   with data      │  { name: 'Alice' }    │                     │
  │                  │                       │                     │
  └──────────────────┘                       └─────────────────────┘

                        ◄── The Internet ──►

  Your JavaScript sends a REQUEST to a URL.
  The server processes it and sends back a RESPONSE.
  Your JavaScript then uses that data to update the page.
sections.description: Diagram showing the flow from browser (client) sending an HTTP request to a server's API endpoint, which processes it and returns an HTTP response with data.

sections.definition:
**REST** (Representational State Transfer) is a set of conventions for how web APIs should work. Most APIs you'll use are RESTful. Key ideas:

- Resources are identified by **URLs**: `/api/users`, `/api/posts/42`
- Actions are represented by **HTTP methods**: GET (read), POST (create), PUT (update), DELETE (remove)
- Data is sent and received as **JSON**
- Each request is **independent** (the server doesn't remember previous requests)

sections.title: What an API Call Looks Like (Preview)
sections.explanation: This is a preview of what you'll master. fetch() sends a request to a URL, the server returns data, and you parse it as JSON. By the end of this module, you'll be building complete apps with real API data.

sections.title: What API Data Looks Like (JSON)
sections.explanation: When you request data from an API, this is what comes back — JSON (JavaScript Object Notation). It looks exactly like a JavaScript object! That's not a coincidence — JSON was designed to be easy for JavaScript to work with. You already know how to work with objects, so you already know how to work with API data.

sections.title: APIs You Use Every Day Without Knowing
sections.company: Google, Spotify, GitHub, Stripe, Twitter/X, and virtually every web company
sections.content:
Every app on your phone is an API client:

📱 **Weather App** → calls a weather API (like OpenWeatherMap) with your location, gets back temperature/forecast data

🗺️ **Google Maps** → calls the Maps API with an address, gets back coordinates and directions

💳 **Any app with payments** → calls Stripe/PayPal API to process your credit card

📧 **Gmail** → calls Google's Gmail API to load/send emails

🎵 **Spotify** → calls the Spotify API to search songs, manage playlists, control playback

🐙 **GitHub** → has a public API that lets you programmatically access repos, issues, pull requests

Every single one follows the same pattern: send a request to a URL, get back JSON data.

sections.title: Free APIs for Practice
sections.content:
You'll use these APIs throughout this module:

🔹 **JSONPlaceholder** — `jsonplaceholder.typicode.com` — Fake data for testing (users, posts, comments)
🔹 **PokéAPI** — `pokeapi.co/api/v2/` — Pokémon data (fun!)
🔹 **Open-Meteo** — `open-meteo.com` — Real weather data, no API key needed
🔹 **REST Countries** — `restcountries.com/v3.1/` — Country information
🔹 **Dog CEO** — `dog.ceo/dog-api/` — Random dog images

All of these are free and don't require signup!

questions.prompt: In the restaurant analogy, what does the API represent?
choices: The waiter — the interface between the client and the server
questions.answer: The waiter — the interface between the client and the server
questions.explanation: The API is like the waiter — it's the intermediary that takes your request (order) to the server (kitchen) and brings back the response (food). You never interact with the server/kitchen directly. The API defines what requests you can make and what responses you'll get.

questions.prompt: What data format do most web APIs return?
questions.answer: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)
questions.explanation: Most modern web APIs return data in JSON format. JSON looks like JavaScript objects and arrays, making it easy to work with in JavaScript. It's lightweight, human-readable, and supported by virtually every programming language.

questions.prompt: What does REST stand for and what is it?
choices: Regular Expression String Testing — a way to validate strings
Representational State Transfer — conventions for how web APIs should work
Remote Execution Server Technology — a type of server
Reactive Event Stream Transport — a data streaming protocol
questions.answer: Representational State Transfer — conventions for how web APIs should work
questions.explanation: REST is a set of conventions that most web APIs follow. It uses URLs to identify resources (/api/users/1), HTTP methods for actions (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE), and JSON for data transfer. RESTful APIs are stateless — each request is independent.

questions.prompt: If an API endpoint is at `https://api.example.com/users/5`, what do you think it returns?
questions.answer: Data about the user with ID 5 — probably a JSON object with the user's name, email, and other profile information.
questions.explanation: REST APIs follow a convention where the URL path represents resources. /users/5 means 'the user resource with ID 5'. The server would return a JSON object containing that user's data. This predictable URL structure is one of the key principles of REST.

lessons.title: HTTP Methods — The Verbs of the Web
objectives: Understand the four main HTTP methods: GET, POST, PUT/PATCH, DELETE
Know when to use each method
Understand HTTP status codes and what they mean
Read and understand HTTP request and response structures

sections.content:
# HTTP Methods — Telling the Server WHAT You Want to Do

When you send a request to an API, you don't just say WHERE (the URL) — you also say WHAT you want to do. Do you want to **read** data? **Create** something new? **Update** existing data? **Delete** something?

HTTP methods (also called HTTP verbs) tell the server your intention. They're the difference between looking at a menu item and ordering it.

sections.title: Real-World Analogy: The Library 📚
sections.content:
Think of an API like a library system:

📖 **GET** = "Can I *read* the book about JavaScript?" → Librarian shows you the book
📝 **POST** = "I want to *add* a new book to the library." → Librarian registers it
✏️ **PUT/PATCH** = "I need to *update* the author's name on this book." → Librarian fixes the record
🗑️ **DELETE** = "Please *remove* this book from the catalog." → Librarian takes it out

Same resource (books), four different actions!

rows: Send data to create a new resource
sections.title: The Four Main HTTP Methods (CRUD)
HTTP Methods in fetch() (Preview)
sections.explanation: GET is the default — just pass a URL. For POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE, you pass an options object specifying the method, headers, and body. The body contains the data you're sending (for create/update operations).

sections.definition: Every HTTP response includes a **status code** — a number that tells you how the request went. Think of them as the server's answer: "All good!" (200), "I created it!" (201), "You messed up!" (400), "Not found!" (404), "I crashed!" (500).

rows: Success, but nothing to return (e.g., delete)
Your request is malformed or invalid
Not logged in / no valid credentials
Server crashed / bug on their side
sections.title: HTTP Status Codes You'll See Most Often
Reading Status Codes in JavaScript
sections.explanation: Every response has a status code. response.ok is a convenient boolean — true for success (200-299), false for errors (400+). Always check response.ok before trying to use the data. We'll explore robust error handling in a later lesson.

sections.title: Quick Status Code Memory Trick
sections.content:
Status codes are grouped by their first digit:

- **1xx** — Informational (rare, you'll almost never see these)
- **2xx** — Success! 🎉 (200 OK, 201 Created)
- **3xx** — Redirect 🔀 (the resource moved somewhere else)
- **4xx** — Client Error ❌ (YOU messed up — bad request, not found, unauthorized)
- **5xx** — Server Error 🔥 (THEY messed up — server crashed, database down)

**The rule:** If the first digit is 2, you're good. If it's 4, check your code. If it's 5, it's not your fault.

sections.title: Anatomy of an HTTP Request & Response
sections.content:
HTTP REQUEST (what you send):
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Method: POST                                   │
│  URL: https://api.example.com/api/users         │
│                                                 │
│  Headers:                                       │
│    Content-Type: application/json                │
│    Authorization: Bearer abc123token             │
│                                                 │
│  Body:                                          │
│    { "name": "Alice", "email": "a@b.com" }      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
            │
            ▼  (travels over the internet)

HTTP RESPONSE (what you get back):
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Status: 201 Created                            │
│                                                 │
│  Headers:                                       │
│    Content-Type: application/json                │
│    X-RateLimit-Remaining: 98                    │
│                                                 │
│  Body:                                          │
│    { "id": 42, "name": "Alice",                 │
│      "email": "a@b.com",                        │
│      "createdAt": "2024-01-15" }                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
sections.description: Diagram showing the structure of an HTTP request (method, URL, headers, body) and the corresponding HTTP response (status code, headers, body).

questions.prompt: Which HTTP method should you use to CREATE a new blog post on the server?
questions.answer: POST
questions.explanation: POST is used to CREATE new resources. You send the data for the new blog post in the request body, and the server creates it and returns the created resource (usually with a new ID and a 201 status code).

questions.prompt: What does HTTP status code 404 mean?
choices: The requested resource was not found
questions.answer: The requested resource was not found
questions.explanation: 404 Not Found means the URL you requested doesn't exist on the server. Maybe the user was deleted, the page doesn't exist, or there's a typo in the URL. It's a 4xx code, meaning it's a client-side issue (you asked for something that isn't there).

questions.prompt: What does `response.ok` return when the status code is 404?
questions.answer: false — response.ok is true only for status codes 200-299. 404 is outside that range.
questions.explanation: response.ok is a boolean shortcut: true for status codes 200-299 (success), false for everything else. 404 is a client error (4xx), so response.ok is false. Always check response.ok before processing the response data.

questions.prompt: What's the difference between PUT and PATCH?
choices: PUT replaces the entire resource; PATCH updates only the specified fields
questions.answer: PUT replaces the entire resource; PATCH updates only the specified fields
questions.explanation: PUT sends the COMPLETE new version of a resource (all fields). PATCH sends only the fields you want to change. Example: To change just a user's email, PATCH { email: 'new@email.com' } is more efficient than PUT { name: 'Alice', email: 'new@email.com', age: 25, ... } with all fields.

lessons.title: The fetch() Function — Your First API Call
objectives: Use fetch() to make GET requests to real APIs
Parse JSON responses with response.json()
Understand that fetch() returns a Promise
Use async/await with fetch for clean syntax
Display API data in the DOM

sections.content:
# fetch() — Your Gateway to Real Data

The `fetch()` function is JavaScript's built-in way to make HTTP requests. It's a global function available in all modern browsers — no libraries needed. It returns a Promise, which means everything you learned about Promises and async/await applies directly here.

This is the single most important function for working with APIs.

sections.title: fetch() Basics — The Two-Step Process
sections.explanation: fetch() returns a Promise that resolves to a Response object. The Response object ISN'T the data itself — it's a wrapper. You need to call response.json() (which ALSO returns a Promise) to extract the actual JSON data. Two awaits: one for the network response, one for parsing the body.

sections.title: Why TWO awaits? Why Can't fetch() Just Return the Data?
Fetching Different Types of Data
sections.explanation: APIs can return single objects or arrays. Use query parameters (?userId=1) to filter results. Each API has different endpoints and response shapes — always check the API documentation to know what to expect.

sections.title: Displaying API Data in the DOM
sections.explanation: This is the complete pattern: 1) Show loading state, 2) Fetch data, 3) Create DOM elements from data, 4) Handle errors gracefully. Notice the loading indicator, the disabled button during the request, and the error recovery with a 'Retry' button.

sections.title: Working with Query Parameters
sections.explanation: URLSearchParams is the clean way to build query strings. It handles URL encoding automatically (spaces, special characters). Always use it when you have multiple query parameters or user-provided values that might contain special characters.

sections.hint: The PokéAPI returns a large object. Access pokemon.name, pokemon.id, pokemon.types, etc.
sections.title: Try It: Fetch and Display Pokémon Data
sections.expected_output:
✨ PIKACHU ✨
  ID: #25
  Height: 0.4m
  Weight: 6kg
  Types: electric
  Sprite: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/...

questions.prompt: Why does fetch() require TWO await statements?
choices: The first await gets the response headers, the second await downloads and parses the body
One is for the request and one is for the response
You only need one await — the second is optional
questions.answer: The first await gets the response headers, the second await downloads and parses the body
questions.explanation: await fetch(url) completes when the server starts responding (headers received). await response.json() completes when the entire response body has been downloaded and parsed into a JavaScript object. Two separate async operations = two awaits.

questions.prompt: Write an async function `getPost(id)` that fetches a post from `https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts/{id}`, checks if the response is ok, and returns the parsed JSON. If not ok, throw an Error.
What does `response.json()` return?
questions.answer: A Promise that resolves to the parsed JSON data (a JavaScript object or array). You need to await it.
questions.explanation: response.json() returns a Promise, NOT the data directly. That's why you need `await response.json()` or `.then(response => response.json())`. The Promise resolves to whatever JavaScript value the JSON represents — usually an object or array.

questions.prompt: What's the best way to build a URL with multiple query parameters?
choices: Use URLSearchParams for clean, encoded query strings
Put parameters in the request body
Use separate fetch calls for each parameter
questions.answer: Use URLSearchParams for clean, encoded query strings
questions.explanation: URLSearchParams handles URL encoding automatically, properly escaping special characters like spaces, ampersands, and unicode. Manual string concatenation can produce invalid URLs. URLSearchParams({ name: 'alice bob' }) correctly produces 'name=alice+bob'.

lessons.title: POST, PUT, and DELETE — Sending Data to Servers
objectives: Send data to a server using POST requests
Update existing data with PUT and PATCH requests
Delete data with DELETE requests
Set proper headers for JSON requests
Understand the request options object

sections.content:
# Sending Data — POST, PUT, and DELETE

So far we've only used GET requests — reading data from servers. But real apps also need to **create**, **update**, and **delete** data. This is where POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE come in.

The key difference: GET requests only need a URL. POST/PUT/PATCH requests also need a **body** (the data you're sending) and **headers** (telling the server what format the data is in).

sections.title: POST — Creating New Data
sections.explanation: POST requires three things in the options object: 1) method: 'POST', 2) headers telling the server we're sending JSON, 3) body containing the data as a JSON string (using JSON.stringify). The server creates the resource and returns it with a new ID.

sections.definition:
**JSON.stringify()** converts a JavaScript object into a JSON string. You MUST do this because the request body must be a string, not an object.

**Content-Type: application/json** tells the server: 'The body of this request is formatted as JSON.' Without this header, the server might not understand your data.

sections.title: PUT & PATCH — Updating Existing Data
sections.explanation: PUT replaces the entire resource — you must send ALL fields, even unchanged ones. PATCH is a partial update — only send the fields you want to change. In practice, PATCH is more common because you usually only change one or two fields at a time.

sections.title: DELETE — Removing Data
sections.explanation: DELETE is the simplest — usually just the method and URL, no body needed. Always confirm destructive actions with the user! After a successful DELETE, remove the corresponding element from the DOM so the UI reflects the change.

sections.title: A Complete CRUD Example — Todo App API
sections.explanation: This todoAPI object encapsulates all CRUD operations. This is a professional pattern — creating an API service layer that your UI code calls. The UI doesn't need to know about fetch details; it just calls todoAPI.create(), todoAPI.getAll(), etc.

sections.title: The Options Object Cheat Sheet
sections.content:
javascript
fetch(url, {
  method: 'POST',              // GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE
  headers: {
    'Content-Type': 'application/json',  // Sending JSON
    'Authorization': 'Bearer TOKEN',     // Auth token
    'Accept': 'application/json',        // Expect JSON back
  },
  body: JSON.stringify(data),  // Data to send (not for GET/DELETE)
  mode: 'cors',                // Cross-origin mode (default)
  credentials: 'include',      // Send cookies with request
})


Most common usage:
- **GET:** `fetch(url)` — no options needed
- **POST/PUT/PATCH:** `fetch(url, { method, headers, body })`
- **DELETE:** `fetch(url, { method: 'DELETE' })`

questions.prompt: Why do you need to call JSON.stringify() on the body of a POST request?
choices: Because the request body must be a string, not a JavaScript object
It's optional — you can send objects directly
questions.answer: Because the request body must be a string, not a JavaScript object
questions.explanation: HTTP request bodies are text-based. You can't send a JavaScript object directly over the network — it must be serialized (converted to a string). JSON.stringify() converts { name: 'Alice' } to the string '{"name":"Alice"}'. The Content-Type header tells the server how to parse that string.

questions.prompt: Write an async function `createComment(postId, name, body, email)` that sends a POST request to `https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/comments` with the given data. Return the created comment.
What header must you include when sending JSON data in a POST request?
questions.answer: Content-Type: application/json
questions.explanation: Content-Type: application/json tells the server that the request body is formatted as JSON. Without this header, the server might not know how to parse your data and could return a 400 Bad Request or misinterpret the data entirely.

questions.prompt: What HTTP status code would you expect when a POST request successfully creates a new resource?
questions.answer: 201 Created — this is the standard status code for successful resource creation.
questions.explanation: While 200 OK also indicates success, 201 Created is the specific code for 'a new resource was created as a result of this request.' The response usually includes the created resource with its server-assigned ID.

lessons.title: Error Handling with fetch — Building Resilient Apps
objectives: Understand what fetch DOES and DOESN'T throw errors for
Build a robust error handling strategy for API calls
Handle network errors vs HTTP errors
Create a reusable fetch wrapper with error handling
Show user-friendly error messages

sections.content:
# Error Handling — The Difference Between Toy Code and Production Code

Here's a critical fact about fetch that trips up almost every beginner:

**fetch() only throws an error for NETWORK failures** (no internet, DNS error, server unreachable). It does **NOT** throw for HTTP errors like 404 or 500!

If the server responds with "404 Not Found" or "500 Internal Server Error", fetch considers that a **successful** network request (the server DID respond, just with bad news). You MUST check `response.ok` yourself.

sections.title: ⚠️ The #1 fetch() Gotcha
The Correct Way to Handle fetch Errors
sections.explanation: The proper pattern: 1) try/catch wraps the entire operation. 2) Inside the try, check response.ok BEFORE parsing JSON. 3) If not ok, throw an Error with the status code. 4) The catch block handles both network failures (TypeError) and HTTP errors (our thrown Error).

sections.title: Reusable API Client with Full Error Handling
sections.explanation: This is a production-ready fetch wrapper. It handles: 1) Network errors with offline detection, 2) HTTP errors with a custom APIError class carrying status code and server error data, 3) 204 No Content responses, 4) Default headers for JSON. The calling code uses try/catch with instanceof checks for specific error types.

sections.title: Showing User-Friendly Error Messages in the UI
sections.explanation: Never show raw technical errors to users! Map error codes to friendly messages. Log the technical details to the console for debugging, but show the user a helpful, jargon-free message. Also handle offline scenarios gracefully.

sections.title: Loading States — The UX Trifecta

questions.prompt: Does fetch() throw an error when the server responds with a 404 status code?
choices: Yes — any non-200 status throws an error
No — fetch only throws for network failures. You must check response.ok manually.
Sometimes — it depends on the browser
Yes — but only in strict mode
questions.answer: No — fetch only throws for network failures. You must check response.ok manually.
questions.explanation: This is the #1 fetch gotcha! fetch() considers ANY server response (including 404, 500) as a successful network request. It only throws for network-level failures (no internet, DNS error, CORS blocked). You MUST check response.ok yourself and throw your own error for HTTP error codes.

questions.answer: It does NOT catch the 404! The code tries to parse the 404 error page as JSON. It might log a JSON error body, garbage data, or throw a JSON parse error if the 404 page is HTML.
questions.explanation: fetch doesn't throw for 404, so the catch block doesn't run (unless JSON parsing fails). The code blindly tries to parse whatever the server sent back as JSON. If the 404 page is HTML, response.json() will throw a SyntaxError, which IS caught. But this is accidental, not intentional error handling.

questions.prompt: Write a function `safeFetch(url)` that fetches data, checks `response.ok`, throws an error with the status if not ok, and catches all errors (network and HTTP). Return the parsed JSON on success, null on failure.
What are the three UI states every API call should handle?
questions.answer: Loading, Success, Error
questions.explanation: Every API call should show: 1) A loading state while waiting (spinner, skeleton), 2) The data on success, 3) A user-friendly error message on failure. Missing any of these creates a poor user experience — users need feedback at every stage.

lessons.title: Building a Weather Dashboard (Real API Project)
objectives: Work with a real public API (Open-Meteo weather API)
Build a complete feature from API docs to working code
Handle loading, success, and error states in the UI
Combine fetch, DOM manipulation, and events in one project

sections.content:
# Project: Weather Dashboard 🌤️

Let's build a real weather dashboard! We'll use the **Open-Meteo API** — it's free, requires no API key, and provides real weather data. This project combines everything: fetch, async/await, DOM manipulation, events, error handling, and classes.

sections.code:
🌤️ Weather Dashboard

⏳ Fetching weather data...

Recent Searches
sections.title: The HTML Structure
sections.explanation: We have an input for city name, a search button, and three state containers (loading, error, weather-result). We also track recent searches. Each state container is hidden by default and shown/hidden via JavaScript.

sections.title: Step 1: Geocoding API — Convert City Name to Coordinates
sections.explanation: Many APIs need coordinates instead of city names. The geocoding API is a common first step. We create a service class with a static method — clean and reusable. Notice how we handle the 'city not found' case by checking if results exist.

sections.title: Step 2: Weather API — Get Current Weather and Forecast
sections.explanation: The WeatherService class handles calling the weather API and transforming the raw response into a clean, easy-to-use format. It maps cryptic weather codes (0, 3, 61) to human-readable descriptions and emojis. The getWeather method returns a structured object with current conditions and a 7-day forecast.

sections.title: Step 3: The Weather App — Putting It All Together
sections.explanation: The WeatherApp class ties everything together: DOM references, event listeners, state management, API calls, and rendering. It follows the Loading/Success/Error pattern. Recent searches are saved to localStorage so they persist across page refreshes. Event delegation handles clicks on dynamically created recent search buttons.

sections.title: Concepts Used in This Project
sections.content:
Count the JavaScript concepts in this single project:

✅ **async/await** — All API calls
✅ **fetch()** — HTTP requests to real APIs
✅ **Promises** — Underlying fetch mechanism
✅ **Error handling** — try/catch with user-friendly messages
✅ **Classes** — WeatherApp, GeocodingService, WeatherService
✅ **Static methods** — Service class methods
✅ **Private fields** — #recentSearches
✅ **DOM manipulation** — innerHTML, classList, disabled
✅ **Events** — click, keydown, event delegation
✅ **localStorage** — Persisting recent searches
✅ **Template literals** — Building HTML strings
✅ **Array methods** — map, filter, join, slice
✅ **URLSearchParams** — Building query strings
✅ **Destructuring** — Extracting API response data

**This is real-world JavaScript!** 🎉

questions.prompt: Why does the weather app need TWO API calls (geocoding + weather)?
choices: It's a design mistake — one call would be better
The weather API requires coordinates (lat/long), so we first convert the city name to coordinates
Because JavaScript can only make one type of request
questions.answer: The weather API requires coordinates (lat/long), so we first convert the city name to coordinates
questions.explanation: Many weather APIs need latitude/longitude, not city names. So we use a geocoding API first to convert 'London' to { latitude: 51.5, longitude: -0.1 }, then pass those coordinates to the weather API. This is a common pattern — chaining API calls where one result feeds into the next.

questions.prompt: Write a function that fetches a random dog image from `https://dog.ceo/api/breeds/image/random` and creates an element in a div with id 'dog-container'. Handle errors.
Why do we save recent searches to localStorage instead of just a variable?
choices: localStorage persists across page refreshes and browser restarts — variables are lost on refresh
questions.answer: localStorage persists across page refreshes and browser restarts — variables are lost on refresh
questions.explanation: Regular JavaScript variables exist only while the page is open. When the user refreshes or closes the browser, all variables are lost. localStorage saves data to the user's disk, so it persists across sessions. Perfect for user preferences, recent searches, and cached data.

lessons.title: Working with APIs: Pagination, Headers, and Authentication
objectives: Handle paginated API responses (loading more data)
Set custom headers including Authorization tokens
Understand API keys and Bearer tokens
Work with rate limiting and API quotas

sections.content:
# Real-World API Patterns

Simple APIs return all data at once, but real-world APIs have additional complexity: pagination (data comes in pages), authentication (proving who you are), rate limiting (preventing abuse), and custom headers. Let's master these patterns.

sections.title: Pagination — Loading Data in Pages
sections.explanation: Pagination is how APIs handle large datasets. You request one 'page' at a time (e.g., 10-20 items). The API tells you how many total items exist. You can implement page numbers, 'Load More' buttons, or infinite scroll. The #loading flag prevents multiple simultaneous requests.

sections.title: Authentication — API Keys and Tokens
sections.explanation: APIs use authentication to identify who's making requests. API keys are simple strings you include in the URL or headers. Bearer tokens (like JWTs) are obtained after login and sent in the Authorization header. The createAuthenticatedFetch pattern creates a reusable function that automatically includes the token.

sections.title: Rate Limiting — Playing Nice with APIs
sections.explanation: Rate limiters queue requests and space them out to stay within API limits. When a 429 response is received, it waits (using the Retry-After header if available) before retrying. This is essential when making many API calls, like fetching data for 100 users.

sections.title: ⚠️ Never Put API Keys in Client-Side JavaScript!
sections.content:
**Important security rule:** API keys in your JavaScript code are visible to ANYONE who views your page source or opens DevTools.

❌ **Bad:**
javascript
const API_KEY = 'sk-secret-12345'; // Anyone can see this!
fetch(`https://api.openai.com/v1/chat?key=${API_KEY}`);


✅ **Good:** Use a backend server as a proxy:

Browser → Your Server (has the API key) → External API


For learning and free APIs with no billing, client-side keys are OK. For production apps with paid APIs, always use a backend proxy.

questions.prompt: Why do APIs use pagination?
choices: Because JSON can only hold 10 items
To avoid sending millions of records at once, which would be slow and wasteful
Because browsers can only display 10 items
questions.answer: To avoid sending millions of records at once, which would be slow and wasteful
questions.explanation: Imagine an API with 1 million users. Sending all of them in one response would take ages to download, use massive bandwidth, and probably crash the client. Pagination breaks data into manageable 'pages' (e.g., 20 items per page) that load quickly.

questions.prompt: What does a 429 status code mean?
choices: Too Many Requests — you've exceeded the API's rate limit
questions.answer: Too Many Requests — you've exceeded the API's rate limit
questions.explanation: 429 Too Many Requests means you're sending requests too fast. The API has a limit (e.g., 100 requests per minute), and you've exceeded it. The response usually includes a Retry-After header telling you how long to wait. Slow down or implement a rate limiter.

questions.prompt: Write a function `fetchWithAuth(url, token)` that makes a GET request with the Authorization header set to 'Bearer [token]'. Check response.ok and return parsed JSON.
Why should you NEVER put paid API keys directly in client-side JavaScript?
choices: Anyone can view your page source or use DevTools to steal the key and use it at your expense
API keys only work on servers
questions.answer: Anyone can view your page source or use DevTools to steal the key and use it at your expense
questions.explanation: All client-side JavaScript is visible to users. Anyone can open DevTools, find your API key, and use it in their own projects — running up your bill. For paid APIs, always use a backend server that keeps the key secret and proxies requests.

lessons.title: Module 16 Capstone: Build a GitHub Profile Explorer
objectives: Build a complete application from scratch using a real API
Combine ALL JavaScript concepts from the entire course
Work with the GitHub REST API (no authentication required for public data)
Implement search, display, error handling, and loading states
Use localStorage for caching and recent searches

sections.content:
# 🎓 Final Capstone Project: GitHub Profile Explorer

This is it — the final project of the entire course! You'll build a complete web application that searches for GitHub users, displays their profiles and repositories, and uses every major JavaScript concept you've learned.

**What you're building:**
- Search for any GitHub user by username
- Display their profile (avatar, bio, stats)
- List their repositories with stars, forks, and language
- Sort and filter repos
- Cache results in localStorage
- Full loading/error/success state handling
- Keyboard navigation (Enter to search)
- Recent search history

**API:** GitHub's public API — no key needed for public data!

sections.title: Part 1: GitHub API Service
sections.explanation: The GitHubAPI class handles all API communication. Key features: 1) In-memory cache with 5-minute expiry prevents redundant requests. 2) Rate limit monitoring (GitHub allows 60 requests/hour without auth). 3) Specific error messages for 404 and rate limiting. 4) Clean data transformation from raw API response to our app's format.

sections.title: Part 2: UI Renderer — Displaying Data Beautifully
sections.explanation: The UIRenderer class has ONLY static methods — it's a pure utility for generating HTML. It formats numbers (1500 → 1.5K), dates, and language colors. The render methods return HTML strings that the main app injects into the DOM. Separating rendering logic keeps the code organized and testable.

sections.title: Part 3: The Main Application — Tying Everything Together
sections.explanation: The GitHubExplorer class is the main application controller. It manages state (#currentUser, #currentRepos), handles user interactions, coordinates API calls (Promise.all for parallel fetching!), manages UI states (loading/error/success), and persists recent searches. This is a production-quality architecture you'd see in real web apps.

sections.title: 🎓 Every JavaScript Concept — In One Project!
sections.content:
Let's count EVERY concept from the entire course used in this capstone:

**Module 1-2:** Variables, data types, template literals, type conversion
**Module 3:** Comparison operators, logical operators
**Module 4:** if/else, ternary operator, switch
**Module 5:** for loops, for...of, array iteration
**Module 6:** Functions, arrow functions, default parameters, closures (debounce!)
**Module 7:** Arrays — map, filter, sort, slice, join
**Module 8:** Objects, nested objects, destructuring, spread operator
**Module 9:** Higher-order functions, method chaining
**Module 10:** DOM manipulation — querySelector, createElement, innerHTML, classList
**Module 11:** Events — addEventListener, event delegation, keyboard events
**Module 12-13:** Building interactive components, localStorage
**Module 14:** Classes, static methods, private fields (#), encapsulation, composition
**Module 15:** async/await, Promises, Promise.all, error handling, debouncing
**Module 16:** fetch(), JSON, APIs, HTTP status codes, headers, caching

**Congratulations — you've gone from ZERO to HERO! 🎉🏆**

sections.hint: Start with the simplest extension (Dark Mode toggle) if you want a quick win. For a challenge, try the User Comparison feature — it uses Promise.all to fetch two users in parallel.
sections.title: Your Challenge: Extend the App!
sections.expected_output: An extended GitHub Explorer with your chosen feature fully implemented.

questions.prompt: Why does the GitHub Explorer fetch the user profile and repos using Promise.all instead of sequential awaits?
choices: Promise.all is required by the GitHub API
The profile and repos are independent requests — fetching in parallel is faster
Sequential awaits don't work with GitHub's API
questions.answer: The profile and repos are independent requests — fetching in parallel is faster
questions.explanation: Getting the user profile and getting their repos are independent operations — neither depends on the other's result. With Promise.all, both requests fire simultaneously. If each takes 500ms, parallel takes 500ms total vs 1000ms sequential. Always use Promise.all for independent async operations!

questions.prompt: Write a function that fetches a GitHub user's repos from `https://api.github.com/users/{username}/repos`, sorts them by stars (most first), and returns the top 3. Handle errors.
Why does the GitHubAPI class use an in-memory cache?
choices: To make the code more complex
To avoid redundant API calls for the same data, saving time and API quota
questions.answer: To avoid redundant API calls for the same data, saving time and API quota
questions.explanation: GitHub limits unauthenticated requests to 60/hour. If a user searches 'torvalds' twice in 2 minutes, the cache serves the stored data instead of making new API calls. This saves API quota, reduces latency, and improves user experience. The cache expires after 5 minutes to ensure data freshness.

questions.prompt: What design pattern does the GitHubExplorer use to handle clicks on recent search buttons that are dynamically created?
choices: A separate listener on each button
Event delegation — one listener on the parent container
questions.answer: Event delegation — one listener on the parent container
questions.explanation: The recent search buttons are dynamically generated (innerHTML). If we tried to add listeners directly, we'd need to re-add them every time the list re-renders. Event delegation puts ONE listener on the parent (#recent-searches) and uses e.target.matches('.recent-item') to identify which button was clicked. This works automatically with dynamically added elements.

questions.prompt: If you search for a GitHub user that doesn't exist, what happens in the app?
questions.answer: The GitHubAPI.getUser() method receives a 404 response, checks response.status === 404, and throws an Error with 'User not found. Check the username and try again.' The app's catch block catches this and calls #showError() to display the message to the user.
questions.explanation: The error flow: fetch returns a 404 response → getUser checks the status and throws a specific error → the catch in #handleSearch catches it → #showError displays a user-friendly message with a retry button. This is the Loading/Success/Error pattern in action. The user never sees raw technical errors.

modules.title: Module 17: Final Capstone — Build Something Real

lessons.title: Course Recap & Choosing Your Capstone Project
objectives: Review every concept learned across all 16 modules
Understand the two capstone project options
Choose which project to build based on your interests
Understand what makes a portfolio-worthy project

sections.content:
# 🎓 You Made It — Time to Build Something REAL

You've completed 16 modules of intensive JavaScript learning. You've gone from not knowing what a variable is to building classes with inheritance, fetching data from real APIs, and handling complex asynchronous operations.

Now it's time for the final test: **build a complete, portfolio-worthy application from scratch**.

This isn't a toy project or a tutorial you follow line-by-line. You'll be given the architecture, key code patterns, and guidance — but YOU will make the design decisions, solve the edge cases, and bring the project to life.

This is what separates someone who "learned JavaScript" from someone who **can actually build things**.

sections.title: 📚 Everything You've Learned — The Full Arsenal
sections.content:
Let's take a moment to appreciate how far you've come:

**Foundations (Modules 1-5):** Variables, data types, operators, control flow (if/else/switch), loops (for, while, for...of)

**Functions & Data (Modules 6-9):** Functions, arrow functions, closures, scope, arrays, objects, destructuring, spread operator, map/filter/reduce, method chaining

**The Browser (Modules 10-13):** DOM selection, manipulation, creating elements, events, event delegation, bubbling, form handling, building UI components

**Advanced (Modules 14-16):** Classes, inheritance, polymorphism, encapsulation, private fields, composition, async/await, Promises, Promise.all, fetch API, error handling, HTTP methods, working with real APIs

**That's 50+ concepts — and you're about to use ALL of them in one project.** 💪

# Choose Your Capstone Project

You have **two options**. Both are equally challenging, equally impressive on a portfolio, and both exercise every concept you've learned. Pick the one that excites you more — you'll build better when you're motivated!

**Complete ONE of the two projects.** If you're feeling ambitious, come back and build the other one too!

rows: Full shopping experience with cart & checkout
TMDB (The Movie Database) — free, rich data
Professional — product grids, cart, checkout forms
OOP, state management, form validation, localStorage
sections.title: 🎬 CineVerse vs 🛒 ShopSmart — Choose Your Path
How to Pick
sections.content:
🎬 **Choose CineVerse if:**
- You love movies/TV shows
- You want to master working with complex APIs
- You enjoy building visually rich interfaces
- You want practice with multiple API endpoints and data relationships

🛒 **Choose ShopSmart if:**
- You're interested in e-commerce or business applications
- You want to master complex state management (cart, quantities, totals)
- You enjoy building forms and checkout flows
- You want practice that directly translates to job requirements

**Both projects use ALL the same JavaScript concepts** — just with different emphasis.

sections.title: Module 17 Learning Path
sections.content:
Lesson 1: Course Recap & Choose Your Project  ← YOU ARE HERE
       │
  Lesson 2: Project Architecture & Planning (shared)
       │
  Lesson 3: Shared Foundations: Modules, Storage, Patterns (shared)
       │
       ├───────────────────────────────────────────┐
       │                                           │
  ┌────▼────────────────┐                ┌─────────▼──────────────┐
  │  TRACK A: CineVerse │                │  TRACK B: ShopSmart    │
  │                     │                │                        │
  │  L4: API + Search   │                │  L7: Catalog + Filter  │
  │  L5: Movie Details  │                │  L8: Cart System       │
  │  L6: Watchlist +    │                │  L9: Checkout +        │
  │      Polish         │                │      Order History     │
  └────────┬────────────┘                └────────┬───────────────┘
           │                                      │
           └──────────────┬───────────────────────┘
                          │
                   Lesson 10: Where to Go From Here

  Complete Lessons 1-3, then choose Track A OR Track B,
  then finish with Lesson 10.
sections.description: A flow diagram showing the two project tracks (CineVerse and ShopSmart) that branch after shared lessons and converge at the final lesson.

questions.prompt: What's the most important thing about a capstone project for your portfolio?
choices: It should use as many npm packages as possible
It should be a complete, functional application that demonstrates real skills and problem-solving
It should have the most lines of code possible
It should be exactly like a tutorial project
questions.answer: It should be a complete, functional application that demonstrates real skills and problem-solving
questions.explanation: Employers and reviewers look for complete, working applications that show you can build real things — not toy demos or tutorial clones. They want to see proper error handling, clean code organization, good UX, and evidence that you can solve problems independently.

questions.prompt: Which JavaScript concepts from the course will you use in BOTH capstone projects?
choices: Virtually ALL concepts: variables, functions, arrays, objects, DOM, events, classes, async/await, fetch, error handling
questions.answer: Virtually ALL concepts: variables, functions, arrays, objects, DOM, events, classes, async/await, fetch, error handling
questions.explanation: Both projects are designed to exercise every major concept from the entire course. That's the point of a capstone — it's the culmination of everything. Variables, control flow, functions, arrays, objects, DOM manipulation, events, classes, Promises, async/await, fetch, and error handling all come together.

lessons.title: Project Architecture & Planning
objectives: Plan a project before writing code
Break a large application into manageable components
Design the data flow and class structure
Understand the separation of concerns principle

sections.content:
# Plan Before You Code — Architecture Matters

Jumping straight into code is tempting, but professionals ALWAYS plan first. A 30-minute planning session saves hours of refactoring later.

Both capstone projects follow the same architecture pattern: **Service → State → Renderer → App Controller**. This separation of concerns keeps code organized, testable, and maintainable.

sections.title: Application Architecture (Both Projects)
sections.content:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    APP CONTROLLER                           │
│          (Coordinates everything, handles events)           │
│                                                             │
│    Responds to user events → calls services → updates UI    │
└──────┬──────────────┬──────────────────┬────────────────────┘
       │              │                  │
 ┌─────▼──────┐ ┌─────▼──────────┐ ┌─────▼─────────────┐
 │  API        │ │  STATE         │ │  UI RENDERER      │
 │  SERVICE    │ │  MANAGER       │ │                   │
 │             │ │                │ │  Generates HTML   │
 │ fetch()     │ │ Current data   │ │  from data.       │
 │ calls,      │ │ Cart items     │ │  Pure functions.  │
 │ caching,    │ │ Watchlist      │ │  No side effects. │
 │ error       │ │ Filters/Sort   │ │                   │
 │ handling    │ │ localStorage   │ │  renderCard()     │
 │             │ │                │ │  renderList()     │
 │ getMovies() │ │ addToCart()    │ │  renderDetail()   │
 │ getProduct()│ │ getTotal()     │ │                   │
 └─────────────┘ └────────────────┘ └───────────────────┘

  SEPARATION OF CONCERNS:
